question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether pothole repairs are checked by her Department, when the repair has been carried out by a sub-contractor.
answer_text: My Department identifies actionable defects for repair through routine highway safety inspections and through public reporting via the online Report a Fault system.Actionable defects are instructed for repair either through our internal or external contractor.  On completion of the repair, inspections are undertaken on a percentage of completed road defect repairs by external contractors before payments are processed. Checks on the work of the Department’s internal contractor are also undertaken.In addition to the contractual checks above, issues will also be recorded at the next routine inspection.If a repair fails to satisfy the required standards, officials will request that it be rectified and subjected to further inspection. This process will continue until the repair fully complies with all stipulated requirements.Officials use DEM 160/21 Road Maintenance Standards for Safety – Limited Service when determining actionable defects and the Manual of Contract Documents for Highways Works when determining a repair to be satisfactory.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department has any course for redress, should pothole repairs not meet the required standards when inspected.
answer_text: My Department identifies actionable defects for repair through routine highway safety inspections and through public reporting via the online Report a Fault system.Actionable defects are instructed for repair either through our internal or external contractor.  On completion of the repair, inspections are undertaken on a percentage of completed road defect repairs by external contractors before payments are processed. Checks on the work of the Department’s internal contractor are also undertaken.In addition to the contractual checks above, issues will also be recorded at the next routine inspection.If a repair fails to satisfy the required standards, officials will request that it be rectified and subjected to further inspection. This process will continue until the repair fully complies with all stipulated requirements.Officials use DEM 160/21 Road Maintenance Standards for Safety – Limited Service when determining actionable defects and the Manual of Contract Documents for Highways Works when determining a repair to be satisfactory.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any conversations regarding the implementation of an multidisciplinary team being placed within Brookeborough and Tempo Practice.
answer_text: Recruitment within the South West Federation has commenced in recent months and is expected to continue at a steady pace in to the new financial year in line with the MDT implementation plan. My Department regularly engage with the Western Trust and the South West GP Federation on the progress of the implementation plan. However, the decision on which individual practice MDT staff will be placed in, and the sequencing of that rollout, will be determined locally by both the Western Trust and South West GP Federation, based on a range of factors including operational considerations, service need and practice readiness.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 38561/22-27, to detail the costs contained within the business case.
answer_text: The business case received from the Belfast is seeking £53.7k recurrently for a Facial palsy Therapist. The business case from the South Eastern Trust is seeking funding of £13.4k for 2025/25 and £53.7k recurrently thereafter for a Facial Palsy Therapist. Both business cases are currently being assessed by departmental officials to ensure they align to the Department’s strategic priorities, represent value for money and are affordable and deliverable.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail which hospitals are adhering to his Department’s revised standards for Emergency General Surgery published in the May 2024 Review of General Surgery.
To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the bluetongue outbreak, following its detection in December 2025.
answer_text: To date there has been five confirmed Bluetongue serotype 3 (BTV-3) infected premises in Northern Ireland, since the first infected premises was confirmed on 5 December 2025.	My officials are working on next steps in close collaboration with their counterparts in the Republic of Ireland and across the United Kingdom. They also remain engaged regularly with key stakeholders.  In the meantime, further updates relating to the Bluetongue outbreak in Northern Ireland will be made available on the DAERA website, when appropriate, via the following webpage https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/articles/bluetongue-situation-northern-ireland
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department will consider renaming of the Senator George Mitchell Peace Bridge on the Belturbet Road, Aghalane, County Fermanagh.
answer_text: I am aware that the bridge is commonly known as the Senator George Mitchell Peace Bridge but was never formally named as such by my Department.  The Department records the name of this bridge as the Aghalane Bridge and as such has no plans to rename.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any restrictions that are still in place, following the bluetongue outbreak in December 2025.
answer_text: Details on the impacts of movements as a result of the current Temporary Control Zone (TCZ) can be found at https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/articles/bluetongue-situation-northern-ireland.		From Friday 19 December 2025, all animals, except for animals that have tested positive for Bluetongue (BTV), will be free to move under general licence within and out of the TCZ unless they are in certain high-risk categories.  A Press Release which provides further detail can be found at https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/news/changes-bluetongue-movement-restrictions.	Guidance for herd and flock keepers on moving animals is available at https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/publications/bluetongue-licences.		My officials are working in close collaboration with their counterparts in the Republic of Ireland and across the United Kingdom on next steps.  Any updates will be communicated to all stakeholders.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of vaccines administered since the bluetongue outbreak, in December 2025.
answer_text: Since the first confirmed outbreak of Bluetongue Virus (BTV) on 29 November 2025, herd and flock keepers in Northern Ireland have applied to my Department for the use of 70,600 doses of the BTV-3 vaccine, based on data up to and including 10 February 2026.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to safeguard measures in relation to the tropilaelaps mite introduced on 6 October 2025 to restrict imports into Great Britain from Ukraine, to detail the position for Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My Department is aware of Defra’s decision to introduce a temporary prohibition on honeybee imports from Ukraine into GB due to concerns around the lack of surveillance measures in place. I am advised that this prohibition was implemented in response to a request to import honeybees into GB from Ukraine. The position in Northern Ireland is that no similar requests have been made to move consignments of honeybees into Northern Ireland from Ukraine.As you are aware, Official Controls applied to movements of honeybee consignments entering Northern Ireland are under the direction and control of the Secretary of State. If urgent disease control is required, I am in a position to take appropriate emergency measures in response. Due to the current risk profile for Tropilaelaps entering Northern Ireland and the Official Controls currently in place for their import, there is no intention to introduce emergency measures at this stage. This position will be kept under review.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any consultation her Department carried out with driving instructors, prior to the introduction of the Graduated Driver Licensing scheme.
answer_text: During the development of the Graduated Driver Licensing (GDL) proposals, my Department was in regular contact with the Northern Ireland Approved Instructors Council (NIAIC) as an individual organisation and as part of a wider GDL Stakeholder Group.  Their input helped shape the GDL provisions within the Road Traffic (Amendment) Act (Northern Ireland) 2016.Officials continued to liaise with NIAIC and the wider approved instructor industry on the 2017 consultation on the consequential changes to the practical driving test.  Since my announcement that GDL will be implemented from 1 October 2026, the Driver and Vehicle Agency (DVA) has arranged a meeting with NIAIC for 12 February 2026.  Monthly meetings have also been scheduled with NIAIC to the end of September 2026 to update them on the practicalities of GDL.  To further ensure broad and representative engagement across the approved instructor industry, regional information workshops will also be held in the coming months.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the process for anyone who has commenced learning to drive before the 1 October 2026 and has not yet passed their test, following the introduction of the Graduated Driver licensing scheme.
answer_text: Transitional arrangements for learners whose provisional licence entitlement to drive a category B vehicle commenced before 1 October 2026 are set out in the Road Traffic (Amendment) Act 2016.  During the six-month transitional period set, in practice covering from 1 October 2026 to 31 March 2027, such provisional licence holders will be able to complete their training and take the driving test for which they have been prepared.  Should they pass a practical test before 31 March 2027, they will be required to display the current ‘amber’ R plate for one year and remain restricted to a speed limit of 45mph as is currently the case.  If those learners do not pass a practical test by the end of the transitional period, then from 1 April 2027 they will become subject to GDL requirements.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any measures in place to support farmers affected by livestock worrying.
answer_text: I recognise the seriousness of livestock worrying and the devastating impact it can have on both animals and farmers. While responsibility for livestock worrying rests with local councils, my department’s Responsible Dog Ownership campaign includes key messages including the need for dog owners to always have control over their dog, especially in the countryside.Further guidance is also available on the Department’s website and farmers are encouraged to report all incidences of livestock worrying to their local council dog wardens promptly for investigation.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he will introduce tougher legislation to reduce livestock worrying.
answer_text: It remains an offence not to have a dog under control on land where livestock are present, let a dog worry livestock, or set a dog on livestock. If a dog warden has reasonable cause to believe a dog has committed one of these offences, they can place control conditions on the dog licence held by that dog owner, which can include confining the dog to a specified place. Where a person is convicted of letting their dog worry livestock, the penalty is a fine of £1000. For setting a dog on livestock, the penalty is a £2,500 fine. The court can also order that the dog is destroyed. I am aware that jurisdictions in Great Britain have recently amended legislation in relation to livestock worrying. However, I believe that the current legal requirements in Northern Ireland, as I have set out, are robust. Given the limited time remaining in the mandate, it is not feasible to conduct a review, or make any amendments, to the legislation in relation to livestock worrying. My focus remains on delivering the reforms set out in my Animal Welfare Pathway, which I set following extensive engagement with stakeholders.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of livestock worrying incidents, recorded in the last 12 months.
answer_text: Councils are responsible under The Dogs (Northern Ireland) Order 1983 (the Order) for investigating incidents of livestock worrying. The Department collates this data from all 11 councils and publishes it on our website. Local councils submit their data on a quarterly basis and records show there was 101 incidences of livestock worrying between September 2024 to September 2025.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of how local councils operate in cases of livestock worrying.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether any assessment has been carried out by his Department on the economic impact of small business closure within Northern Ireland communities.
answer_text: Responsibility for such an assessment would rest with the Department for the Economy given its responsibilities in relation to business support and wider economic policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether any funding has been ring-fenced for pothole repairs in each of the four DfI Roads Divisions.
answer_text: For routine maintenance activities such as patching and grass cutting, the factors taken into consideration when distributing the resources available includes information on actionable road defects, areas of grass to be cut and numbers of gullies to be cleaned. In addition to this, the capacity of my Department’s internal contractor, and any associated requirement to use external contractors, is factored into allocation considerations to ensure that the current ‘Limited Maintenance Service’ is delivered.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether he will use the Barnett consequential funding provided as a result of the £300 million support package for the hospitality industry in Great Britain, to provide a similar package of support to businesses in Northern Ireland, as a result of NI Reval2026.
answer_text: I refer the member to my Oral Statement of 2 February 2026 at the link below:-https://www.finance-ni.gov.uk/publications/finance-minister-oral-statement-reval-2026 As regards Barnett consequential funding from Westminster this is unhypothecated which means its allocation is a decision for the Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether any assessment has been carried out by his Department on the economic impact of small business closure within Northern Ireland communities.
answer_text: My Department has not carried out a specific assessment of the economic impact of small business closures in Northern Ireland communities as this would primarily come under the remit of the Department for the Economy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether any assessment has been carried out by her Department on the economic impact of small business closure within Northern Ireland communities.
answer_text: Small businesses form the backbone of our economy here in the North, accounting for approximately 99% of the local business base. 		In 2024 there were an estimated 131,900 small businesses in the local economy which by 2025, had grown to 137,500 – this is encouraging, but I am also aware that positive moves at the aggregate level do not tell the whole story. 		Small businesses are important parts of our local communities, supplying vital goods and services, providing employment and contributing to regional balance, and I am aware of both the challenges they continue to face, and the impacts their closure can have on employees, owners and communities. 		I am committed to building an economy where small businesses can scale, grow and transform. To that end, my Department and Invest NI aim to ensure that small businesses can access the support they need to thrive. 		This includes a range of solutions from Invest NI to support small businesses, including its Access to Finance programme, the Ambition to Grow programme, and the Business Innovation Grant. 		To further support small businesses, I have also established new Local Economic Partnerships across all council areas, and each Partnership will determine the projects to be developed based on local economic priorities.		I, along with my officials continue to monitor the local business environment and to work with relevant stakeholders, Executive colleagues and business representatives to help our small businesses grow and thrive.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any measures she will put in place within Fermanagh and South Tyrone, to support those who are made redundant as a result of ABP Linden Foods announcement.
answer_text: I was extremely concerned at the announcement made by ABP on 20 January 2026 regarding its intention to cease production at its retail packing facility at Dungannon’s Granville Industrial Estate, putting 338 jobs at risk.I am aware that ABP has commenced a 90-day minimum consultation period. Notwithstanding, officials from Invest NI are meeting with ABP senior management at the start of February. Invest NI will continue to work with the company’s management over the coming period, and will engage with the Department for Communities, Mid Ulster District Council and other stakeholders to seek to affect the best possible outcome for staff concerned.In relation to Fermanagh and South Tyrone, Invest NI is working with stakeholders on the delivery of Local Economic Partnership Plans, and in the delivery of a series of Regional Business Advice clinics across the area. Invest NI will continue to work with its economic development partners to support the startup and scaling of businesses within the area, including working with any affected employees considering starting a business.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the new timeline for the A4 Enniskillen Southern Bypass scheme.
answer_text: The Enniskillen Southern Bypass is a strategically important scheme which will provide significant benefits to the town and its surrounding road network. Last year the procurement process commenced, and a shortlist of contractors was invited to bid for the contract to construct the scheme. Since the judgment on the A5 Western Transport Corridor project and the Department’s appeal, I am taking time to carefully consider the potential impact on other projects as they move through the next stages of their delivery, this includes A4 Enniskillen Southern Bypass.  Currently, the scheme has not incurred any direct costs as a result of this, and a new timeline will be considered when the outcome of the A5 appeal is known.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the A4 Enniskillen Southern Bypass scheme.
answer_text: The Enniskillen Southern Bypass is a strategically important scheme which will provide significant benefits to the town and its surrounding road network. Last year the procurement process commenced, and a shortlist of contractors was invited to bid for the contract to construct the scheme. Since the judgment on the A5 Western Transport Corridor project and the Department’s appeal, I am taking time to carefully consider the potential impact on other projects as they move through the next stages of their delivery, this includes A4 Enniskillen Southern Bypass.  Currently, the scheme has not incurred any direct costs as a result of this, and a new timeline will be considered when the outcome of the A5 appeal is known.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on whether the delay to the Enniskillen Southern Bypass scheme has incurred any extra spend.
answer_text: The Enniskillen Southern Bypass is a strategically important scheme which will provide significant benefits to the town and its surrounding road network. Last year the procurement process commenced, and a shortlist of contractors was invited to bid for the contract to construct the scheme. Since the judgment on the A5 Western Transport Corridor project and the Department’s appeal, I am taking time to carefully consider the potential impact on other projects as they move through the next stages of their delivery, this includes A4 Enniskillen Southern Bypass.  Currently, the scheme has not incurred any direct costs as a result of this, and a new timeline will be considered when the outcome of the A5 appeal is known.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any emergency engagement her Department will have with ABP Linden Foods, to protect jobs at its Granville site, Dungannon.
answer_text: I was extremely concerned at the announcement made by ABP on 20 January 2026 regarding its intention to cease production at its retail packing facility at Granville Industrial Estate in Dungannon, putting 338 jobs at risk. Immediately on foot of that announcement officials from Invest NI contacted ABP senior management to discuss the matter.I am aware that ABP has commenced a 90-day minimum consultation period and I would emphasise to ABP the importance of working collectively and constructively with my officials to achieve the best outcome possible for staff affected.To that end, my officials from Invest NI have requested an urgent face-to-face meeting with ABP senior management as soon as is practicable. This meeting will include officials from Invest NI’s sector team as well as key Invest NI personnel in the agency’s Western office so that local officials can be fully engaged with ABP during its consultation process.Invest NI will work with the company’s management over the coming weeks and months, and my officials will engage with Department for Communities Jobs and Benefits, Mid Ulster District Council, and other local businesses and stakeholders as appropriate, to seek to affect the best possible outcome for staff concerned.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any immediate actions that would be taken should a suspected case of tropilaelaps mite enter Northern Ireland.
answer_text: In the event of the Department being notified of a suspected case of Tropilaelaps, DAERA would facilitate immediate confirmatory diagnostics and testing at the AgriFood and Biosciences Institute (AFBI). If the presence of Tropilaelaps was confirmed, Department officials would implement appropriate actions as required under legislation to minimise the risk of spread, including prohibitions on movements and destruction or treatment of the affected honeybees and associated materials.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department, in conjunction with Translink, have any plans for the further development or improvements of Dungannon Bus Station.
answer_text: Dungannon Bus Station has had a series of improvement works including; a new site wide emergency lighting system; resurfacing of the bus yard, installation of safety protection bollards, drainage replacement and upgrades, increased lighting levels in line with current safety standards and updated signage. Translink are currently working on a Feasibility Study to look at replacing the electrical distribution boards and lighting within the Bus Engineering Garage in the future. This work, as all capital investment plans, will be subject to the availability of future funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department, in conjunction with Translink, have any plans for the further development or improvements of Enniskillen Bus Station.
answer_text: Enniskillen Bus Station has had a series of improvement works including: a new site wide emergency lighting system; resurfacing of the bus yard: installation of safety protection bollards; drainage replacement and upgrades; and increased lighting levels in line with current safety standards and updated signage. Translink will also continue to deliver a range of activities to maintain the station.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 34196/22-27, whether he has received a business case from the Belfast and South Eastern Health and Social Care Trusts for the management of specialist facial palsy patients who require facial therapy from a specialist therapist as part of their treatment pathway.
answer_text: A business case has now been received from the Belfast and South Eastern Health and Social Care Trusts for the management of specialist facial palsy patients who require facial therapy from a specialist therapist as part of their treatment pathway. Both business cases will now be subject to independent scrutiny by departmental officials to ensure they align to the Department’s strategic priorities, represent value for money and are affordable and deliverable.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any preventative measures in place to stop Tropilaelaps from entering Northern Ireland, including import controls, movement restrictions and support for beekeepers.
answer_text: Under the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024, Official Controls, which are applied to consignments of honeybees entering Northern Ireland to mitigate the risk of introduction of pest and diseases, including Tropilaelaps, are under the direction and control of the Secretary of State for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs. Risk profiles for animal health diseases, which include those for bee health, are kept under review and if emergency measures are deemed to be necessary then I can put appropriate measures in place.DAERA officials are in regular contact with honeybee stakeholders in Northern Ireland to share information and provide advice on bee health issues, including the threat of Tropilaelaps. More information can be found at https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/articles/bee-health.Additionally, DAERA has support in place for the beekeeping sector in the event of any outbreaks of honeybee pests and diseases, including Tropilaelaps, and contingency arrangements are in place for any outbreaks of exotic pests and diseases of honeybees.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any water quality monitoring carried out in rivers and lakes in Fermanagh and South Tyrone.
answer_text: The Water Environment (Water Framework Directive) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2017 sets out monitoring requirements. For rivers and lakes in Fermanagh and South Tyrone this entails:Monthly sampling of general physico-chemical elements, specific pollutants, and priority substances.Sampling of macrophytes, diatoms, fish, and hydromorphology at least once in a six year cycle.In addition, in lakes sampling of phytoplankton takes place annually in July, August, and September and in rivers, invertebrate sampling takes place in Spring and Autumn on a rolling three year basis.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any chemicals and substances that are tested for in waterways.
answer_text: My Department undertakes monitoring of waterways under the Water Environment (Water Framework Directive) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2017 and Water Framework Directive (Classification, Priority Substances and Shellfish Waters) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2015. A combination of monthly and quarterly monitoring is undertaken for 29 chemicals and trace metals. During each six-year River Basin Plan Cycle, additional monitoring is undertaken each month for a twelve-month period. This additional testing is for a wide range of pollutants that are considered potential threats to water quality.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail how often water quality monitoring is carried out in Lough Erne.
answer_text: The Water Environment (Water Framework Directive) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2017 sets out monitoring requirements. For Lough Erne this includes:Monthly sampling of general physico-chemical elements, specific pollutants, and priority substances.Phytoplankton sampled annually in July, August, and September.Sampling of macrophytes, diatoms, fish, and hydromorphology every six years.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 37742/22-27, whether (i) his Department; or (ii) CCEA, is undertaking any preparatory work with schools for the introduction of a sign language qualification, to ensure there are no barriers for any school wishing to offer it to pupils.
answer_text: As outlined in my previous response to AQW 37742/22-27, a process is currently underway in England to develop a GCSE in British Sign Language. Following the completion of this process, CCEA Regulation will be able to complete any requisite preparatory work, including the accreditation of the qualification, to allow it to be delivered in Northern Ireland for any school wishing to offer it to pupils. As with the introduction of any GCSE, support materials will be available to schools from the awarding organisation delivering the qualification.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the engagement her officials are having regarding the steps required to use electronic proof of motor insurance, when taxing a motor vehicle.
answer_text: Removal of the legislative requirement to check a hard copy proof of motor insurance when purchasing Vehicle Excise Duty (Motor Tax) for the first time, would be dependent on the introduction of a Continuous Insurance Enforcement (CIE) scheme here.In line with Action Point 4.4 of the Motor Insurance Taskforce Report, published in December 2025, my officials have commenced exploratory engagement with key stakeholders to assess the benefits and feasibility of introducing a CIE scheme here.As this work is at an early stage, it is not yet possible to set out the legislative, operational or system changes that would be required to introduce CIE here and, as a consequence, enable the acceptance of electronic proof of insurance when taxing a vehicle. Once this work has progressed further, officials will provide me with an update.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether the Executive have undertaken a (i) lessons learned exercise; or (ii) review of storm preparedness, following Storm Éowyn.
answer_text: We consider lessons management and continual improvement as essential cornerstones of civil contingencies preparedness, response and recovery.  Following Storm Éowyn in January 2025, the Civil Contingencies Division in The Executive Office conducted a multi-agency lessons identification review. Subsequently, a ‘Storm Éowyn Civil Contingencies Lessons Identified’ report was published to civil contingencies partners on 11 December 2025.  A copy of that report has been placed in the Assembly Library.The report notes the actions undertaken in response to Storm Éowyn, highlights evidence of good practice, and identifies areas for improvement. It sets out 12 recommendations across strategic themes of policy, people, preparedness, and pressures to inform future collective civil contingencies preparedness.At its meeting on 19 November 2025, the Civil Contingencies Group (NI) approved the establishment of a Task and Finish group to take forward the implementation of those recommendations.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any discussions they are having regarding (i) Artificial intelligence development; and (ii) associated technologies, with other UK jurisdictions.
answer_text: We co-chaired the 43rd British-Irish Council meeting in June 2025, the focus of which was the role of AI in reform and transformation of public services. The Council discussed the various approaches of the Council’s Member Administrations in harnessing AI technologies and discussed the potential benefits, and the associated challenges, of the more widespread and scaled-up use of AI technologies in public administration. An update was provided at official level during the 44th British-Irish Council meeting held in December 2025. We have consistently emphasised the importance of the ethical and responsible use of Artificial Intelligence across all Executive Departments. This commitment is being delivered through the work of the Office of AI and Digital and the development of a comprehensive cross-Executive AI Strategy which will be grounded in the principles of transparency, respect and fairness.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the procurement of new equipment, following the judgement of the High Court case with Worldwide Environmental Products Inc.
answer_text: An open procurement competition for a new Vehicle Testing Solution was published on 30 April 2025 and closed on 30 June 2025.  The evaluation stage is now completed, and the Most Advantageous Tender has been identified. The Driver and Vehicle Agency, in conjunction with Construction and Procurement Delivery (DoF), is currently progressing the remaining elements of the process and expects the contract to be awarded shortly.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of staff that will be employed in GP surgeries within the South West Federation area to facilitate the roll-out of multidisciplinary teams.
answer_text: In line with the agreed workforce planning model for the rollout of multidisciplinary teams (MDTs), staffing levels are based on a ratio of one wholetime equivalent (WTE) post per 10,000 registered GP patients.The South West GP Federation has a registered population of 128,527, which provides an entitlement of approximately 12.8 WTE posts for each MDT profession. This includes First Contact Physiotherapists, Mental Health Practitioners, Social Workers, and Social Work Assistants (SWAs).These posts will be recruited over an eightyear period, as set out in the MDT implementation plan published in July 2025. Recruitment will proceed in line with practice readiness and capacity to host MDT staff.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the 13 GP surgeries in which multidisciplinary teams will be rolled out in the South West Federation area, in this financial year.
answer_text: There are 13 GP practices within the South West Federation area that are ready to accommodate multidisciplinary team (MDT) rollout in this financial year. These practices are:Drs Toal & McGuire (562)Devenish Practice (563)Lakeside Medical Practice (564)Dr Mallon & McConville (568)Dr Cathcart & Partners (571)Cuilcagh Medical Practice (585)Drumquin Surgery (652)Drumquin Health Care (655)Grange Family Practice (654)Three Spires Surgery (657)Drumragh Family Practice (662)Strule Practice (663)In addition, Maple Healthcare (574) is ready to accommodate a small number of MDT sessions.The decision on which individual practice the MDTs will enter, and the sequencing of that rollout, will be determined locally between the Western Trust and the South West Federation, based on a range of factors including operational considerations, service need and practice readiness.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the date Hydebank and Mallusk MOT centres, will be open to the public
answer_text: The Driver and Vehicle Agency expects the new test centres at Hydebank and Mallusk to open for vehicle testing by the end of 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail which of the 20 GP surgeries in the South West Federation area have indicated that they will need support for infrastructure readiness to facilitate the roll-out of multidisciplinary teams.
answer_text: My Department remains committed to supporting developments in primary care infrastructure and continues to engage with all GP Practices in the South West Federation area to assess options for additional accommodation. This work aims to support the delivery of  Physiotherapy, Mental Health and Social Work services as part of the multi-disciplinary teams (MDT) roll out, alongside core General Medical Services (GMS). We also consider opportunities to increase physical capacity for GP Training consultation rooms and associated accommodation.There are 20 GP Practices operating from 13 premises in the South West Federation Area. Five premises have infrastructure investments already completed and are ready to accept MDT staff.  These Practices include Drumquin Healthcare, Drumquin Surgery, Cuilcagh Medical Centre, Erne Health Centre and Omagh Health Centre. Lisnaskea Health Centre is currently in construction.Business cases have been approved and feasibility/design works are underway for MDT accommodation at Irvinestown Health Centre and Carrickmore Health Centre.  Infrastructure works are expected to be completed by late Spring 2026 and the practices have advised that they can accommodate staff through scheduling arrangements in the interim, if required.Sperrin Family Practice and Rathmore Practice have indicated that they can accommodate staff in their existing premises through scheduling arrangements and no infrastructure work is required at present.Potential solutions are currently being explored in collaboration with Western Trust and the Strategic Planning and Performance Group to address the accommodation needs of Fintona Group Practice, Dromore and Trillick Surgery, and Brookeborough and Tempo Surgery.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on Enniskillen Library capital works.
answer_text: Whilst this is an operational matter for Libraries NI, I understand that they continue to progress plans for the proposed capital project at Enniskillen Library. It is anticipated that works will commence on site by summer 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of council owned car parks.
answer_text: Responsibility for council-owned car parks rests with individual councils following local government reform in 2015. My department does not hold this information.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the number of council owned car parks that have parent and toddler spaces; and (ii) the location of these car parks broken down by constituency.
answer_text: Responsibility for the provision and management of council-owned car parks, including the designation of parent and toddler spaces, rests with individual councils following local government reform in 2015. The Department does not hold this information.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any consideration her Department has given to increasing the financial support provided to apprentices, as they incur additional expenses whilst in placements.
answer_text: Apprentices participating on the ApprenticeshipsNI and Higher-Level Apprenticeships programmes are employees, rather than on placement.  As employees earning a wage, they do not receive extra financial support from the Department.  Employers must pay at least the appropriate National Minimum Wage rate, as determined by HMRC.	The Department fully funds the off-the-job training element of both apprenticeship programmes. This ensures that apprentices do not pay training fees and employers are not financially liable for the delivery of training.  Under both programmes, apprentices who require disability support are provided with appropriate assistance to help them overcome barriers and fully participate in training.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any financial support available to apprentices.
answer_text: Apprentices participating on the ApprenticeshipsNI and Higher-Level Apprenticeships programmes are employees, rather than on placement.  As employees earning a wage, they do not receive extra financial support from the Department.  Employers must pay at least the appropriate National Minimum Wage rate, as determined by HMRC.	The Department fully funds the off-the-job training element of both apprenticeship programmes. This ensures that apprentices do not pay training fees and employers are not financially liable for the delivery of training.  Under both programmes, apprentices who require disability support are provided with appropriate assistance to help them overcome barriers and fully participate in training.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any cross-border work undertaken to prevent the spread of quagga mussels when they were discovered in the Republic of Ireland.
answer_text: Work in Northern Ireland, applicable to all aquatic invasive species, has focused on the Check Clean Dry messaging campaign which has been ongoing since 2011. This has targeted water users (boating, angling, canoeing, etc) to raise aquatic biosecurity awareness and make them aware of the need to clean boat hulls and equipment in contact with water.Colleagues in Ireland have carried out a number of activities since quagga mussels were first confirmed in the River Shannon and Loughs Ree and Derg in July 2021, including communications with all relevant organisations across the island and the maintenance of an online reporting function.A focused cross-border Check Clean Dry signage campaign, funded by the Shared Island Biosecurity and Invasive Species Initiative, is being developed to raise awareness of the ease of spread of all aquatic invasive species. Messaging and final designs are scheduled for confirmation in February.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail why the CCEA will not be introducing the education record app for students to receive GCSE and A Level results, as will be implemented in the rest of the United Kingdom.
answer_text: CCEA Regulation advised that consideration had previously been given to a similar approach to GCSE results publication here. However, concerns were raised about the appropriateness of students aged 15 or 16, receiving their GCSE results potentially on their own, without the opportunity to receive on-site pastoral, academic and careers support from school staff.   As with all aspects of examinations service delivery, the Department and CCEA will keep the matter under review.  It should be noted that AS level and A level students in Northern Ireland, are able to access their results digitally through an online system. It was judged to be appropriate given the age profile of pupils who would be availing of this service.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he was informed that quagga mussels were present in the waterways of the Republic of Ireland.
answer_text: Quagga mussels were first confirmed present in the Republic of Ireland in 2021, before the current mandate. Officials informed me of their presence there by way of background to the first confirmed record in Northern Ireland in December 2025.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail when he became aware of the presence of quagga mussels in (i) the Republic of Ireland; and (ii) Northern Ireland.
answer_text: On 10 December 2025, officials informed me of the presence of quagga mussels in Northern Ireland and, by way of background, of their presence in the Republic of Ireland.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any seps being undertaken to enforce Check Clean Dry protocols to create better biosecurity and avoid the spread of (i) quagga mussels; and (ii) demon shrimp.
answer_text: Quagga mussel and Demon shrimp are not listed on Schedule 9 of the Wildlife Order nor are they Species of Union Concern under EU Regulation 1143/2014.The Check Clean Dry protocols are accordingly advisory. It would be impossible to police every access/egress point on every water body, changing public attitudes to how they use our water bodies is therefore vital.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the timeline of the Good Jobs Employment Rights Bill.
answer_text: The Employment Rights Bill will be introduced to the Assembly following agreement by the Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any (i) costs incurred as a result of moving Royal Enniskillen Grammar School to the former Devenish College site; and (ii) savings that will be made as a result of this decision.
answer_text: To date the costs incurred as a result of moving Enniskillen Royal Grammar School to the former Devenish College site at Algeo Drive, Enniskillen, are in the region of £2m.   This cost includes works to the existing building, external site works, professional fees and security works.  The move to the former Devenish site is a key stage in the delivery of the main construction programme for the new Enniskillen Royal Grammar School.  The main works project will provide a new build school and refurbish the existing Portora House building.  The project is fully designed, with planning approval for the project granted on 19 March 2025.  Vacating the former Portora Royal school site in anticipation of the main works, will realise savings in terms of a better more efficiently delivered construction programme, as the contractor (once appointed) will not have to work around an operational school.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the introduction of sign language as a GCSE.
answer_text: I would refer the member to my previous answer to AQW 22209/22-27, AQW 15113/22-27 and AQW 13803/22-27.  The position has not changed.A process is in train to develop a GCSE in British Sign Language in England.  Once that qualification is available in England it can be accredited for use in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether her Department has considered introducing neonatal care leave.
answer_text: I intend to introduce an entitlement to Neonatal Care Leave and Pay on the same basis as that available in Britain in my forthcoming Employment Rights Bill.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail why neonatal care leave was not introduced in Northern Ireland, at the same time as England, Scotland and Wales.
answer_text: The Assembly and Executive were not functioning when the Bill to introduce the Neonatal Care (Leave and Pay) Act 2023 was first introduced in Britain in 2022. As a result, officials were unable to scope the introduction of, or consult on, a similar entitlement in the North in parallel with developments in Britain.  		The necessary consultation took place as part of the Good Jobs Employment Rights consultation during 2024, and I announced in April 2025 that I plan to introduce this entitlement as part of my forthcoming Employment Rights Bill.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the projects that were completed using funding provided by her Department for rural roads, as announced in previous budgets since 2023-24 and 2024-25.
answer_text: Details of all schemes completed in 2023/24 and 2024/25 were included in my Department’s Annual Report to Council, which can be viewed via the link below:Council Reports | Department for Infrastructure .

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail a breakdown of the £4million for rural roads, as announced in the December Monitoring, broken down by Department of Infrastructure Divisions.
answer_text: In recognition of the importance of the rural road network, c£1m has been allocated to each of the four road divisions to primarily target rural roads for the benefit of rural communities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether the £4million funding for rural roads is ringfenced within Department of Infrastructure Divisions.
answer_text: In recognition of the importance of the rural road network I allocated a further c£4m of funding in the current year to primarily target rural roads across all divisions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail how the success of her Department's road safety awareness campaigns are measured.
answer_text: Subject to budget availability and other factors, such as whether a campaign is a new launch, my Department's road safety awareness campaigns are evaluated using independent quantitative benchmarking and/or tracking studies to understand attitudes on specific driving behaviours and to assess campaign performance and impact. These studies utilise an Omnibus survey in the North to accurately capture audience opinions of the campaign. An Omnibus survey reaches a robust and representative sample of the adult population in a time and cost-efficient fashion.An omnibus study consists of a freshly drawn sample of 1,000 adults in the North, controlled to be representative of the population across sex, age, social class and regional groupings. All questions included are confidential to each individual participating client as is the resulting data, with the full range of demographic details such as religion, working and marital status, household size, presence, and age of children in the household, disability and tenure included as standard. The advertising agency also deploy regular media performance reviews. •	Weekly digital campaign performance report - including reach, engagement and traffic.•	Annual media review - at the end of the campaign year an overall media performance review is conducted. This evaluation includes, reach, frequency, impressions, cost per thousand and added value for money across each channel.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of tonnes of grit that was used from the available stockpile, in the last five years.
answer_text: Over the summer months, our grit barns are filled to capacity in preparation for the beginning of Winter Service. In addition to what the barns can hold, the department also holds a strategic reserve of 20,000 tonnes and a tender is in place for a minimum of 15,000 tonnes of grit each month which is used to re-stock the barns. In spells of adverse weather, these levels are constantly fluctuating.The figures for the total amount of grit used over each of the past five winters, and grit stockpiled prior to commencement of Winter Service are detailed below:YearTotal Salt Used (tonnes)Salt at start of Winter Season (tonnes)2024/202564,200 54,345 2023/202453, 453 49,317 2022/202368,441 55,736 2021/202249,353 54,347 2020/202167,736 58,228

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for a breakdown in tonnes, of the available grit stockpile for Northern Ireland, in the last five years.
answer_text: Over the summer months, our grit barns are filled to capacity in preparation for the beginning of Winter Service. In addition to what the barns can hold, the department also holds a strategic reserve of 20,000 tonnes and a tender is in place for a minimum of 15,000 tonnes of grit each month which is used to re-stock the barns. In spells of adverse weather, these levels are constantly fluctuating.The figures for the total amount of grit used over each of the past five winters, and grit stockpiled prior to commencement of Winter Service are detailed below:YearTotal Salt Used (tonnes)Salt at start of Winter Season (tonnes)2024/202564,200 54,345 2023/202453, 453 49,317 2022/202368,441 55,736 2021/202249,353 54,347 2020/202167,736 58,228

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail what happens to any remaining stockpile of grit, after the normal winter gritting schedule comes to an end.
answer_text: After the normal winter gritting schedule comes to an end, any residual stockpile of grit is retained in the salt barns and is then used for the following winter.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail how grit stockpiles are calculated, to ensure there is enough stock for the winter period.
answer_text: In preparation for the beginning of Winter Service, our salt reserves were filled to capacity over the summer months, with additional re-orders available throughout the winter as required. 55,000 tonnes of salt are held in salt barns across the North in advance of the winter season with an additional 20,000 ton strategic reserve held by the Irish Mining Company, Carrickfergus for use by the Department.Re-orders are set to 90% of full capacity to ensure stock levels are maintained. From 1st January onwards, salt stocks are maintained in a linear fashion against a minimum holding for each depot of 6 days’ supply for two actions per day.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail when school bus fleets are reviewed for end-of-life.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) is responsible for the operation and delivery of school transport, which includes the management of its school bus fleet. The EA advises that its Finance Policy specifies the economic life spans for school buses, grouping them by vehicle size as follows: Description EA Economic Benefit Life (Years)> 33-Seater  14< 33-Seater  10Small Minibus 5The EA school bus fleet is managed in accordance with Public Service Vehicle standards. Buses receive regular safety inspections throughout the year as well as repair and maintenance as required. EA buses often remain in good condition after their economic lifespan and can still be used.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) whether any consideration is being given to changing school bus fleets to electric or hybrid vehicles; and (ii) any assessment of the associated cost of this.
answer_text: (i) The Education Authority (EA) is responsible for the operation and delivery of school transport which includes procurement and management of its school bus fleet. The EA has given consideration to the inclusion of zero emission vehicles within its school fleet and had included the purchase of 2 such vehicles as a pilot exercise in its previous school transport fleet replacement programme. This pilot exercise could not be progressed due to capital funding constraints within the Department of Education budget. It was necessary for the EA to prioritise available funding to replace the maximum affordable number of diesel buses in most critical need. The EA is currently working on a new fleet replacement strategy for 2026 onwards within which consideration will be given to the introduction of zero emission vehicles.(ii) The EA informs me that it is unable to gather cost data outside of Procurement exercises, however previous industry engagement identified that the cost of an electric bus was significantly higher than that of a diesel bus. In addition, charging infrastructure and required electrical network upgrades to the EA estate would incur significant cost.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether any consideration will be given to funding more dedicated children's ambulances via Northern Ireland Specialist Transport and Retrieval.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Specialist Transport and Retrieval Service (NISTAR) is a 24/7 service that facilitates critical care or clinically supported transfer and repatriation of adults, paediatrics and neonates.  The NISTAR service is provided by specialist doctors and nurses who are highly skilled in completing complex patient transfers. The objective of NISTAR is to ensure that resources are in place to deliver a robust, resilient service for the specialist transfer and retrieval of patients between hospitals in Northern Ireland and between hospitals in Northern Ireland and the Republic of Ireland. NISTAR is currently supported by a fleet of five dedicated ambulance vehicles. All of these ambulances are suitable to undertake neonatal, paediatric and adult transfers.The service has one vehicle which was supported with funding made available through a charitable donation. Whilst this vehicle has the same specification as the other ambulances, it has been fitted out with additional features such as themed graphics, LED lighting and a video games console to make the vehicle more child friendly. This vehicle was commissioned specifically to support transfers to and from Dublin as part of the All Island Paediatric Congenital Cardiac Network. When there are no planned transfers to Dublin on any specific day, the vehicle can be used to support neonate and paediatric transfers between hospitals in Northern Ireland. During 2024 there were a total of 152 transfers undertake by the children’s ambulance.There are no plans to commission additional themed children’s ambulances as part of the NISTAR service.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs when he will answer AQW 34809/22-27.
answer_text: AQW 34809/22-27 was answered on 10 December 2025.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 30002/22-27.
To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the pressures facing the health and social care system due to rising flu cases.
answer_text: The health and social care system is experiencing significant seasonal pressures due to a sharp rise in influenza cases. Public Health Agency (PHA) surveillance shows that the 2025/2026 influenza season has already surpassed last year’s peak. In week 49, there were 1,184 new influenza episodes - a 24% increase on the previous week with Influenza A (H3) accounting for the vast majority of cases. Test positivity is now 33%, highest among children aged 5 to 14.Hospital admissions have risen across all age groups, with 346 new influenza-related admissions in week 49 (up to 7 December 2025), up from 264 the previous week. Older people are most affected, with those aged over 75 accounting for the highest admission rate, followed by children aged 0 to 4. Intensive care admissions have also increased, though remain below last season’s peak.Care homes are under particular strain, with 10 new influenza outbreaks reported last week, up from four the previous week. Attack rates ranged from 5% to 23%. All care homes have completed on-site vaccination programmes, achieving 77% uptake among residents, and the PHA continues with proactive outreach and education to reinforce infection prevention and control.Vaccination remains our strongest defence. Since 6 October 2025, 486,426 vaccines have been delivered across schools, pharmacies, GPs, and Trusts. Uptake among those aged over 80 is 81%, and school-based programmes are ongoing to reduce transmission among children, who remain key vectors.My Winter Plan is designed to mitigate, rather than eradicate, the impact of these challenges. It focuses on improving patient flow, increasing capacity in key areas, and strengthening community-based alternatives to hospital care. This includes measures such as enhanced discharge processes, rapid access clinics, and additional staffing support to manage surges in demand.The pressures in Emergency Departments (EDs) are driven not only by attendance numbers but also by the complexity of patient conditions and delays in hospital discharge due to limited social care capacity. This impacts patient flow and waiting times. My long-term vision is to ensure patients access the most appropriate care pathway first time, reducing unnecessary ED attendances through initiatives such as expanding Phone First services, increasing rapid access ambulatory clinics, and strengthening community provision via Hospital at Home.Managing these pressures is complex and requires both immediate actions and longer-term solutions. Staff across health and social care continue to work under extremely demanding circumstances, and their dedication and professionalism are central to sustaining resilience during this period of intense pressure.The earlier start and higher case numbers of the 2025/2026 influenza season have been associated with significant clinical and health service impacts. While the reduction in the rate of increase of new cases

answer_text: The information requested is neither collated nor held by my Department. You may, therefore, wish to address your question directly to the Chief Constable given it relates to an operational matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the average time to process a firearms appeal, in the last two years.
answer_text: The average time to process a firearms appeal, within the last two years is 651 days. This includes a number of older cases.  Within recent years, staffing and resource issues in both PSNI Firearms and Explosives Branch (PSNI FEB) and my Department’s Firearms and Explosives Branch contributed to delays in processing firearms appeals.  Extra resources were allocated to progress older appeals to completion and reduce the backlog.The number of live firearm appeals has been reduced by 20% in the past six months.Firearm appeals are considered on a case-by-case basis with each appeal having varying complexities that require background information to be gathered from PSNI FEB and, in some cases, from other external parties, such as GP’s or other health professionals. Appellants also have the opportunity to make representations and, in many cases, request further time.   Occasionally, an appeal will be held in abeyance pending the outcome of legal proceedings if it is considered relevant to the fitness of the appellant to possess firearms and ammunition.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of PSNI domestic abuse officer posts that are vacant, on a permanent basis.
answer_text: The information requested is neither collated nor held by my Department. You may, therefore, wish to address your question directly to the Chief Constable given it relates to an operational matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any steps her Department is taking to support the PSNI in responding to domestic and sexual violence reports.
answer_text: As my response to AQW 36793/22-27 stated, I welcome the focus that the PSNI has placed on responding to domestic and sexual abuse. My Department is continuing to take forward a range of steps to support them in this, including:currently providing PSNI with annual funding to support the implementation of Gillen recommendations aimed at improving the police response to sexual violence;currently working with PSNI to update Achieving Best Evidence practitioner guidance which will ensure that vulnerable victims and witnesses are supported effectively when providing evidence;introducing significant legislative changes, including new offences to tackle domestic abuse, stalking, non-fatal strangulation, voyeurism and cyber-flashing which have provided law enforcement with a wider suite of tools that can be used to hold perpetrators of domestic and sexual abuse to account;working with PSNI and other criminal justice agencies to establish a pilot to test Domestic Abuse Protection Notices and Orders (DAPN/O), which will provide police with additional powers to offer immediate protection to domestic abuse victims at the point of crisis; and,leading a project to put in place new, enhanced arrangements to improve multi-agency risk assessment and mitigation for high risk victims of domestic abuse which is currently provided by the Multi Agency Risk Assessment Conference (MARAC) system.  The new arrangements will close identified weaknesses in the current process and will strengthen risk assessment, action plans and oversight, whilst also applying a wider whole family lens and considering how to reduce risk posed by perpetrators.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any steps his Department is taking to curtail any spread of bluetongue in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Following the first detection of Bluetongue Virus (BTV) at a farm near Bangor, County Down, my department quickly implemented a 20km Temporary Control Zone (TCZ) around the holding.  The TCZ has expanded with suspected BTV incursions on two further farms in County Down.  Recent announcements have been made regarding movements into the TCZ and the recommencing of trade with Great Britain for animals in the BTV free zone. DAERA vets have also progressed representative sampling and testing on cattle herds in the TCZ to ascertain if there is further infection.Tracings, where necessary, have been completed of any animals which moved onto or off the affected premises on the first infected premises with ongoing work tracing animal movements from the further two infected premises, in the risk window from 1 September 2025.  Any holdings identified as receiving animals from the affected farm will be subject to surveillance and testing if required. An enhanced active surveillance testing protocol was also introduced at Northern Ireland abattoirs in June 2024, and this is ongoing.  You will be aware that it is as a result of this rigorous surveillance programme that my Department operates, that Bluetongue was originally detected in cattle from the premises outside Bangor.In addition, any reports to DAERA by livestock keepers or private veterinarians throughout Northern Ireland of the suspicion of epizootic diseases such as Bluetongue will be thoroughly investigated by my veterinary officials, with the relevant surveillance conducted including sampling and testing at the Agri-Food and Biosciences Institute (AFBI) in order to detect or rule out disease. Updates on this developing situation will be made available on the DAERA website at https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/articles/bluetongue-situation-northern-ireland
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, following the detection of bluetongue, whether his Department is considering providing any support for a vaccine roll-out.
answer_text: I authorised the voluntary use of three bluetongue serotype 3 (BTV-3) vaccines in Northern Ireland, subject to licensing, from July 2025. These vaccines are intended to reduce the severity of clinical signs and mortality in affected animals, though they do not prevent infection or transmission. A decision to vaccinate animals is one for farmers to make in close consultation with their private veterinary practitioners, and there are no plans at this juncture to mandate or support any wide scale vaccine roll out.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, following the detection of bluetongue, whether his Department will consider rolling out any preventive measures outside of the 20 kilometer temporary control zone.
answer_text: My department is keeping this situation under constant review, with a veterinary epidemiological investigation currently ongoing.I can assure you that DAERA will take the necessary steps to implement disease control measures which are proportionate to the risk.  Any potential for preventative measures outside the zone will depend on the evolving disease picture and further findings from these ongoing veterinary investigations. We will continue to keep affected farmers and stakeholders updated as next steps are determined.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, following the detection of bluetongue, whether his Department will consider expanding the area of testing, to try and identify and prevent any further spread.
answer_text: Following the detection of Bluetongue Virus (BTV) at a farm near Bangor, County Down, my Department quickly implemented a 20km Temporary Control Zone (TCZ) around the holding.  This zone includes restrictions on movements of susceptible animals within and out of the area in order to mitigate the risk of onward spread of the virus. DAERA vets are now carrying out representative sampling and testing on 20 large cattle herds in the TCZ to ascertain if there is further infection.Tracings are being conducted of any animals which moved onto or off the affected premises near Bangor, in the risk window from 1 September 2025.  Any holdings identified as receiving animals from the affected farm will be subject to surveillance and testing if required. Further consideration will be given to next steps once the epidemiological picture becomes clearer and we will remain in close contact with affected farmers, and wider industry, on next steps.  Updates on this developing situation will be made available on the DAERA website at https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/articles/bluetongue-situation-northern-ireland
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, following the detection of bluetongue, whether his Department is considering measures to increase its testing capacity.
answer_text: My officials remain in close contact with the Agri-food and Biosciences Institute (AFBI) regarding both capacity for, and ongoing results from, samples submitted by my Department for Bluetongue testing.  My officials will keep the matter of testing capacity under close review.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether preparations are being made within the Driver and Vehicle Agency for the pay-per-mile tax on electric vehicles, following the Chancellor's announcement in the Autumn Budget 2025.
answer_text: Following the budget announcement, Treasury has requested a meeting with the Driver and Vehicle Agency to discuss how electric vehicle excise duty (eVED) will operate here.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any discussions she has had regarding the announcements made on the reduction of car models, consumers are able to access through the Motability Scheme.
answer_text: My Department is not directly involved in setting policy or administering the Motability Scheme, however, I have discussed the proposed changes with the Minister for State for Social Security and Disability as part of wider Budget measures. I welcome the changes announced on the basis that they ensure fairness for the taxpayer, while continuing to support disabled people by enabling them to lease a car, wheelchair accessible vehicle, scooter or powered wheelchair.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of the funding of school places at the Buddy Bear Trust Conductive Education School, Dungannon.
answer_text: As an independent school, Buddy Bear does not fall under the grant-aided status for receiving funding under Article 17A of the Education and Libraries (Northern Ireland) Order 1986.The school is approved by the Department of Education (DE), under Article 26 of the Education (Northern Ireland) Order 1996, as an institution suitable for the admission of children with SEN.This approval means that the Education Authority (EA) is able to place children in Buddy Bear without seeking approval from the department under Article 12 of the Education (Northern Ireland) Order 1996.The average cost of a placement in Buddy Bear equates to £14-15K per term or £42-45K per annum compared to the average cost per special school placement of c£22k per pupil, as identified under the End-to-End (E2E) Review of SEN.Buddy Bear Trust has received just over £62,500 from the Pathway Fund from 2020-21 to 2025-26. They have received a Pathway award of £12,500 in 2025-26.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any considerations her Department is giving to the roll-out of the electric vehicle pay-per-mile tax, following the Chancellor's announcement in the Autumn Budget 2025.
answer_text: The roll-out of the electric vehicle pay-per-mile tax is a wholly reserved matter for the British Treasury. While my officials continue to work with their counterparts to encourage the uptake of zero emission vehicles, my remit does not cover taxation of these vehicles.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any assessment her Department has made on the Chancellor's announcement of a pay-per-mile tax on electric vehicles, in respect of the Vehicle Emissions Trading Schemes implemented in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The roll-out of the electric vehicle pay-per-mile tax is a wholly reserved matter forthe British Treasury. While my officials continue to work with their counterparts to encourage the uptake of zero emission vehicles, my remit does not cover taxation of these vehicles. Electric Vehicle Excise Duty Tax (eVED) will come into force from April 2028. The Vehicle Emissions Trading Schemes legislation is a UK wide legislation that requires a percentage of manufacturer’s new car sales to be zero emission. By 2028 52% of all new cars and 46% of all new vans will be zero emission from the tail pipe.Further information on eVED and the British Government’s consultation can be accessed here: https://www.gov.uk/government/consultations/consultation-on-the-introduction-of-electric-vehicle-excise-duty-eved.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail how the current transport policy is addressing public transport provision in rural areas of Fermanagh and South Tyrone,  particularly in areas where services remain limited for those accessing evening time provision at weekends.
answer_text: My Department’s draft Transport Strategy sets out a new vision and strategic priorities for our transport system through to 2035 aiming to support economic growth, regional balance, and improved connectivity across both urban and rural areas. The public consultation closed at the end of September 2025, and officials are now analysing responses to help shape a system that meets the needs of all communities.My Department provides funding to Translink to provide public transport services and facilities. This means creating a consistent customer experience for everyone, ensuring accessible public transport for people of all ages and abilities. Both the Translink website and App can provide transport options from any location, address or stop, urban and rural, where transport links exist.  The website and app allow for address-to-address planning, park & ride planner and a bike it option.  Community transport schemes, including Dial-a-Lift, provide essential support for those without access to private vehicles, helping them reach key services and employment opportunities. I have allocated an additional 41% in 2025/26 to support the rural Dial-a-Lift transport service in all areas in the north which improves access to local goods and services, helping people travel independently and connected to their local communities.  The level of funding was commensurate with the level of funding requested by those providing this service.Finally, my Department is currently preparing a new Transport Plan for the Fermanagh and Omagh District Council (FODC) area called the Fermanagh and Omagh Sub Regional Transport Plan 2035 (FO SRTP). This Transport Plan is being developed in an integrated manner with the council’s local development plan. The FO SRTP will cover local transport issues (active travel, public transport, the local road network and parking etc.) and will set the framework for transport policy and investment decisions up until 2035. The FO SRTP will provide most detail on the towns of Omagh and Enniskillen. It will also consider local transport issues across the district, where appropriate.The draft Vision and Objectives of the Plan have been agreed. My officials and design team partners are currently at a technical stage in the plan process, refining the list of transport options for the plan area in line with agreed vision and objectives.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any actions his Department is taking following the suspected case of avian influenza in whooper swans at Enagh Lough, Caledon.
answer_text: DAERA’s primary function in respect of avian influenza (AI) is the detection and response to incursions in poultry and captive bird flocks and it is not involved in control of the disease in wild species. DAERA collects dead wild birds for AI surveillance purposes and once AI has been detected in a specific area, further testing is not required and therefore, not all birds will be collected.Where dead wild birds are not required for surveillance purposes the routine collection of dead wild birds and their disposal, if deemed necessary, rests with the landowner.DAERA officials have been working closely with local councils and public sector landowners in relation to their responsibilities as landowners regarding dead wild birds found on their lands. Any decision in that regard is for the council or landowner in conjunction with the Public Health Agency (PHA).DAERA officials remain available to provide advice and guidance as required and officials continue to communicate key messages alongside the PHA, which has advised that human infections with HPAI are rare as it is primarily a disease of birds and the risk to the health of the public is very low. Members of the public are advised not to pick up or touch sick, dying or dead birds, and keep pets away from them. If you find dead waterfowl (swans, geese, or ducks) or other dead wild birds, such as gulls or birds of prey report them using the DAERA Dead Wild Bird Online Reporting Tool on the DAERA Avian Influenza Hub at the following link:  https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/services/daera-dead-wild-bird-online-reporting-toolA wild bird advice poster has also been developed in collaboration with the PHA, communicating the above guidelines. These posters have been distributed to local councils for display in public spaces around NI.  Further advice and guidance can be found on the Department’s website at:  https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/articles/wild-birds-and-advice-public-november-2025
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs
