question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she will consider introducing a Translink bus route between Dungannon Bus Station and Belfast International airport.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and have been advised as follows:Given the current funding climate there are no immediate plans to enhance services that are not  economically sustainable; this includes a service between Dungannon and Belfast International Airport. Translink currently invest in regular services between Dungannon and Belfast, with connections to the high frequency 300 Airport service for those who wish to avail of this option. For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact Translink directly at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.  Making direct contact with Translink will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps he has taking to deliver a real Living Wage for care workers employed by care provider companies.

To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any actions his Department is taking to reassure poultry farmers in Caledon and the surrounding areas, following a suspected case of avian influenza in the area.
answer_text: Since the first confirmed case in this current outbreak, on 3 October 2025, the Department has undertaken frequent engagement and communications with local industry and other stakeholders to heighten awareness and deliver key messages on how to protect the Northern Ireland (NI) poultry flock from an avian influenza incursion.My Department continues to deploy wide ranging communications reminding all flock keepers of the necessity of adhering to high levels of biosecurity.  Further information including comprehensive biosecurity guidance is available on the DAERA website at https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/topics/avian-influenza-ai
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of GP practices, that are Veteran Friendly GP Practices (VFGPP); and (ii) any steps being taken to encourage practices to become an accredited VFGPP.
answer_text: In Northern Ireland there is currently no accreditation for practices for support specifically to veterans.  Under the terms of their contract GPs are required to provide General Medical Services to everyone registered with the GP Practice – this would include veterans.  As such, my Department currently has no plans to introduce specific accreditation for practices who are deemed Veteran Friendly.The information provided at the following gov.uk website may be helpful in terms of services available to veterans: Access a local GP - GOV.UK

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 30898/22-27.
To ask the Minister for the Economy whether her Department provides any support programmes, through regional colleges or other bodies, for those who wish to obtain a HGV licence.
answer_text: My Department does not provide specific support programmes for individuals wishing to obtain an HGV licence. However, a number of the Further Education colleges provide training related to the maintenance and repair of heavy goods vehicles, and some colleges work in partnership with specialist providers allowing learners to access licence-based training alongside wraparound skills.

answer_text: I answered AQW 33664/22-27 on 12 November 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any discussions his Department is having with the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust regarding car parking issues at the Royal Victoria Hospital.
answer_text: The aim of the Department of Health’s policy for Car Parking Provision and Management in the Health & Social Care (HSC) Sector is to ensure the effective provision and management of car parking across all of the Health Estate for patients, visitors and staff. Decisions on how the policy is applied is for each HSC Trust to determine to enable them to take into account local operational issues. The Department of Health’s regional Hospital Parking Charges working group meets regularly with regional HSC Trust representatives, including the Belfast HSC Trust, to discuss any car parking policy issues.The Belfast HSC Trust undertook a scoping exercise for the addition of a modular style car park on the RVH site. Trust staff met with Departmental officials in October 2024 to discuss delivery options and potential for capital funding.In advance of developing more detailed proposals for modular style parking, the Trust have decided it would be prudent to engage with the Planning Service to determine the likelihood of achieving the required planning approval.The development of options to alleviate car parking issues at the Royal Victoria Hospital (RVH) are restricted by the severe financial challenges facing the Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the Stroke Action Plan.
answer_text: My Department’s Stroke Action Plan sets out six priorities for stroke reform in Northern Ireland (NI). While funding and resource pressures have constrained the pace of implementation, important progress has been made in each priority area. Regarding priority one which focuses on prevention, progress includes the development of a regional model for a 7-day Transient Ischemic Attack (TIA) or mini stroke assessment service and the development of action plans for implementation within each Trust. In line with priority two, an audit of thrombolysis has been undertaken which has informed the development of recommendations to help promote the achievement of a target rate of 16% for NI. The focus of priority three is on the thrombectomy service and progress includes the roll out of Artificial Intelligence software to all eight stroke receiving sites to assist in clinical decision making. In addition, it is anticipated that work on a Strategic Outline Case which will set out the resources required for a 24/7 thrombectomy service will be completed early in 2026. Priority four focuses on long term support and rehabilitation. Progress includes the development of regional models for both community stroke services and the management of spasticity.Priority five relates to the introduction of Hyperacute Stroke Care. A Hyperacute Stroke Project Board (HSPB) has been established to oversee a three-phase process to identify the optimum configuration for NI. Phase one of the process which used updated modelling to review measures to drive patient outcomes is now complete. Phase two of the process will involve an assessment of the sustainability of current services. This will be informed by an assessment of the workforce which is currently being progressed by the stroke workforce review under priority six. Priority six focuses on a stroke workforce review which is now underway. A Workforce Review Steering Group was established in June 2025 to oversee this work and an outline workplan was agreed.  The initial focus of the review is on an assessment of the hyperacute and acute workforce which will inform decision making on hyperacute services. It is anticipated that this phase of the review will conclude early in 2026.It is important to note, that the Stroke Action Plan priorities will continue to be progressed as far as possible within current resources. However, full implementation will continue to be impacted by funding constraints.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department are engaging with Translink and the Department for Infrastructure to explore options to alleviate some of the issues of staff travelling to the Royal Victoria Hospital and not being able to avail of car parking permits.
answer_text: Departmental and Belfast HSC Trust officials engage regularly with Translink and Department for Infrastructure (DFI) representatives at both a regional and local level.Issues including the funding of public transport services, the lack of public transport in the early hours and late evenings as well as options for covering the final mile journey between Grand Central Station and the Royal Victoria Hospital (RVH) site are discussed. Translink also share their plans in relation to future service provision and development of the Glider service.  Improvements to the bus services to the RVH have taken place over recent years with improved frequencies as well as the addition of onsite transport infrastructure. A free Park & Ride option from Blacks Road to the RVH has also helped to alleviate traffic and parking pressures on the site. The development of options to further alleviate some of the issues of staff travelling to the RVH are restricted by the severe financial challenges facing the Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on her Department's efforts in preparation of its 2025-26 winter service activities.
answer_text: Prior to the start of each winter service season, my Department carries out a significant amount of pre-planning to ensure a state of readiness. Staffing arrangements have been finalised, pre-season checks on winter service equipment are complete and salt stocks have been replenished. My Department commits significant resources to its winter service programme and every night, from the end of October, up to 300 people will be on standby ready to salt main roads, helping drivers across the North to cope with the wintry conditions. Contracts are also in place to enable contractors and farmers to help to clear roads during periods of prolonged snow. While my Department targets the limited resources available for winter service on the busier main through routes, salt bins and grit piles are provided for use by the public, on a self-help basis, on other routes adopted or maintained by my Department, which do not qualify for inclusion on the gritting schedule. Our salt reserves have been filled to capacity over the summer months in preparation for the beginning of winter service, with additional re-orders available throughout the winter as required.In addition, my Department has an arrangement with Councils to salt city and town centre footways during times of prolonged ice and snow, and the council will be contacted to ensure continuity of this service for the incoming winter season should conditions dictate.My Department’s Winter Service Policy can be viewed via the following link : RSPPG E022, and details of all salting routes can be viewed on my Department’s website via DFI Roads Salted Network.Our routine maintenance work to provide an annual clean to all DfI gullies in each Council area is also on-going alongside the routine cleaning of roadside channels and open drains and will continue throughout the winter season.In preparation for and during flood events local staff will utilise available resources to support community resilience groups in flood risk areas and respond, where possible, to requests for assistance from the public where property has suffered or is threatened by flooding. Departmental depots are fully stocked with sandbags for temporary flood protection which will be distributed directly when required.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any engagement her Department has had with NI Water and the other utility providers regarding the commencement of repairs on the Maydown Road, Benburb.
answer_text: As a result of Storm Amy, a retaining wall on the Maydown Road, Benburb, suffered damage causing a partial collapse of the wall, verge and carriageway. In the interest of public safety, my Department took the decision to close a short section of Maydown Road in Benburb and is working at pace to assess the damage and carry out repairs.   As the collapse left a number of utility pipes and cables exposed, my officials immediately made contact with the relevant third parties to make safe and divert these important services, whilst maintaining an uninterrupted supply to local residents. Communication between my Department and the relevant Utility Providers has continued over the past number of weeks to ensure the coordination and progression of this work. I am pleased to advise the diversionary works have now been completed, and clearance works have commenced on site in the coming days in preparation for the reconstruction of the wall and carriageway. I fully appreciate the impact of the closure and apologise to the local community, including businesses in the area, for the inconvenience caused. Local diversions are in place, and I urge the travelling public to drive with due care and attention and allow extra time for their journeys.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the recent outbreak of avian flu, including the threat it poses to the poultry industry.
answer_text: There have been three incursions of avian influenza (AI) into commercial premises in Northern Ireland (NI). Disease control measures were put in place around each infected premises.  3km Protection Zones and 10km Surveillance Zones remain in place around two commercial premises, one in Pomeroy, County Tyrone and one in Lisnaskea, County Fermanagh.I introduced an Avian Influenza Prevention Zone (AIPZ) across NI, effective from 00:01 on Saturday 1 November 2025. The biosecurity measures required under the AIPZ are vital tools in our fight against this disease.  Furthermore, I took the decision to implement a Housing Order and a ban on certain bird gatherings as additional measures under the AIPZ, effective from Thursday 6 November.  It is now mandatory for all bird keepers, including owners of pet birds, commercial flocks, backyard and hobby flocks, to keep their birds indoors or otherwise separate from wild birds. The Republic of Ireland (ROI) has also confirmed four cases of HPAI H5N1 to date.  The most recent confirmed on Monday 10 November at a turkey premises in Clontibret, County Monaghan. The Surveillance Zone for this case in the ROI encroaches into County Armagh and we have introduced a corresponding zone, as legislation requires. Officials continue to work closely with DAFM counterparts as the situation progresses.The disease picture continues to deteriorate across Great Britian (GB). Since 1 October 2025, over 30 cases of AI in poultry and captive birds have been confirmed. Poultry and egg production contributes significantly to the NI agri-food economy valued at over £600 million. Therefore, any incursion of AI here is of concern however, my Department is implementing prompt and robust disease control measures in close collaboration with industry and wider stakeholders to mitigate for any onward spread and minimise impact.Since the first confirmed case in this current outbreak, officials have undertaken frequent engagement and communications with local industry and other stakeholders to heighten awareness and deliver key messages on how to protect the NI poultry flock.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his Department will provide a support payment for turkey farmers that have been impacted by the suspected outbreak of avian flu in County Fermanagh.
answer_text: Compensation is payable for birds culled at the Department’s direction for disease control purposes.  There are no plans for the introduction of additional financial support.While I acknowledge the impact of an incursion of highly pathogenic avian influenza on farmers, I have to act within the current legal and budgetary framework. I am therefore grateful to industry for the ongoing efforts to protect flocks from avian influenza. I would reiterate the need for all bird keepers to practice the highest levels of biosecurity and adhere to the requirements of the Avian Influenza Prevention Zone which includes the housing of all birds.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, following the suspected avian flu outbreak in County Fermanagh, to detail what advice and support is being provided by his Department to neighbouring farms to minimise the risk of spread.
answer_text: Following an outbreak of avian influenza and implementation of disease control zones, all flock keepers within these zones are contacted by my Department, with information on the restrictions that must be adhered to, in order to mitigate the risk of disease spread.My Department also operates an avian influenza text message alert service which provides updates on developments to all DAERA registered flock keepers.My Department continues to deploy wide ranging communications reminding all flock keepers of the necessity of adhering to high levels of biosecurity.  Further information including comprehensive biosecurity guidance is available on the DAERA website at https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/topics/avian-influenza-ai
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any actions taken by his Department following the suspected avian flu outbreak in County Fermanagh.
answer_text: Details of actions taken by my Department following suspected avian influenza outbreaks, including details and mapping of the control zones established around the three infected premises, can be found at https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/articles/latest-situation. To note that depopulation has also been completed at all sites.		Further, I took the decision to implement mandatory housing measures for all kept birds and poultry across Northern Ireland, effective from Thursday 06 November 2025 as part of the Avian Influenza Prevention Zone (AIPZ) currently in force.		This strengthens actions taken by my Department to ensure mandatory biosecurity measures were implemented, since 1 November 2025. 		Details of the measures that must be adhered to as part of the AIPZ can be found at https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/publications/disease-control-zone-declarations
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail how his Department is addressing the challenges that arise from avian flu.
answer_text: My Department is actively addressing the challenges that arise from avian influenza through a range of measures including the introduction of mandatory biosecurity measures, compulsory housing measures for all poultry and captive birds and a ban on certain bird gatherings. In addition, there has been extensive engagement with a wide range of stakeholders coupled with increased communications across DAERA social media channels to raise awareness of the disease and how flock keepers can help mitigate against further cases. Details on actions taken by my Department in response to suspected and confirmed cases of avian influenza can be found at https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/topics/avian-influenza-ai
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any actions he is taking to bring gynaecology waiting times within clinically-safe timescales.
answer_text: There is currently significant work underway across the health and care system to reduce waiting times and develop pathways for gynaecology-specific conditions within each Trust across Northern Ireland.To support this, the Department has established a Regional HSC Gynaecology Services Forum, which includes representation from Trusts, the Public Health Agency, and General Practitioners.  This forum is overseeing ongoing service improvements and the development of gynaecology service pathways across primary, intermediate, secondary, and tertiary care levels which are clinically led.  It is also responsible for implementing the recommendations of the “Getting It Right First Time” (GIRFT) Gynaecology Report.Most referral pathways begin in primary care, and the gynaecology pathways being developed reflect this.  If a GP is unable to assess or treat a patient locally, the patient may be referred to the Gynaecology Enhanced Primary Care Service—if they meet the criteria—or to secondary care gynaecology services.Primary and Secondary Care teams are working collaboratively to ensure patients are seen in the right place, at the right time, by the right professional.  In addition to accepting referrals from primary care, a pilot initiative is underway to identify patients on secondary care waiting lists who could be managed within the Enhanced Primary Care Service.  Eligible patients will be assessed and treated by one of the 16 specialist GPs with enhanced skills in gynaecology.  This service currently manages referrals for pessary fitting, HRT management, initial assessment and treatment of heavy menstrual bleeding, and contraceptive management including coil fitting.  As of 30 September 2025, the Enhanced Primary Care Service has delivered 1,567 face-to-face appointments and 587 remote consultations at a cost in-year of £1.07m.  When fully operational, it is expected to manage approximately 6,072 patient contacts annually (including both face-to-face and remote appointments).Secondary Care services are also developing action plans to address waiting times and implement relevant GIRFT recommendations within each Trust.  Between April and December 2025, additional non-recurrent funding has been approved of £3.9m to provide waiting list initiatives—both in-house and via the independent sector to address red flag or other time-critical assessments and treatments.  As well as Trusts carrying out administrative validation exercises, to review and cleanse current outpatient waiting lists, clinicians are also carrying out enhanced clinical validation of outpatient lists.  Additional outpatients are also in place to provide one stop clinics for colposcopy, hysteroscopy, smears and pessaries that cannot be provided in primary care.   Trusts have established a Gynaecology Provider Collaborative - February 2025.  As part of this process, two Collaborative sub-groups have been established, namely:  A Clinical Standardisation Group – with Clinical and Director/Assista

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the average waiting times for routine gynaecology referrals, in each Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The Trust responses can be seen below:BHSCT Response:The longest waiting time for a routine gynae appointment within Belfast Trust is presently 3 years 4 months.  Work is ongoing to reduce this waiting time to 3 years by the end of March 2026.NHSCT Response:Routine average waiting times for gynae are 72 weeks.WHSCT Response:Average waiting times for gynae are 120.7 weeks.SHSCT Response:As of Monday 10th November 2025, the average waiting time for Routine gynaecology referrals is: 2 years and 4.5 months.SEHSCT Response: The routine average waiting times for routine gynaecology referrals is 157 weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 30184/22-27, for an update on when Cladagh Glen will be reopened and the associated costs of doing so.
answer_text: Initial cost estimates to reinstate the public path range from £36,000 to £84,000.  However, safety concerns remain given the level and frequency of erosion being experienced on site, the ongoing risk of further collapse of the path and potential for further landslip in the area. Consequently, appropriate geotechnical expertise is currently being sought to provide a specialist evaluation of this risk, which can then inform a sustainable approach to the reinstatement of the public path. The Department has not yet received costs for this work, nor a timeframe for this work to be completed. I am therefore unable to advise at this time as to the level of all associated costs and when the site will be re-opened for safe public access.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in relation to the Health and Social Care NI - Three Year Plan commitment to reform General Dental Services by April 2027, whether consideration been given to the mandatory inclusion of a percentage of NHS dental patients in all dental practices.
answer_text: The Health and Social Care NI Three-Year Plan (2024–2027) includes a commitment to reform General Dental Services by April 2027, with a focus on stabilising Health Service dentistry, improving financial sustainability, and enhancing access. The Department’s approach and current proposals are centred on reform and incentivising participation in Health Service dental provision. Future policy development will continue to explore a range of options aimed at improving access to Health Service dentistry.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the average waiting times for red flag gynaecology referrals, in each Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The following information has been provided by the Trusts:BHSCT Response:Gynaecology Red Flag waiting times are within the 2-week targetNHSCT Response:Gynaecology Red Flag average waiting times 4 weeksWHSCT Response:Gynaecology Red Flags are 1-6 average weeks waitingSHSCT Response:As of the 12th of November, the average waiting times for Red Flag Gynaecology referrals is 6 daysSEHSCT Response: Gynaecology Red Flag average waiting times 4 weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 31915/22-27.

To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department will carry out a review of the reasons for the delay of the UK-Wide BEST4 trial being carried out by the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust, given that permission had been received from the Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency on 16 October 2024.
answer_text: A detailed review of the reasons for the delay of the BEST4 Surveillance Trial being carried out by Belfast Health and Social Care Trust has been performed by the HSC Research and Development Division and the HSC Research Governance service.   However, it is important to note that the permission granted by the Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency (MHRA) on 16 October 2024 was for Great Britain only, not for Northern Ireland. There is an extensive record of correspondence between HSCNI, MHRA and the study sponsor relating to the requirements for the study to take place in Northern Ireland in light of the potential for regulatory differences between NI and GB.  However, it was not until 16th October 2025 that MHRA wrote to HSCNI to provide final clarification about Northern Ireland requirements for the sponsor.  HSC R&D Approvals met with the study sponsor on 23rd October 2025 to discuss these requirements and following that meeting the HSC R&D Approvals Service were then able to share their assessment with sites involved in this study. NI Health Trusts may now participate subject to their own internal approvals process and contractual arrangements with the study sponsor.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any efforts his Department is making to ensure that the UK-Wide BEST4 trial being carried out by the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust can commence as soon as possible.
answer_text: The commencement of the BEST4 surveillance trial is dependent on the completion of internal processes within Belfast Trust.My Department has indicated to the Trust that we would like to see the study commence at an earliest possible date and, through the HSC Research and Development Division, has agreed to provide funding to cover some costs associated with the study

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department will consider providing a hub in the South West Acute Hospital to perform arthroscopies and hand and wrist procedures.
answer_text: My Department continues to explore all opportunities to improve access to orthopaedic services for local residents and the recent partnership arrangement with the independent sector has enabled additional hip and knee procedures to be delivered utilising the surplus theatre space on the South West Acute Hospital site. However, there are a number of factors which would need to be considered before moving to multi-site working for upper limb procedures. Trauma and orthopaedic services are inextricably linked and delivering additional orthopaedic services on the South West Acute Hospital could negatively impact on the trauma rota. Additional equipment would also need to be secured to ensure that any additional surgical lists could be delivered without impacting on the elective orthopaedic surgery currently being delivered on the Altnagelvin site. It should also be noted that concentrating orthopaedic services on a limited number of sites reduces clinical risk and helps improve productivity by minimising the clinical time lost due to travelling across sites.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail when she will be in a position to provide an update on the review of the Energy Efficiency Capital Grant.
answer_text: A comprehensive review of its first year is currently underway, with the outcome expected to be finalised in the coming weeks. The review will set out recommendations for proposed changes to facilitate the re-opening of the scheme, ensure its effective delivery and better support business needs. Following the review, any changes will need to go through a formal business and approvals process, therefore I am unable to confirm a definitive reopening date at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any policies his Department is bringing forward to support patients with arthritis, to assist them to manage their condition and prevent further deterioration.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail any actions his Department is taking to ensure those who need musculoskeletal care and treatment are able to avail of it.
To ask the Minister of Health whether the newly-commissioned theatre space at the South West Acute Hospital for elective surgery will concentrate solely on NHS waiting list patients.
answer_text: My department is actively working to commission all available theatre capacity in the South West Acute Hospital (SWAH), however it will take time and recurrent funding to build the HSC core capacity. In the interim period, we are working in partnership with the independent sector to ensure that SWAH facilities are utilised. The work to establish this and other contracts started in May this year, following the publication of the Elective Care Framework: Implementation and Funding Plan, and we continue to work with a range of independent sector providers to secure additional capacity. While the initial contract is only for this financial year, there is the option to roll the contract forward into next year subject to available funding.The commissioning of the additional theatre space and the associated capacity is being delivered in conjunction with the independent sector. This is a hybrid arrangement where the independent sector will provide the anaesthetist, surgeon, ward and theatre staff while the Western Health and Social Care Trust will be paid to provide radiology, pharmacy, ward clerks, sterile supplies and administrative support. The staff employed will be across all pay bands.    It is expected that approximately 550 primary joint replacements will be undertaken in the newly commissioned theatre space in the SWAH. These procedures will be predominantly primary knees, with 380 procedures expected to be delivered. A further 170 primary hips are also planned to be scheduled before the end of the financial year. The case mix of procedures may change depending on the outcome of patient pre-operative assessments.The newly commissioned theatre capacity will be used to help reduce the backlog of NHS patients who are currently waiting for primary knee or primary hip procedures and we continue to explore other opportunities to increase the range of procedures which can be undertaken on this site. Work is underway to expand gynaecology capacity and once all staff are recruited this will allow two additional inpatient sessions and one additional day case session per week to be delivered.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) whether the commissioning of theatre space at the South West Acute Hospital (SWAH) will aid the return of emergency general surgery at the hospital; and (ii) whether emergency general surgery at the SWAH was part of his decision making in providing investment for the hospital theatre space.
answer_text: The temporary suspension of Emergency General Surgery (EGS) at SWAH, in place since December 2022, is continuing to remain in place.The Western Trust plan to work with all relevant stakeholders to develop a clear vision for health and social care provision in Fermanagh and West Tyrone, including SWAH.The Western Trust have reaffirmed their strong and ongoing commitment to the long-term sustainability and development of SWAH as a vital and successful hospital within the Health and Social Care (HSC) regional network.My decision to commission additional theatre space in SWAH is also to support the implementation of the Elective Care Framework. The commissioning of the additional theatre space and the associated capacity is being delivered in conjunction with the independent sector.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail why the announcement of commissioning theatre space at the South West Acute Hospital was made in the Impartial Reporter newspaper during Assembly recess with no announcement at the time of print made on his Department's website or via the Assembly.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail any actions his Department is taking to reduce the waiting times for diagnostic endoscopy.

To ask the Minister of Health whether there has been any update in relation to the BEST4 study from the ongoing discussions between the HSC Approvals Service, the trial sponsor and the Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency, to resolve the regulatory requirements for the trial to proceed.
answer_text: In relation to the Best4 Surveillance Trial, multiple parties have been involved in discussions about the regulatory requirements to allow the study to proceed.  Following the completion of these discussions, the HSC R&D Approvals Service shared the completed Study Wide Governance Report with Belfast Health and Social Care Trust on the 23 October 2025. The Belfast Health and Social Care Trust will now take the study forward, working with the study team and sponsor to issue site approval and undertake contracting processes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the current waiting times for diagnostic endoscopy.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail any current initiatives being taken by his Department for oesophageal cancer prevention.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail any actions his Department have taken to ensure patients can get access to the BEST4 trial of capsule sponge testing for Barrett's oesophagus and oesophagael cancer.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail any engagement he has had with GP's about the agreed pathway for patients with facial palsy, to assist them with timely and appropriate referrals.
answer_text: A Facial Palsy Regional Planning Group was established in 2023.  Each of the five Health and Social Care Trusts were invited to be a member of the regional group.  The group has developed a new Facial Palsy referral/assessment pathway for adults and children.  This has been a complex process with multiple services involved in the pathway development, and multiple access points into the service across all Trusts in Northern Ireland.  These standardised pathways will ensure appropriate treatment of patients in local services as far as possible and will also outline the appropriate referral pathway for those requiring more enhanced specialist intervention. To assist patients who are experiencing facial palsy symptoms a new NI Direct webpage page has recently been established https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/conditions/facial-palsy    This will signpost patients to helpful information whist waiting for assessment.  A video has been produced by a service user advocate, Janet Robb which has recently been used on social media platforms to promote the new NI Direct Webpage.A webinar was held on Thursday 18th September to raise awareness about the new Facial Palsy referral/assessment pathway. The Webinar was targeted at Primary and Secondary care clinicians across the region. The webinar was also recorded and is now available on the GPNI website, which is a trusted platform used by primary care teams across Northern Ireland, including GPs, nurses, pharmacists, and allied health professionals. Hosting a webinar here ensures you're reaching the right people with relevant content.  A link has also been shared with Secondary Care teams.  GPNI has a mailing list of over 3,000 subscribers, ensuring strong visibility.  GPNI is recognised as an impartial and effective regional communications platform. Despite several prompts, to date my officials have not received a business case from the (i) Belfast Health and Social Care Trust; and (ii) South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust, for the management of facial palsy patients who require facial therapy from a specialist therapist as part of their treatment pathway.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department currently lease the former premises of St Eugenes School, Lisnaskea.
answer_text: The former St Eugene’s Primary School closed on 31 August 2013. The site was sold by the school Trustees on 4 October 2019 and the outstanding Grant Recovery was subsequently repaid to the Department. As this is no longer a vested school site, the use of these premises is matter for the present owners.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department is still responsible for St Eugenes School, Lisnaskea.
answer_text: The former St Eugene’s Primary School closed on 31 August 2013. The site was sold by the school Trustees on 4 October 2019 and the outstanding Grant Recovery was subsequently repaid to the Department. As this is no longer a vested school site, the use of these premises is matter for the present owners.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of breaches of Article 87 of the Roads (Northern Ireland) Order 1993, since the Order has been in statute.
answer_text: My Department does not hold records on the number of breaches of Article 87 in a readily accessible format and any attempt to do so would require an extensive exercise to be conducted across the department which might only be expected to produce data with a limited degree of exactitude, and this exercise itself would very likely involve disproportionate costs.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any support his Department can provide for heritage buildings and their maintenance.
answer_text: I have more than doubled the support available this year to listed building owners from £240k to £500k via the department’s Historic Environment Fund.  I am keen to do more, and my officials are currently developing a business case, building upon departmental research published earlier this year, looking at how best to stimulate investment in the historic environment. This will seek to improve support for such homeowners and, recognising the wide benefit of these historic assets to our communities, help them meet required repair and maintenance costs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the current measures that are being taken to monitor the water quality of Lough Erne.
answer_text: The Water Environment (Water Framework Directive) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2017 sets out monitoring requirements. For Lough Erne this includes:Monthly sampling of general physico-chemical elements, specific pollutants, and priority substances.Phytoplankton sampled annually in July, August, and September.Sampling of macrophytes, diatoms, fish, and hydromorphology every six years.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail what his Department has delivered for Fermanagh and South Tyrone, since he took office in February 2024.
answer_text: Since I came into office in February 2024, my Department has delivered the following for Fermanagh and South Tyrone:£2.78 million to various Voluntary and Community organisations£388K for Mid Ulster District Council Revitalisation Scheme Phases 1 and 2£915K for Perry Street Urban Development Grant£749K for Covid Recovery Small Settlement Scheme£1.17 million for Neighbourhood Renewal Fund£9K for Cost of Living Voluntary and Community Supports£27,450 for Football Ground Business Case Development Fund to Dungannon Swifts£26,571 for Gaelic Sports and £28,138 for Rugby Union for Renewable Energy FundSupported the network of 5 public libraries£3 million for new library in FivemiletownMake the Call - 1,662 people supported to access additional benefits of £3.8m£970,600 for 4,050 Winter Fuel claimants in 2024/25Labour Market Partnership - £775,000 for Fermanagh & Omagh, £738k for Mid Ulster 5 job fairsSupporting People providers within the Fermanagh and Omagh District Council area received £4.98 million in 2023/24 and £5.1 million in 2024/2537 Co-Ownership completions6 schemes delivered, creating 122 new social homes167 home improvement grants issued209 homes supported through the Affordable Warmth Scheme, with 520 measures installedNIHE maintenance carried out on 926 dwellings£67,010 for Historic Environment Fund Repair Stream£27,450 for Historic Environment Fund Research and Revival Stream£2.25k Historic Environment Fund Monument Regeneration Stream£29K for Village Catalyst Viability GrantDelivery of new Tourism route – Lough Erne Pilgrim Way9 new buildings added to list of architectural or historic interest£53,521 for Access and Inclusion Programme £38,919 for pool pods and wet gallery at Dungannon Leisure Centre

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether her Department will work with the PSNI to take increased action to tackle the illegal use of e-scooters on public roads in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The Department for Infrastructure (DfI) has lead responsibility for road safety and has confirmed that e-scooters remain illegal for use on public roads and public spaces in Northern Ireland unless they meet the requirements of a motor vehicle. I am aware of the harm and risks associated with the illegal use of e-scooters on public roads and note that the PSNI has confirmed road safety is a strategic priority, with collaborative work ongoing with a number of external partners to address legislative concerns, develop opportunities to engage with and educate the public on various road safety issues and undertake enforcement activity in order to make Northern Ireland’s roads safer.   It is important that a partnership approach continues to be taken to identify and address all community safety issues, including those relating to road safety. My Department and the NI Policing Board jointly fund Policing and Community Safety Parternships (PCSPs), which are statutory standalone bodies based in each council area, with membership including elected members, independent members and seven Designated Organisations (the PSNI; NI Housing Executive; Youth Justice Agency; Probation Board NI, NI Fire and Rescue Service; Health Trusts and Education Authority). PCSPs engage with local communities to help identify community safety issues, including road safety concerns, and develop action plans to help address these. I am aware that several PCSPs have integrated e-scooter messaging within broader road safety work, with examples including: collaboration with the PSNI to deliver safety talks in schools and youth settings; sharing PSNI social-media content and promoting an e-scooter leaflet; targeted poster and leaflet campaigns raising awareness of the legal position; and, in one area, a social-media and leaflet initiative highlighting that vehicles used for anti-social behaviour may be seized under Article 62 of the ASB Order 2004.  While a partnership approach is key to identifying and helping address community safety issues, I am mindful that dealing with any criminal activity, such as illegal e-scooters, remains an operational matter for the PSNI, with the Chief Constable operationally independent and accountable to the NI Policing Board. You may therefore wish to also direct any concerns regarding this issue to the PSNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 30002/22-27.
To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the Dilapidation Bill.
answer_text: The Dilapidation Bill is currently in Committee Stage, which has now been extended to 15 May 2026 to allow the AERA Committee adequate time to scrutinise the Bill.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice when she will introduce her Sentencing Bill to the Assembly.
answer_text: It remains my intention that the Bill will be introduced before the end of 2025, subject to Executive approval.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 30073/22-27.

To ask the Minister of Health to outline what input his Department will have with the Western Health and Social Care Trust on the next steps of the consultation process on emergency general surgery at the South Western Acute Hospital.
answer_text: As you will be aware, the Western Trust Board has now taken the decision to close the current Public Consultation and will now move forward on visioning work for ‘Future Health and Care Services in Fermanagh and West Tyrone’.The Western Health & Social Care Trust advised that it now plans to work with all relevant stakeholders to develop a clear vision for health and social care provision in Fermanagh and West Tyrone, with a particular focus on SWAH.  My officials are already working closely with Western Trust colleagues on the scope of the work to be undertaken.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline what input his Department will have on the visioning work for Future Health and Care Services in Fermanagh and West Tyrone.

To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of what the Western Health and Social Care Trust's statement on the emergency general surgery at the South Western Acute Hospital consultation will mean for the Hospital Network consultation.
answer_text: The Hospital Network consultation report is currently being finalised and will shortly be presented to me for consideration.  Once I have approved the report, it will then be published. It is important to recognise that the Hospital Network consultation document did not propose any specific service changes. Instead, it set out high level principles to provide a strategic context for future reconfiguration decisions, including any such proposals taken forward by Trusts, and to facilitate greater collaboration to move towards an increasingly networked approach to managing our hospitals.The Western Health & Social Care Trust advised that it now plans to work with all relevant stakeholders to develop a clear vision for health and social care provision in Fermanagh and West Tyrone, including SWAH.  There is, therefore, no need to await any individual Trust vision plans before publishing the outcome of the Hospital Network consultation. I would expect any vision plan developed by a Trust to align with the objectives and commitments of his HSC Three Year Plan, published in December 2024, and the Reset Plan, published in July this year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will seek an update on a timeline for the visioning work for Future Health and Care Services in Fermanagh and West Tyrone following the Western Health and Social Care Trust's announcement in relation to the consultation.
answer_text: The Western Health & Social Care Trust has advised that it plans to work with all relevant stakeholders to develop a clear vision for health and social care provision in Fermanagh and West Tyrone, including SWAH.  I would expect any vision plan developed by a Trust to align with the objectives and commitments of my HSC Three Year Plan, published in December 2024, and the Reset Plan, published in July this year.My officials are working closely with Western Trust colleagues on the scope of the work to be undertaken and, as yet, the timeline for completion is not confirmed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the temporary control zone measures enacted following the avian flu outbreak near Omagh.
answer_text: Details of measures to be adhered to within disease control zones enacted following an outbreak of avian influenza can be found on the DAERA website at:https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/publications/disease-control-zone-declarations
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of whether current staffing levels are sufficient to manage the waiting times for initial appointment for cataract referrals in the ophthalmology department, in the Western Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: I acknowledge that the current waiting times for ophthalmology are lengthy and growing across Northern Ireland. This situation has arisen due to several factors, including funding that falls short of objectively assessed need and previous missed opportunities to reform health and social care delivery. I am committed to addressing this, subject to available funding.Analysis has identified sustained demand-capacity gap across ophthalmology regionally, and this includes the Western Health and Social Care Trust area. This is due to a number of factors, including demographic pressures, new and emerging treatment modalities, and the long-term condition/ nature of many ophthalmic diseases.Cataract typically accounts for around 30% of all referrals into ophthalmology, with a conversion rate of approximately 85% of these being deemed suitable for surgery and where surgery is indicated.Waiting lists and times are a Departmental and Ministerial priority, including those for ophthalmology. Extensive work has been ongoing to manage and reduce the cataract surgery waiting list. In order to address this waiting list and times, a network of three Cataract Day Procedure Centres was established in 2018/19 to manage lists regionally, to equalise and reduce waits across Northern Ireland and to avoid clinic and theatre short notice cancellations that may happen in larger more acute hospital settings due to emergencies. These measures have seen regional urgent surgical cataract waiting lists fall from over 3,700 in March 2021 to close to zero today.The Western Health and Social Care Trust has embraced these service reforms and has been at the vanguard of leading change; adopting a multi-disciplinary team (MDT) approach that maximises all resources available and using non-medical staff (optometrists, nurses and orthoptists) in roles previously undertaken by consultants. Although consultant-led the one-stop pre-assessment outpatient clinics required to meet cataract demand are now routinely delivered within this MDT approach.My Department has maintained a strong focus on productivity and efficiency within the cataract pathway, but additional capacity will be needed to meet demand, including within Western Health and Social Care Trust. To this end, part of the £215m available within my current Elective Care Framework package will be to facilitate capacity-building, and my officials are currently working with Western Trust around a business case to inform investment priorities in ophthalmology.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline the urgent actions her Department is taking to repair the Maydown Road, Benburb, following the damage caused by Storm Amy.
answer_text: As a result of Storm Amy, a retaining wall on the Maydown Road, Benburb suffered damage causing a partial collapse of the wall, verge and carriageway. In the interest of public safety, my Department took the decision to close a short section of Maydown Road in Benburb and is working at pace to assess the damage and carry out repairs.   I fully appreciate the impact of the closure and apologise to the local community, including businesses in the area, and all road users for the inconvenience caused. Local diversions are in place and I urge the travelling public to drive with due care and attention and allow extra time for their journeys.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any support and advice being provided by his Department to neighbouring farms to minimise the risk of spread of avian flu, following the outbreak near Omagh.
answer_text: Following an outbreak of avian influenza and implementation of disease control zones, all flock keepers within these zones are contacted by my Department, with information on the restrictions that must be adhered to, in order to mitigate the risk of disease spread.My Department also operates an avian influenza text message alert service which provides updates on developments to all DAERA registered flock keepers.My Department continues to deploy wide ranging communications reminding all flock keepers of the necessity of adhering to high levels of biosecurity.  Further information including comprehensive biosecurity guidance is available on the DAERA website at https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/topics/avian-influenza-ai
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the staffing levels in the ophthalmology department, dealing with cataract referrals, in the Western Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: All cataract referrals to the Western Health and Social Care Trust are triaged by the Trust’s Consultant team, graded as either routine or urgent, and put on the waiting list for the Cataract Day Procedure Centre or, if more complex, within the acute hospital setting at either Altnagelvin or Omagh Hospital and Primary Care Complex. On referral and subject to triage outcome these patients can be seen in one-stop pre-assessment clinics delivered within the multi-disciplinary team (MDT) by nurse specialists, GP with special interests, optometrists and Consultants at clinic.The funded consultant compliment at Western Health and Social Care Trust Ophthalmology service is 9.5 wte, with current head-count at eight consultants. Recruitment into these vacancies is a Trust and commissioning priority.I acknowledge that the current waiting times for ophthalmology are lengthy and growing across Northern Ireland. This situation has arisen due to several factors, including funding that falls short of objectively assessed need and previous missed opportunities to reform health and social care delivery. I am committed to addressing this, subject to available funding.Although my Department has maintained a strong focus on productivity and efficiency within the cataract pathway, it is recognised that additional capacity will be needed to meet growing demand, including within Western Health and Social Care Trust. To this end, part of the £215m available within my current Elective Care Framework package will be to facilitate capacity-building, and my officials are currently working with Western Trust around a business case to inform investment priorities in ophthalmology. Given the challenges around consultant recruitment outlined above, additional investment may be proportionately directed towards recruiting into the MDT skillset.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline any engagement her Department had with (i) broadband; and (ii) telephone communication providers, in advance of Storm Amy.
answer_text: Officials from my Department contacted Openreach, Fibrus, BT, Virgin Media O2 and VodafoneThree in advance of Storm Amy, asking to be kept informed of significant impacts on their networks.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of her Department's level of preparedness, in advance of Storm Amy.
answer_text: Preparations for Storm Amy began at the earliest opportunity with multi-agency co-ordination taking place ahead of the weekend, well in advance of the storm arriving on Friday and prior to the severe weather warnings coming into effect.Alongside our multi-agency partners, officials worked in difficult conditions to respond and minimise disruption. This was a multi-weather event which brought wind and rain causing widespread fallen trees and flooding issues. Approximately 2005 sandbags were distributed and staff worked through the weekend to clear debris to help flood waters to get away. This work did not just start when the storm arrived - proactive culvert screen maintenance work was undertaken by staff prior to the event and existing flood alleviation schemes prevented flooding in many areas. This preventative work contributed to hundreds of homes being protected.As a result of Storm Amy, my Department responded to reports of approximately 1500 incidents and work on the clear-up is now well advanced. Following the initial clear up, it will take a period of time to assess and repair damage to the network.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline any engagement her Department had with NIE Networks, in advance of Storm Amy.
answer_text: On Wednesday 1 October 2025, my officials attended a Local Impact Assessment Call hosted by Northern Ireland Emergency Preparedness Group, following a Met Office weather warning for Storm Amy.  On Friday 3 October 2025, my officials attended a Tactical Co-Ordination Group (TCG) meeting, hosted by PSNI in relation to Storm Amy.At both meetings NIE Networks provided an overview of the escalation measures it had taken in preparation for Storm Amy.TCG meetings continued into Friday evening and throughout Saturday, with NIE Networks keeping my officials informed throughout the event.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline any support his Department can provide to schools, who have had facilities damaged following Storm Amy.
answer_text: The Education Authorities (EA) Tactical Coordination Group was activated on Friday 3 October 2025 to provide practical support to schools, Education Other Than at School and youth facilities. The EA has advised that support was provided to 33 schools that had reported storm-related incidents to the Maintenance Service over the weekend of Storm Amy. These mainly involved damage to roofs and fallen trees. The EA also co-ordinated with Northern Ireland Electricity Networks to identify and resolve power outages to schools ahead of re-opening on Monday 6 October. EA can confirm there were 90 schools without power on 3 October, and all were restored by Sunday 5 October. EA Maintenance Service continues to provide support to those schools affected by Storm Amy through the Maintenance Helpdesk and out of hours contact numbers.Voluntary Grammar and Grant Maintained Integrated schools can submit Unavoidable Minor Works Applications to my Department via minorworks@education-ni.gov.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of his Department's level of preparedness in advance of Storm Amy.
answer_text: I consider that my Department was sufficiently prepared for Storm Amy.  The Department has in place emergency response and business continuity plans which can be activated if required. In the case of Storm Amy the Department received early warning information which was shared with relevant Departmental business areas and was involved in related multi agency meetings.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the amount that her Department has spent to support apprenticeships, in each of the last three years.
answer_text: My Department funds the directed, off-the-job training element of an apprenticeship delivered under the ApprenticeshipsNI and Higher Level Apprenticeship programmes.Training providers are funded for delivering the training. Under the ApprenticeshipsNI programme, employers are eligible to receive an incentive payment upon the apprentice’s successful achievement of a Level 2 or Level 3 apprenticeship.Departmental funding in support of both programmes over the last three years is set out in the table below.  This includes funding in relation to Covid intervention support for apprenticeships.2022-23(£,000)2023-24(£,000)2024-25(£,000)ApprenticeshipsNI22,96227,24137,670Higher Level Apprenticeships6,7408,76510,570Covid Apprenticeship Support5,037(1,102)56Total34,73934,90448,296			Notes		Covid Apprenticeship Support relates to my Department’s Recruit and Return, Retain, Result intervention programmes.  	The negative spend in 2023-24 against Covid Apprenticeship Support is due to an over accrual in 2022-23.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any measures her Department is taking to support local businesses with the insurance costs associated with taking on trade apprenticeships.
answer_text: Whilst there is no specific funding available towards insurance costs for employers who take on apprentices, my Department fully funds the directed, off-the-job training element of an apprenticeship, investing over £45 million annually across the ApprenticeshipsNI and Higher Level Apprenticeship programmes. This funding ensures that employers are not financially liable for the delivery of apprenticeship training.Businesses are also eligible for an employer incentive payment upon the apprentice’s successful achievement of a Level 2 or Level 3 apprenticeship framework, ranging from £670 to £2,008 depending on the occupational area. This incentive is designed to assist employers with the broader costs of supporting and mentoring apprentices. 	Employers in the North are also exempt from paying Class 1 National Insurance contributions for apprentices under the age of 25, subject to certain conditions being met. I want to help more employers and individuals benefit from our apprenticeship model.  I recently published an Apprenticeship Action Plan which aims to ensure that apprenticeship pathways bring real value to businesses.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when her Department plans to carry out resurfacing works in Moygashel Park, Dungannon.
answer_text: My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government.While there are many roads that would benefit from investment, due to limited budget resources, resurfacing work is taken forward through the prioritisation of those deemed most in need for intervention.Details of the resurfacing schemes planned for the 2025/26 financial year will be included in my Department’s Annual Report to Council.Officials will continue to undertake regular inspections and repair defects in accordance with my Department’s Limited Service policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment of the challenges for local businesses in taking on trade apprentices.
answer_text: I recognise that whilst apprenticeships provide significant benefits, developing the next generation of skilled workers requires commitment from businesses - including those employing trade apprentices.The latest Employer Skills Survey 2022 highlighted several perceived barriers including the costs associated with training, limited capacity to mentor apprentices and uncertainty around future business demand. My Department is committed to strengthen and grow the apprenticeship provision throughout the north and to support this we have recently launched an Apprenticeship Action Plan which sets out a strategic framework to improve, grow, and make apprenticeships more inclusive. The Plan includes measures to:Enhance support for employers, especially SMEs.Promote innovative delivery models.Strengthen partnerships with training providers and industry bodies.Increase participation from under-represented groups. While some challenges, such as economic uncertainty and sector-specific constraints, are outside the Department’s direct control we remain committed to working collaboratively with stakeholders to support apprenticeship uptake.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to provide an update on the Digital Skills Action Plan 2024-2034.
answer_text: My Department and officials continue to progress the actions within the Digital Skills Action Plan. This is a 10-year collaborative plan, developed by my Department, the Department of Education (DE) and industry, to encourage more people into the digital sector. The actions which my Department are responsible for and have progressed, include the launch of the most recent Skills Barometer, which forecasts future demand in sectors, including digital. In addition, the Women Returners programme supported through the Skills Fund engaged 135 females to enhance their digital skills. Regarding apprenticeships, business have the flexibility to set a ratio to train apprentices which suit both the individual and business, and Higher-Level Apprenticeships participants pursuing an ICT-related qualification continue to increase in number. DE is progressing three actions under the Digital Skills Action Plan. Following a Strategic Review of the Curriculum, DE has launched a major curriculum reform programme, led by a new Curriculum Taskforce. This includes development of a digital skills framework to support teaching of coding, computational thinking, data handling, and digital literacy ensuring young people are prepared for life and work in a digital world. DE will continue to work collaboratively with other departments and stakeholders to ensure that the education system plays a central role in delivering the ambitions of the Digital Skills Action Plan. Digital Industry are due to give an update to the Northern Ireland Skills Council in January 2026 with their progression of actions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any actions he is taking to increase the capacity of NHS dentistry in Fermanagh and South Tyrone.
answer_text: Access to General Dental Services is challenging across Northern Ireland at present however, I am aware that some areas of the country have historically lower registration and treatment rates – particularly in the West, including the Fermanagh and Omagh District Council area. This is at least partly due to geography; areas with a low population density will have a lower density of practices.It is important to remember that General Dental Practitioners (GDPs) are self-employed, independent contractors who are free to choose the amount of HS care they provide. Nor does the Department have a role in deciding the location of new practices as this is a business decision made by practice owners.In May I announced a £7m investment in General Dental Services for 2025/26. This investment is funding the continuation of the Enhanced Child Examination Scheme and 30% enhancement on fees paid to dentists for Health Service fillings, extractions, and root canals; alongside a new £1.6m allocation to provide additional support to dental practitioners who continue to provide Health Service dental care for their patients.While I am pleased to have been able to announce this investment, it has been made in the face of a £600m deficit for my Department, which has severely limited my ability to implement the kind of transformative change we would all like to see. Longer term, my Department’s Health and Social Care NI three-year plan includes a commitment to develop an action plan and programme of work for the reform of General Dental Services by April 2027. While that work is progressed consideration will be given to other actions which could be taken to improve access to HS dental care for both registered and unregistered patients.As for the particular issues in Fermanagh and South Tyrone, the challenges in Dental Services, and other HSC services, in rural areas like Fermanagh are not limited to Health alone. The same challenges are evident in education, economy and infrastructure both built and otherwise. In this matter, improving health outcomes is not the sole purview of the Department of Health. Addressing rural challenges will require the full weight of the Northern Ireland Executive to address.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the work his Department is undertaking to introduce a practitioner health service for doctors and dentists.
answer_text: Commissioning of a Practitioner Health Programme for Northern Ireland is currently under consideration by my Department, however, the current financial position of my Department makes the introduction of new services particularly challenging at this time.
