question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) whether he has considered amending the Forestry Grant Scheme to include support for the clearance of windblown timber following exceptional storms, as is the case with the Scottish scheme; and (ii) whether there are any other emergency support mechanisms being considered by his Department for this scenario.
answer_text: Timber harvesting operations, including clearance of windblown trees takes place within a competitive commercial market environment. My Department is not considering amending forestry grant schemes to include support for harvesting windblown trees.However, the Department’s Forest Service administers a Woodland Investment Grant which provides grant aid for the replanting of woodlands following felling, which includes clearance of storm damaged areas. This is to assist replanting sustainable and resilient woodlands and details of the grant are available via the Department’s website.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail why Northern Irish medical students studying in England are unable to avail of a bursary, like their English student counterparts.
answer_text: Health and Student Finance are devolved matters, and the support arrangements vary markedly across the UK, depending upon where the individual is ordinarily resident. Policy reflects the resources available to each administration and their assessment of workforce supply priorities. My Department currently pays final year tuition fees for Northern Ireland domiciled students undertaking a primary medical degree at a UK Medical School. Tuition fee support may also be available for medical students undertaking an intercalated year as part of their degree. In addition to that fee support students may also be eligible for a means tested bursary which is household income assessed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he has considered introducing an emergency reconstitution scheme to assist private owners with the costs of replanting and dealing with storm-damaged crops.
answer_text: I have no plans to introduce an emergency reconstitution scheme to assist private owners with the costs of replanting and dealing with storm-damaged crops.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline the off-street parking solutions, considered by her officials, for those wishing to install a domestic electric vehicle charge cable across a public footpath.
answer_text: I have recently launched a trial of two charging solutions that will enable EV owners without access to a driveway, to charge their vehicle at home. The first option is an ‘engineered channel’, where the footway is excavated to install a shallow channel that extends across the footway from the property to the kerb line, with a cover that allows the cable to be inserted and retracted. The second option, which will be progressed via a six-month rolling pilot, will involve the use of temporarily placed cable covers on a footway. The trial will include ongoing monitoring and evaluation to inform a permanent way forward for both solutions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on Project Gigabit.
answer_text: Project Gigabit is still in the procurement phase as my officials, along with Building Digital UK (BDUK), are assessing new commercial rollout plans that became available over the summer. That assessment is due to complete shortly, allowing the procurement to be completed and a contract to be awarded, subject to all necessary approvals and value for money assessments being given.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when her Department plans to carry out resurfacing works in Killymerron Park, Dungannon.
answer_text: My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government.While there are many roads within the Mid Ulster District Council area that would benefit from investment, due to limited budget resources, resurfacing work is taken forward through the prioritisation of those deemed most in need for intervention.Details of the resurfacing schemes planned for the 2025/26 financial year will be included in my Department’s Annual Report to Council.Officials will continue to undertake regular inspections of the road network and repair any defects in accordance with the Department’s Limited Service policy.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the amount of funding, and other support, that will be made available to (i) Fermanagh and Omagh District Council; and (ii) Mid Ulster District Council, under the Ending Violence Against Women and Girls strategy.
answer_text: To date, Fermanagh and Omagh District Council and Mid Ulster District Council have been awarded £135,000 and £165,000 respectively from the EVAWG Local Change Fund. These amounts may increase as part of the £2.2m additional funding secured for EVAWG projects, the process for allocating this funding is underway. TEO officials have supported Councils in delivery of actions to end violence against women and girls with advice and guidance, as well as providing opportunities for learning and collaboration such as the Showcase and Shared Learning Event held in June of this year.All Council areas are also benefitting from support and activities provided under the £1.4m EVAWG Regional Change Fund. The eight organisations currently funded are detailed below.  Women’s Aid Federation of NIRelate NINexusBarnardo’s White Ribbon NI NSPCCWomen’s Resource & Development Agency  Youth Action

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the amount of funding his Department awarded to Sure Start projects, in Fermanagh and South Tyrone, in the 2024-25 financial year.
answer_text: Table A shows the 2024-25 funding allocations to the Sure Start projects which provide services in the Fermanagh and South Tyrone constituency. Table A: Sure Start Projects - Funding Allocations*Sure Start ProjectConstituencies in Catchment Area2024-25Cherish Sure StartFermanagh & South Tyrone and West Tyrone£1,102,555Rainbow Sure StartFermanagh & South Tyrone and West Tyrone£579,145Clogher Valley Sure StartFermanagh & South Tyrone £624,516Dungannon Sure StartFermanagh & South Tyrone and Mid Ulster£889,140Total £3,195,356*The Department of Health (DoH) which administers the programme on behalf of the Department of Education (DE), advises that it is not possible to disaggregate individual Sure Start project allocations where project catchment areas span more than one constituency.In addition, DoH administers centrally held funding to provide further regional support to all 38 Sure Start projects. Table B shows the total centrally held funding in 2024-25.Table B: Sure Start Programme – Central Funding Allocation**2024-25Total £1,336,154**DoH advises that it is not possible to disaggregate central funding values by individual Sure Start project allocation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on her Department's plans to provide active travel routes in the Fermanagh and South Tyrone constituency.
answer_text: Delivery of future active travel routes within our towns and cities will be prioritised primarily in line with the emerging Active Travel Delivery Plan, including Enniskillen and Dungannon in the Fermanagh and South Tyrone constituency. Public consultation on the draft plan closed on 28 February 2025 and my officials are now considering the responses received with a view to presenting a final plan to me for consideration shortly. I hope to be in a position to publish the final plan by the end of this year. In addition, my officials are currently developing a number of active travel schemes within the Fermanagh and South Tyrone Constituency that are at various stages of the design process including Boa Island Road, Belleek and Granville and Mullaghmore roads, Dungannon. Advanced site works have also commenced along the A32 Irvinestown road, Enniskillen.My Department also carries out many other activities for the benefit of pedestrians and cyclists and details of upcoming footway resurfacing and other pedestrian enhancements will be outlined in my department’s annual reports to Mid Ulster District Council and Fermanagh and Omagh District Council.Furthermore, officials regularly engage with councils, including Fermanagh and Omagh District Council and Mid Ulster District Council regarding delivery of their greenway priorities. My department recently issued a capital funding Letter of Offer to Fermanagh and Omagh District Council to support geotechnical survey work as part of the scheme development of the Sligo to Enniskillen Greenway. With respect to Mid Ulster District Council, in the 2024-25 Greenway and Active Travel Enabling Grant Funding Round, the department issued Letters of Offers to support active travel enhancements in Dungannon Park. To provide continued support to councils to deliver on their greenway priorities, I also recently instructed officials to invite councils to bring forward greenway projects to be considered for 50% departmental capital funding in 2025-26.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline her Department's future plans for Enniskillen Courthouse.
answer_text: Enniskillen Courthouse is currently designated as a Hearing Centre and is opened for three days per week primarily for the delivery of Magistrates’ Court business. The NICTS Estate Strategy, published in 2023, provides a long-term framework for investment over the next decade and beyond.To implement the Strategy, NICTS is using data and evidence to baseline the existing estate against the agreed Strategic Outcomes, assess the adequacy of its buildings, and in the context of the finite resources available, develop prioritised investment plans that will be subject to public consultation, as appropriate.Developing future plans for Enniskillen Courthouse will form part of this process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether there are plans to allow ordinary category B licensed drivers , who have passed their driving test from 1 January 1997, to tow a trailer with a maximum weight of 3.500kg, similar to that of the rest of the UK.
answer_text: Legislation introduced in Britain on 16 December 2021 removed testing requirements for towing a trailer (BE entitlement), meaning that all drivers who passed a category B (car) driving test from 1 January 1997 are now entitled to tow trailers up to 3,500kg Maximum Authorized Mass. This change was made to address the HGV driver shortage situation in England, Scotland and Wales by freeing up space for testing. At that time testing was not seen as a significant reason for HGV driver shortages here. This position has not changed. In EU Member States BE testing is still a requirement, as it is here. Road Safety continues to be of paramount importance and therefore I have no plans to introduce similar changes to those introduced in Britain.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how often her Department carries out rubbish removals along the A4 between Dungannon and Ballygawley.
answer_text: Article 7(2) of the Litter (Northern Ireland) Order 1994 requires that my Department ensures that any road which is not a relevant road (e.g. a motorway) is kept clean, so far as is practicable.The A4 route between Dungannon and Ballygawley is a relevant road under the legislation and therefore my department does not undertake any litter picking operations along this route.Under the same Order, there is a duty on councils, to ensure that any relevant road for which it is responsible (i.e. all other publicly maintained roads within their district), is kept clean.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the number of jobs supported by Invest NI that were located in Fermanagh and South Tyrone, in each of the last three years.
answer_text: Invest NI proactively publishes a full breakdown of support offered to businesses over the past 5 years on the NI Open Data online portal. The portal contains the following information which will facilitate access to the data requested:	Portal | Invest NI Total Tables 1 April 2020 and 31 March 2025	Provides a complete picture of Invest NI support through summary tables that aggregate both published and non-published data (indirect support, like advice and guidance, and offers that cannot be released due to data protection issues). This information is broken down into several different variables, including council and constituency areas.	Portal | Invest NI Financial Offers of Assistance (Detail)	Provides detail on Individual offers of direct financial assistance issued by Invest NI to businesses between 1 April 2020 and 31 March 2025. Does not include indirect support, like advice and guidance, or offers that cannot be released due to data protection issues.	Portal | Invest NI Open Data Guidance	Provides guidance on the data contained in 1 and 2 above.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the amount of money provided by his Department to rugby clubs for Capital Works Projects, in the last year.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Sport NI.I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“Two capital awards were made directly to rugby clubs, and a further award made to a multisport facility which also included rugby during 2024/25. Please see table below for details:Fiscal YearOrganisation NameFundProgrammeGrant Amount2024/25Enniskillen Rugby ClubLotteryRenewable Energy Fund£28,1382024/25Randalstown RFCLotteryRenewable Energy Fund£23,1402024/25Academy Sports Club*LotteryRenewable Energy Fund£13,649Total£64,927*Please note Academy Sports Club is a multi-sport facility including rugby with Academy RFC.“A new capital programme, the Olympic Legacy Fund, was recently launched by the Minister and is currently open for applications. The Olympic Legacy Fund aims to improve the quality of offering at club/community sports facilities, encouraging people to engage in sport and physical activity. Details on applying, including eligibility criteria is available at https://www.sportni.net/olympic-legacy-fund/ “Sport NI would welcome applications from eligible projects from the sport of rugby.”

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any actions his Department plans to take to address (i) rural isolation; (ii) lack of access to services in rural areas; and (iii) declining employment opportunities in rural areas.
answer_text: I am committed to taking actions to improve the lives of rural dwellers, but I recognise that all departments are responsible for exercising their functions both in urban areas and in rural areas. Earlier this year, I sought support from my Executive colleagues to develop a new rural policy for Northern Ireland.  In developing a new rural policy, my Department is engaging with a wide range of stakeholders including central and local government representatives as well as the voluntary and community sector to explore future opportunities for supporting rural communities and to identify how my Department can work more effectively in the future to help deliver better outcomes for rural dwellers.I intend to launch a public consultation on the new policy proposals in early 2026.  These proposals, which will require Executive approval, will detail plans to support, understand and champion rural communities. 	While a new rural policy for Northern Ireland is being developed, I remain committed to continuing to address the needs and develop the assets of rural communities.	My Department is taking forward initiatives in partnership with the Special EU Programmes Body (SEUPB) and Councils, to deliver funding of €80m to strategic projects under PEACEPLUS, in addition to £31 million of City and Growth and the Complementary Fund initiatives, all of which will positively impact our rural communities.  In addition, in July I approved a budget allocation of £6.5m for the delivery of interventions in 2025/26 through the Tackling Rural Poverty and Social Isolation (TRPSI) framework. I recently announced the opening of calls for applications for the Rural Micro Capital Grant Scheme and the Rural Business Development Grant Scheme. A call for grant aid applications from existing Social Farmers and those intending to deliver Social Farming in the near future is also open.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she will ensure that Enniskillen and Dungannon are serviced as part of any arrangements for the introduction of late-night public transport services.
answer_text: In addition to existing late-night services to Derry, Newry and the Airport, Translink plan to run late night services on key Metro routes as well as on 4 key Ulsterbus corridors that have no rail alternative.  Belfast to Dungannon is one of the key corridors along with Newcastle, Downpatrick and Crumlin.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the (i) guidelines; (ii) training, received by employment tribunal Chairs, regarding the awarding of costs for false or vexatious claims.
answer_text: The Industrial Tribunals and the Fair Employment Tribunal are independent judicial bodies.  The President and Vice President of the Tribunals, rather than the Department for the Economy, are responsible for the provision of training and guidance to employment tribunal chairs.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline any plans her Department has to review or update the costs regime in Industrial Tribunals, to align more closely with practices in England and Wales, including for example deposit orders.
answer_text: My Department consulted on the possibility of increasing the maximum amount of a costs order from £10,000 to £20,000 in 2024 when updating the Industrial Tribunals and Fair Employment Tribunal (Constitution and Rules of Procedure) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2020.While this would have brought the Regulations into line with Britain, objections were raised during the consultation process and my predecessor decided not to proceed with this aspect of the proposed changes.  My Department has no current plans to update the regulations.Industrial Tribunals have the power currently to make deposit orders and may do so when they consider it is appropriate.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) the total value of costs ordered in employment tribunal cases; and (ii) the amount actually recovered by parties' awarded costs, over the last two years.
answer_text: The Office of the Industrial Tribunals and the Fair Employment Tribunal does not currently collect information regarding the value of costs ordered in tribunal cases and it is not possible to extract this information from the office’s current case management system.   Work has commenced on upgrading the case management system to improve administration and to provide enhanced reporting capability in future.Enforcement of awards is outside the remit of the Tribunals and the Department does not hold information on whether orders have been paid or not paid.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether an evaluation will be made on staffing in the Children and Young People’s Autism Service in the Western Health and Social Care Trust, in order to manage the current waiting list in a timely manner.
answer_text: The WHSCT Children and Young People’s Autism Service ensures that highly efficient processes are in place when organising clinics and appointments. The Trust are mindful of the need to ensure that the current workforce complement is managed in a manner which optimises operational capacity. Efforts are made to recruit to any post that becomes vacant as quickly as possible and non-recurring investment is utilised to purchase additional diagnostic assessments from a private provider. The WHSCT ASD Head of Service attends regional monthly meetings with all HSC Trusts Heads of ASD Services, and department officials, who actively participate in regional discussion to explore issues associated with demand and capacity. These discussions also provide a platform to critically reflect upon the contemporary challenges arising in practice and to standardise pathways. Services are observed to be adapting to increasing complexity, with more children presenting with comorbid ASD and ADHD and more intensive assessment being required accordingly. Services are working tirelessly to offer the maximum number of comprehensive clinical assessments possible for children within the resources available to them. However, demand for these services continues to significantly outweigh commissioned capacity.On the 22 May my Department published the Children and Young People’s Emotional Health and Wellbeing Framework for public consultation. The Framework promotes a person centred and needs based care pathway that will allow for a holistic early offer of support to help with presenting needs.Health and Social Care Trusts are reviewing and refining assessment pathways to reduce clinical time per case while maintaining quality. Assessments are aligned with NICE guidelines to ensure consistency and clinical robustness. Plans are also ongoing with PHA and Child Health colleagues to develop regionally defined neurodevelopmental pathways. This will help define service parameters and will allow for an evaluation of workforce capacity requirements in all Trusts to take place.I acknowledge that the current waiting times for autism spectrum disorder (ASD) are lengthy and growing for children across Northern Ireland. This situation has arisen due to several factors, including funding that falls short of objectively assessed need and previous missed opportunities to reform health and social care delivery. I am committed to addressing this, subject to available funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether her Department has considered bringing forward possible funding support for low carbon technologies in residential properties, such as heat pumps.
answer_text: At present, financial assistance is available through the Northern Ireland Sustainable Energy Programme (NISEP) for the installation of energy efficiency measures and heat pumps, with most of the funding targeted at lower income customers.	My department consulted on future support for low carbon heating in residential properties, which closed in January this year. Officials are currently finalising the analysis of responses provided, which will be used to inform policy decisions on the design, rules, and guidance of future support for low carbon heat, including heat pumps. My department plans to publish a response to the consultation in the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will introduce a bursary scheme, similar to the NHS Wales Bursary Scheme, to ensure that students studying healthcare-related degrees will return to work in Northern Ireland after receiving their qualification.
answer_text: My officials are currently reviewing the existing funding arrangements for commissioned and non-commissioned tertiary level courses which lead to careers within the HSC. This review will help inform the development of options for a public consultation which I hope to launch later this year.Several options are being considered, including models that incorporate a return of service obligation similar to the NHS Wales Bursary Scheme. However, further details cannot be provided until the consultation process has been completed and all responses have been fully considered.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline when her Department will reopen the Energy Efficiency Capital Grant.
answer_text: A comprehensive review of its first year is currently underway, with the outcome expected to be finalised in the coming weeks.  The review will set out recommendations for proposed changes to facilitate the re-opening of the scheme, ensure its effective delivery and better support business needs. Following the review, any changes will need to go through a formal business and approvals process, therefore I am unable to confirm a definitive reopening date at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for an update on the Maggie's Call initiative between the Northern Ireland Fire and Rescue Service and the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service; and (ii) whether discussions have taken place at the National Joint Committee to address the challenges that have arisen about the future roll-out of this initiative.
answer_text: (i) The co-responding initiative, known as Maggie’s Call, remains active in Carnlough; however, has not been further rolled out following a legal challenge from the Fire Brigades Union (FBU).Both NIFRS and NIAS remain committed to progressing this co-responding scheme.(ii) As Emergency Medical Response is not within the nationally agreed role map of a firefighter the National discussion around an opportunity to broaden the role of the firefighter is through the National Joint Committee (NJC). The Chief Fire and Rescue Officer is a member of the NJC and is actively pursuing this through the agreed mechanism.  I am assured this work is being taken forward as a matter of urgency.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has had engagement with the Department for Infrastructure and Translink to consider a funding arrangement to ensure there is a direct public transport provision from Enniskillen to Altnagelvin Area Hospital, Londonderry.
answer_text: My Department is currently reviewing the Transport Strategy for Health and Social Care Services in Northern Ireland.  My officials have being engaging with officials from the Department for Infrastructure on this piece of work but not specifically on the issue of direct public transport provision from Enniskillen to Altnagelvin Area Hospital.In some instances, my Department has a statutory responsibility under Article 10 of the Health and Personal Social Services (Northern Ireland) Order 1972 to provide transport to healthcare facilities for patients with a medical need, as identified by a clinician. As a general principle, patients are expected to utilise their own transport, public transport, or schemes that exist to help people who have transport difficulties. Where this is not possible, my Department can make arrangements for transport to be provided, as outlined in the current transport strategy. These services are primarily delivered by the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service through their Patient Care Service however, there are a range of options open to Trusts, including the use of volunteer car drivers, private ambulance services and the use of taxis. For those needing support, but without an identified medical need, I am aware of a range of services provided by other departments, such as the Dial-a-Lift scheme, the Rural Community Transport Scheme, or services provided by Community Transport Associations. Additionally, those on low incomes who depend on Universal Credit may be eligible to reclaim hospital transport costs through the Hospital Travel Costs scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of children currently waiting for an autism spectrum disorder assessment.
answer_text: On the 22 May my Department published the Children and Young People’s Emotional Health and Wellbeing Framework for public consultation. The Framework promotes a person centred and needs based care pathway that will allow for a holistic early offer of support to help with presenting needs.Health and Social Care Trusts are reviewing and refining assessment pathways to reduce clinical time per case while maintaining quality. Assessments are aligned with NICE guidelines to ensure consistency and clinical robustness. Plans are ongoing with PHA and Child Health colleagues to develop regionally defined neurodevelopmental pathways. This will allow earlier intervention needs to be assessed and help define service capacity requirements.To promote a specific focus on Adult pathways, a dedicated project lead for Adult Autism was appointed in March 2025. Since recruitment, they have been working with Trusts to map current diagnostic and support pathways, and hosted a regional workshop on 2nd July to explore steps that can be taken to manage waiting lists and reduce variation within existing resources. Department officials meet monthly with HSC Trusts Heads of ASD Services. These meetings are structured separately for adult and children’s services and provide a forum where professionals can participate in regional discussion to explore demand-capacity challenges and consider opportunities for standardised pathways. Services are observed to be adapting to increasing complexity in referrals characterised by more children presenting with comorbid ASD and ADHD and requiring more intensive assessment accordingly. There has been significant increase in the number of children referred for an Autism Diagnostic Assessment and unfortunately Autism Services do not currently have the capacity to meet this need. Additional investment in autism services is required to reduce waiting lists. I acknowledge that the current waiting times for autism spectrum disorder (ASD) are lengthy and growing for children across Northern Ireland. This situation has arisen due to several factors, including funding that falls short of objectively assessed need and previous missed opportunities to reform health and social care delivery. I am committed to addressing this, subject to available funding. The table below details the number of children waiting for an ASD assessment. Number of Children waiting for Autism Diagnostic AssessmentHSC TrustChildren waiting Belfast4,539Northern5,923South Eastern2,545Southern1,681Western2,517Total17,205

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment on the current waiting times for an appointment for gynaecology patients.
answer_text: Gynaecology waiting times are too long and there is a consider programme of work underway to reduce current waiting times.As part of the elective care funding and implementation plan, £3.5m has been earmarked for capacity building for red flag and time critical gynaecology services.   In year, funding has been allocated to continue non recurrent additionality from both Trusts and the Independent Sector whilst recurrent capacity is being secured (including additional workforce).Between April and July 2025, 1968 women were assessed or treated using waiting list funding, costing £924, 951, with more activity planned.There is ongoing discussions between the Department, Trusts and the Gynae Provider Collaborative around Gynaecology Services and its medium to long term investment requirements. All parties are working to reduce red flag and time critical waiting times for patients, as well as to provide a more robust and resilient gynaecology service in the longer term. Led by clinician teams, the ongoing work will regionally standardise clinical criteria,  develop clinical pathways that stretch from primary care to tertiary centre, support clinical e-triage processes, reducing variances in performance, as well as developing the nurse practitioner role and the role of gynaecology hubs.  These actions are all consistent with the “ Getting it Right First Time” (GIRFT) recommendations ensuring patients are managed in the right place, at the right time by the right professional.I can also advise that Gynaecology Primary Care Elective have been re-established to 100% capacity as of 01 April 2025. With an additional £1,073,986 cye funding being committed of the £2.9m recurrent funding.This additional investment will enable 6,072 patients to be managed by 16 Gynaecology GPs with Enhanced Skills across 18 Host Practices.As of 31 August, there have been 1,683 patients assessed and treated through this service. It is expected this activity will incrementally increase with full activity to be delivered by 31 March 2026.

answer_text: On the 22 May my Department published the Children and Young People’s Emotional Health and Wellbeing Framework for public consultation. The Framework promotes a person centred and needs based care pathway that will allow for a holistic early offer of support to help with presenting needs.Health and Social Care Trusts are reviewing and refining assessment pathways to reduce clinical time per case while maintaining quality. question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when her Department will carry out resurfacing works on the Killyman Road, Dungannon.
answer_text: My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government.While there are many roads that would benefit from investment, due to limited budget resources, resurfacing work is taken forward through the prioritisation of those deemed most in need for intervention.Details of the resurfacing schemes planned for the 2025/26 financial year will be included in my Department’s Annual Report to Mid Ulster District Council.Officials will continue to undertake regular inspections of Killyman Road and repair any defects in accordance with my Department’s Limited Service policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department is reviewing the current system of domiciliary care packages, where care provider companies allocate care based on location rather than priority need, resulting in a differing levels of care for rural patients.
answer_text: The issue of shortage of homecare (domiciliary care) workers in rural areas is largely because of two issues:  either there are no/few people who live in rural areas that are interested in applying for work in this field or because provider organisations are not willing to travel to rural areas.  The latter can be an issue for a number of reasons such as, some providers not paying a travel allowance; some providers finding delivery of rural packages of care economically unviable, choosing instead packages of care in urban areas where the travel distance between calls is less and therefore more profitable. Also, some care workers do not have a car or indicate a preference to work only in specific areas.One of the HSC Trusts has been trialling payment of an enhanced rate for rural areas to encourage providers to pick up these calls.  The Trust involved is reporting low up-take of this initiative which is disappointing.  However, the same Trust is currently reviewing their demand and capacity across all geographical zones with a view to introducing a postcode zoning system to better match providers with care users. It is hoped that this approach will help to identify gaps in rural provision and will assist in improving the provision through designing a more sustainable model in all sectors going forward.Another Trust is hoping to enhance their team profile in a very rural part of Fermanagh where there is a high level of unmet need, to include a reablement focus to ensure people have an opportunity to reach their full potential and therefore may not need a long-term homecare packageApproximately 80% of home care provision in Northern Ireland is from the independent sector, with only a small number of companies in this sector that are ‘not for profit’ organisations.  Through recent engagement sessions with the Independent Sector providers, the Trusts and my Department we have been reviewing what can be done in both the short and the long term to improve Homecare provision regionally.  This has included discussion around: Introduction of block contracts across all Trusts to address pressures, particularly in relation to hospital delayed discharges, rural areas or postcodes with high levels of unmet needRobust implementation of measures to improve system flow, including the roll out and spread of Early Review Teams across all programmes of care in each of the Trusts.  My Department officials are currently drafting a position paper outlining the cost and return on investment of early reviews, which will inform a potential bid for additional fundingAchieving regional consistency in terms of the duration of care packages remaining protected when a service user is admitted to hospital. 14 days is the agreed preferred duration to hold a care package in these circumstances. Consideration is also being given to public messaging in regard to the general public’s expectations from Homecare services Work continues to promote Homecare as an attractive career optio

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the current staffing levels in the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service control centre.
answer_text: I would like to acknowledge the outstanding dedication of staff in the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service (NIAS) Emergency Operations Control Centre (EOC). These professionals deliver a high-quality service under extremely challenging circumstances, often supporting people at the most critical moments of their lives. The Northern Ireland EOC is internationally recognised as an Accredited Centre of Excellence, reflecting the consistently high standards of care and professionalism demonstrated by the team.My Department is working with NIAS to ensure sufficient ambulance and staffing capacity to meet Category 1 and Category 2 response time standards through the development of a 10-year workforce and financial plan.To ensure a full staff complement each night, NIAS is strengthening workforce capacity within the EOC. A structured training programme is underway, with seven new emergency medical dispatchers expected to qualify in October and a further intake of ten staff scheduled for training in November. These steps are part of a broader strategy by NIAS to enhance operational resilience, particularly in preparation for winter pressures.In addition, reciprocal arrangements with other UK ambulance services are in place to provide mutual support during periods of exceptional demand or staff shortages. These arrangements ensure that 999 calls can be answered promptly and triaged using standard protocols, helping to maintain continuity of service.My Department remains committed to ensuring safe and effective staffing levels in the Control Centre and will continue working closely with NIAS to monitor and respond to emerging needs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline the current waiting time for (i) routine; and (ii) urgent referrals for Ear, Nose and Throat surgery in the Western Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The current long waiting times at the Western Trust have been due to three consultant vacancies following resignations, retirements and unsuccessful recruitment drives in the last few years.As a consequence of recent action by the Trust, however, three new consultants have now been appointed.One consultant commenced on 1 August 2025 and the start dates for the other two ENT consultants will be later in October and November 2025.By the end of this year the Trust expects to have a full complement of 6 WTE Consultant ENT surgeons which is the Trust’s funded service level (FSL) capacity.Ahead of one consultant leaving in the coming months, the Trust also plan to advertise locally in December 2025 for the recruitment of a further ENT consultant and a locum consultant will be in post temporarily.In addition to appointing new consultants, the Trust has been part of regional work to validate referrals, devise new patient pathways for ENT services and implement new ways of working.The Western Trust has also submitted plans to provide additional waiting list activity to address outpatient, day case and inpatients waiting times.  As well as securing help from Belfast Trust consultants to enable head and neck cancer patients and complex ENT patients to be managed with consultant vacancies.The waiting times for ENT at the Western Trust and all specialties across the five Hospital HSC Trusts can be found at:https://online.hscni.net/my-waiting-times-ni/whsct/whsct-outpatient/

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline any measures implemented to reduce Ear, Nose and Throat surgery waiting times in the Western Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The current long waiting times at the Western Trust have been due to three consultant vacancies following resignations, retirements and unsuccessful recruitment drives in the last few years.As a consequence of recent action by the Trust, however, three new consultants have now been appointed.One consultant commenced on 1 August 2025 and the start dates for the other two ENT consultants will be later in October and November 2025.By the end of this year the Trust expects to have a full complement of 6 WTE Consultant ENT surgeons which is the Trust’s funded service level (FSL) capacity.Ahead of one consultant leaving in the coming months, the Trust also plan to advertise locally in December 2025 for the recruitment of a further ENT consultant and a locum consultant will be in post temporarily.In addition to appointing new consultants, the Trust has been part of regional work to validate referrals, devise new patient pathways for ENT services and implement new ways of working.The Western Trust has also submitted plans to provide additional waiting list activity to address outpatient, day case and inpatients waiting times. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline (i) why after six months the full quota of staff vacancies have not been filled at Woodlawn House care facility; and (ii) what is being done to address the issues with this recruitment process.
answer_text: Woodlawn House has been experiencing an interruption to service delivery due to challenging staffing levels as a result of Band 5 Nurse and Band 3 Senior Nursing Assistant vacancies; sickness absence; maternity leave and supporting current Health Care Assistants in undertaking the Open University Nursing Degree course.  In respect of recruiting to staffing vacancies, there have to date been three recruitment exercises regarding both Band 5 Nurse and Band 3 Health Care Assistant positions. Whilst there has been some success at recruitment, not all positions have been filled or a delay experienced in filling posts for a variety of reasons including:High withdrawal rate of applicants prior to interviewApplicants not arriving for interviewOverseas applicants and visa application processes post-interviewTemporary posts (to cover maternity leave) not attractive to applicants and difficult to fillApplicants declining posts offered following interviewAs outlined in the AQW 31205/22-27 response there are 2 applicants who are going though pre-employment checks at present; these will be progressed. This will leave the vacancy number at 8 posts still to be filled.  To address the current vacancies: Band 5 Nurse – Permanent and temporary posts are being offered to candidates on a live waiting list.  Going forth, there will be a rolling recruitment advertisement in place when the regional recruitment process is concluded. A promotional video created and shared across social media alongside mainstream recruitment advertisements. Job Descriptions have been reviewed and applications welcomed from Registered Learning Disability Nurses, Registered Mental Health Nurses and Registered General Nurses.Band 3 Senior Nursing Assistant – Advertisements for these posts are due to go out to advert again as the waiting list has been exhausted.  Woodlawn House Recruitment Open Day is planned for 16th October 2025 for Band 3 posts. Band 2 trainee posts are being introduced into Woodlawn House to recruit individuals keen to work in the care sector, but do not currently have the experience or qualifications to apply for vacant posts. These staff will be trained and once they have completed the Trainee Induction it is envisaged they will progress to vacant Band 3 Nursing Assistant posts.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any actions his Department is taking to expand the current capacity of all respite care facilities.
answer_text: My Department is leading on the reform of Adult Social Care through the Social Care Collaborative Forum. The ‘Maximising Capacity Workstream’ of the Forum will support expansion of placement provision within care home settings from a residential and nursing care perspective. This will enhance traditional models of respite/short break provision.In addition to these traditional bed-based services, my Department is also progressing the Shared Lives Model, to further enhance access to day support and short break services in Shared Lives Carer’s homes. Shared Lives is a fully regulated service which offers an alternative and highly flexible form of accommodation and care support. Shared Lives is based around Shared Lives Carers sharing their home, family and community life with another person who needs support.  The development of Shared Lives will offer additional choice for older people and adults with a learning disability and their families and carers, regarding access to day support and short breaks. Health and Social Care Trusts continue to offer Carer’s Assessments and alternatives to bed based respite/short breaks including Day Care, Direct Payments or a sitting service in the person’s own home, aligned to assessed need.  In respect of Adult Learning Disability Services, my Department launched the Learning Disability Service Model for Public Consultation on 19 August 2025. The Learning Disability Service Model sets out the Department of Health’s vision in respect of the following themes; Home, Transitions, Health & Wellbeing, Meaningful Lives & Citizenship, Families & Carers (including plans regarding respite/ short breaks), and Mental Health & Behavioural Support. It is considered that the Public Consultation will enable adults with a learning disability, their carers and families to shape service delivery regionally, across Adult Learning Disability Services. My Department’s strategic response to the increasing level of need for Children with Disability across Northern Ireland has been set out through a regional Framework for Children with Disability. This has been partly progressed through the Regional Children’s Services Strategic Reform Programme, which my Department is leading on, and which was established in response to Professor Ray Jones’ Independent Review of Children’s Social Care Services. The regional needs of Children with Disability are being considered across specific workstreams focusing on Residential Care (Workstream 3), Fostering (Workstream 4), Children with Disability (Workstream 5), and Family Support (Workstream 9). Focused work in respect of the projected residential care need for Children with Disability across the five HSC Trusts, in addition to specific work regarding enhancing the short break provision (both residential & fostering) for Children with Disability across each of the five HSC Trusts is being completed through the Regional Social Care Reform Workstreams. This work will form the basis

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any action he is taking to address the shortage of respite breaks available at Woodlawn House care facility.
answer_text: Provision of Health and Social Care Services in accordance with assessed need, is the responsibility of Health and Social Care Trusts.Officials within my Department regularly engage with the Southern Health and Social Care Trust (SHSCT), regarding actions being undertaken by the Trust; to address the shortage of respite breaks available at Woodlawn House care facility.The SHSCT report a number of actions being undertaken to increase respite/short break provision and to restore full service delivery at Woodlawn House. AQW 31206 response sets out Trust actions to address staff shortages, in terms of Band 5 Nursing and Band 3 Health Care/Nursing Assistant vacancies; impacting respite/short break provision within Woodlawn House. Currently the Trust report the following operational hours, six days and five nights at Woodlawn House Short Breaks Service and express commitment to maintaining and improving service provision, within appropriate staffing requirements. The Trust advise that staff have been in contact with those affected to apologise for reduced service provision and to consider alternative service provision options, aligned to assessed need. The Trust further advise that alternative service provision options including bed based short breaks have been offered to individuals and their families in accordance with assessed need, and where individual’s needs may be met by an alternative provider.My Department will continue to monitor and request regular updates from the Trust regarding progress towards restoring full service delivery at pace, within safe and appropriate staffing levels.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the current legal position on the use of e-scooters on public roads.
answer_text: It is currently illegal to use e-scooters on public roads and public spaces here and I have noplans to change that position. Anyone using an e-scooter illegally may be subject toenforcement action by the PSNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment on the risk of tropilaelaps mites to the honey bee population.
answer_text: Tropilaelaps is not currently known to be present in the EU or UK, however my Department, and authorities across the EU and UK remain vigilant to the potential threat of this mite, which is known to have recently established in Russia. 		The risk to Northern Ireland is still considered relatively low due to the current geographically distribution and regulated movement controls that are in place. My Department officials have advised that there have been no known imports of bees to NI from countries known to have Tropilaelaps present.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any actions his Department is taking to build capacity within the Bee Health Inspectorate to allow for more specialised and responsive disease surveillance.
answer_text: Bee inspections are currently carried out by appropriately trained inspectors from within the agriculture inspectorate, as part of their wider range of duties. The Department plans to maintain adequate numbers of agriculture inspectors to ensure that its overall legislative responsibilities and capacity to respond to disease outbreaks, including for honeybees, can continue to be fulfilled.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his Department will review the list of countries free of tropilaelaps to ensure import controls remain aligned with the most current risk data.
answer_text: Under the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024, the movement of animals into Northern Ireland, including honeybees and their Import Controls, is under the direction and control of the Defra Secretary of State.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any contingency plans being introduced by his Department to prevent the threat of tropilaelaps mites to the honey bee population.
answer_text: My Department has a contingency plan for exotic pests and diseases of honeybees for Northern Ireland in place. This plan, developed in conjunction with the Agri Food and Biosciences Institute (AFBI), is a framework for the detection, control and management of any outbreak of pest and disease that may threaten honeybee health, including Tropilaelaps. The plan is reviewed annually to ensure that it reflects any changing risk profiles.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any plans her Department has for upgrades to the A4 road between Enniskillen and Ballygawley.
answer_text: The A4 Enniskillen Southern Bypass scheme is a key priority on the A4 route which is currently in the final stages of its procurement and will provide major benefits to the strategic road network.My officials are also developing a Regional Strategic Transport Plan (RSTP), which will set out investment priorities for the strategic road network, as well as for inter urban bus routes and Park & Ride sites, for the next ten years to 2035. The Plan is currently at the long listing stage where all potential strategic transport schemes are being reviewed against the plan’s objectives. At this stage, no decisions have been made in relation to the Plan. We anticipate the draft RSTP will be ready for public consultation in Spring 2026, during which time all feedback will be welcomed on the proposals set out.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail whether there has been disruption with supplies from Nottingham Rehab Limited to Health and Social Care Trusts since they entered receivership.
answer_text: The contracts which were held by Nottingham Rehab Limited, trading as NRS, in Northern Ireland are substantially different to those held by NRS elsewhere in the UK.  We can confirm that NRS, along with other suppliers, were a provider on the framework for supply of daily living equipment (community equipment) and held the contract for the Regional Wheelchair Approved Repairer Service.  As my department had well developed contingency plans in place, identification of alternative supply sources and transfer of the wheelchair repair service happened within a short timeframe of receiving notification that NRS was experiencing financial difficulty.  This  strategic planning and timely response has meant that there has been no significant disruption to supply as a result of the issues faced by NRS.
