question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the actions she is taking to support businesses in tackling retail crime.
answer_text: Business and retail crime has a real impact on local businesses, staff, individual consumers and the wider community, and a partnership approach is central to preventing and addressing retail crime. The assessment of, and response to any criminal activity is primarily an operational matter for the Chief Constable and policy responsibility for the retail sector lies with the Department for Economy.  Additionally, businesses have a key role to play in partnership with Government, the police and local communities, in tackling crime and building safer communities for all. My Department is fully committed to working with all partners, including businesses, the community and voluntary sector, the Police Service of Northern Ireland (PSNI), and other Departments and organisations to ensure that we have a community where we all feel safe to live and work.  My Department is a member of the Business Crime Partnership (BCP).  The BCP, chaired by the PSNI, consists of representatives from the Department of Justice, Northern Ireland Policing Board, Northern Ireland Retail Consortium, Federation of Small Businesses, Retail NI, Belfast City Centre Management, Trading Standards and Policing and Community Safety Partnerships (PCSPs).  The BCP is a networking, information sharing and engagement forum, set up to share best practice and crime prevention advice to support businesses.  Additionally, my Department, alongside the Northern Ireland Policing Board provides £4.25m annual operational funding to PCSPs to identify and help address local community safety issues, including the issue of business and retail crime. PCSPs address the issue of business crime via a number of initiatives including on-street projects such as Community Safety Warden schemes, radio link and text alert schemes as well as the Business Watch scheme which provides crime prevention advice. Assault of a retail worker can be prosecuted under existing laws designed to protect any member of the public from harm. However, in light of increasing concerns about assaults on a range of workers who serve the public, including retail workers, I also intend to introduce an offence of assaulting a person engaged in providing a public service, performing a public duty, or providing services to the public.  The new offence will carry a higher maximum penalty in the magistrates’ courts than that currently available for the offence of common assault, which applies regardless of the victim’s occupation.  Alongside this new offence, I intend to make assault on such a person in more serious cases a statutory aggravating factor, meaning the courts will be required to reflect that fact in their sentencing decisions.  I intend to bring this new offence forward via my proposed Sentencing Bill which will be introduced to the Assembly by the end of this year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she will include Howard Memorial Primary School, Dungannon, in the next tranche of schools to be included in the 20mph speed limit scheme.
answer_text: Road safety is a high priority for my Department and we are committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and address the needs of all road users particularly vulnerable road user groups, including pedestrians.I was pleased to announce the next phase of part-time 20mph speed limit schemes for schools across the North. A full list of schools included in the next tranche is available on my department’s website: https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/2025-06/tranche3-programme-part-time-20mph-speed-limits-at-schools.pdf      The programme of schools to be treated this financial year was developed, based on the existing scored listing, giving priority to rural schools on roads with the national speed limit. Regrettably, Howard Memorial Primary School did not achieve as high a ranking as the other selected schools at this time but will remain on the Department's list for potential inclusion in future phases of the programme.I would also like to see what can be done to improve attitudes to speeding, not only for road safety purposes, but to make places cleaner and greener. I have therefore asked my officials to prepare a paper on the use of permanent 20mph speed limits particularly near schools, in predominately residential areas and places such as town centres where there is a higher number of those walking, wheeling and cycling. This important work is ongoing and will be subject to public consultation later this year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the A32 Cornamuck Road Scheme, between Enniskillen and Omagh.
answer_text: As part of the work associated with the Major Projects Prioritisation, my predecessor was ableto commit to the progression of the A1 Junctions Phase 2 project, followed by my announcement of the A24 Ballynahinch Bypass in March of this year.  On 20 June 2025 I was pleased to follow this up by announcing the progression of the A32 Cornamuck scheme.The upgrade to this road will provide greater connectivity to both acute hospital services andeducational facilities for local communities as well enhancing safety measures and reducingjourney times.These important infrastructure projects will be delivered sequentially to manage the fundingimpact on other departmental priorities.My Department will now commence work to review and update the necessary documents to bring the scheme to procurement.  It is anticipated that procurement could commence in Spring 2030, with the construction contract awarded in Spring 2031. This timeline will be kept under review.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of pollution incidents in Lough Erne reported to the Northern Ireland Environment Agency in the last twelve months.
answer_text: 123 water pollution incidents were reported to the Northern Ireland Environment Agency in the Upper and Lower Lough Erne Local Management Area’s between July 2024 and July 2025. Of these, 77 were confirmed and 46 unsubstantiated.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the (i) meetings; and (ii) considerations, that have taken place on the A4 Enniskillen Southern Bypass, following the High Court ruling on the A5 Western Transport Corridor scheme.
answer_text: Every project will have its own specific considerations but in common with all Departments, I am mindful at present of the impact of the judgement in the A5WTC project, which was extremely disappointing and where I have lodged an appeal.  It would not be appropriate for me to comment in detail on the judgement while the appeal is live. In the months following the A5 judgement and my subsequent appeal, I have had regular engagement with my officials through meetings and correspondence, to consider its impact on the major project portfolio which includes the A4 Enniskillen Southern Bypass scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she will include St Mary's Primary School, Fivemiletown, in the next tranche of schools to be included in the 20mph speed limit scheme.
answer_text: Road safety is a high priority for my Department and we are committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and address the needs of all road users particularly vulnerable road user groups, including pedestrians.I was pleased to announce the next phase of part-time 20mph speed limit schemes for schools across the North. A full list of schools included in the next tranche is available on my department’s website: Tranche 3 - Part-Time 20mph Speed Limit at Schools 2025/26      The programme of schools to be treated this financial year was developed, based on the existing scored listing, giving priority to rural schools on roads with the national speed limit. Regrettably, St Mary’s Primary School did not achieve as high a ranking as the other selected schools at this time but will remain on the Department's list for potential inclusion in future phases of the programme.I would also like to see what can be done to improve attitudes to speeding, not only for road safety purposes, but to make places cleaner and greener. I have therefore asked my officials to prepare a paper on the use of permanent 20mph speed limits particularly near schools, in predominately residential areas and places such as town centres where there is a higher number of those walking, wheeling and cycling. This important work is ongoing and will be subject to public consultation later this year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to provide a timescale for when funding will be made available to commission extra theatre space in the South West Acute Hospital.
answer_text: In recent months, I have announced a number of initiatives to reduce current waiting lists and build recurrent capacity to make our services more sustainable. In line with the Elective Care Framework Implementation and Funding Plan, delivering these changes will require an expansion of the current workforce and full utilisation of our existing outpatient and theatre infrastructure.As part of the £215m ringfenced allocation for Health in this year’s budget, funding will be invested to expand core capacity to reduce red flag/time critical capacity gap and  increase core routine capacity. Work is currently underway to explore the potential of appointing an additional gynaecology consultant post in the Western Health and Social Care Trust (WHSCT), with the associated operating sessions planned for the South West Acute Hospital (SWAH) site. However, the timescales for implementation will be subject to the successful recruitment of suitable staff.In addition, the regional General Surgery Network are currently engaged with the WHSCT to agree the priorities for general surgery which will may include commissioning additional theatre capacity in SWAH.In the interim period, my Department will continue to work with the Western Trust to ensure that any available theatre capacity is utilised. This will include the continuation of partnership arrangements between WHSCT and the independent sector, where patients on an orthopaedic waiting list have opportunity to have their surgery at SWAH, with the independent provider using those facilities to undertake primary hip replacements.My Department will continue to work with the WHSCT to address the needs of the local population.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the plans her Department has to upgrade the road surface at the A29 Stangmore Roundabout, Dungannon.
answer_text: My Department is currently considering a resurfacing scheme at Stangmore Roundabout, Dungannon. Scoping works will be undertaken over the next month to ascertain the level of intervention that is required and the projected scheme cost. Delivery of the scheme will depend upon a future funding allocation.Details of the planned list of resurfacing schemes for the 2025/26 financial year in the Mid Ulster council area will be included in the Department’s Annual Report to Council in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of vehicle damage claims received by her Department, broken down by constituency, in the last 12 months.
answer_text: The management information my Department holds in relation to roads related public liability claims can be found online at: https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/road-asset-maintenance-directorate-management-information-roads-related-claims-period-20202021-20242025 Please note that my Department maintains its records by Roads Section Office, which broadly align with Council areas.  In addition, my Department maintains its records by financial year.  Table 3 in the above link provides details of the number of vehicle damage claims received broken down by Roads Section Office.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she will consider providing veterans with travel discounts, similar to the Veterans Railcard in England, Scotland and Wales.
answer_text: The Veteran Railcard available in Britain is a commercial product offered by the train companies operating in Britain. Translink currently offers a range of commercial discounts to all passengers travelling on the public transport network by rail and road in the North.In addition to these discounts, under the NI Concessionary Fares Scheme, residents here in receipt of a War Disablement Pension or a Guaranteed Income Payment from the Service Personnel and Veterans Agency may apply for a War Disablement SmartPass, which allows the card holder free concessionary travel on both bus and rail services within the North.  In addition, everyone over the age of 60 is entitled to free travel on public transport services in the North, while those over the age of 65 are entitled to free travel across the island of Ireland. Therefore, there are no plans to introduce a veterans discount scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on when Cladagh Glen will be reopened to the public and the associated costs to complete the required works which will enable the site to be re-opened for safe public access.
answer_text: Departmental officials are awaiting confirmation of design proposals and associated cost estimates in relation to the required works. I am therefore unable to advise at this time as to the level of these costs and when the site will be re-opened for safe public access.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will await the Western Health and Social Care Trust’s vision plan document before proceeding with any decision-making regarding the Hospital Reconfiguration Framework.
answer_text: Following the consultation process on the Hospital Reconfiguration Framework, my officials have been analysing the consultation responses. This is nearing completion and I expect to be in a position to publish the consultation report soon.  It is important to recognise that the consultation document did not propose any specific service changes. Instead it set out high level principles to provide a strategic context for future reconfiguration decisions, including any such proposals taken forward by Trusts, and to facilitate greater collaboration to move towards an increasingly networked approach to managing our hospitals.  There is, therefore, no need to await any individual Trust vision plans before publishing the outcome of the Hospital Reconfiguration Framework consultation. I would expect any vision plan developed by a Trust to align with the objectives and commitments of my HSC Three Year Plan, published in December 2024, and my Reset Plan, published in July this year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any financial support is available for sheep farmers to remain competitive in current markets comparable to the Republic of Ireland's National Sheep Welfare Scheme.
answer_text: DAFM’s Sheep Improvement Scheme and National Sheep Welfare Scheme provide financial support to farmers to improve the quality and welfare of their flock. Given the different fiscal landscapes in which the two Departments operate it is not possible to draw comparisons or for me to provide similar levels of support.		It is my intention that work focused on sheep sector support, within the Sustainable Agriculture Programme, in conjunction with stakeholders will commence towards the end of this year, once the legislation is in place to introduce the new Farm Sustainability Payment.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any financial support available for stand-alone sheep farmers within the interim period of the policy development of the Sustainable Agriculture Programme.
answer_text: The main financial support available within the Sustainable Agriculture Programme for sheep only farmers is the Farm Sustainability Transition Payment in 2025 and the planned Farm Sustainability Payment from 2026 onwards. The Farming with Nature Transitions Scheme, which closed for applications on 04 August 2025 was available to all farmers. It is planned that this Scheme will open again in 2026. Further phases of the Farming with Nature package are also planned. A new Sustainable Farming Investment Scheme is also under development for later this year, which will offer farm businesses support for equipment and technology to improve environmental performance and business efficiency. It is proposed that the new scheme will be open to all agricultural sectors, including sheep.It is my intention that work focused on sheep sector support, within the Sustainable Agriculture Programme, and in conjunction with stakeholders will commence towards the end of this year, once the legislation is in place to introduce the new Farm Sustainability Payment.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she will publish the report setting out her Department’s revised definition of active travel spend and the reasoning for that change.
answer_text: My department has not changed what it considers to be active travel, which is defined as travelling by physically active means such as walking, wheeling, or cycling. What has changed since the introduction of the Climate Change Act is that, for the first time, there is a need to capture and report all active travel expenditure funded from overall transport budgets across my department in line with Section 22 of the Act. Details of the active travel activities and related spend will be published on the department’s website in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will commit to funding for the commissioning of two theatres, which are currently not utilised at the South West Acute Hospital.
To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the decanting process for Enniskillen Royal Grammar School from their current site to the former Devenish College site.
answer_text: Following the appointment of a contractor in July 2025, refurbishment works are currently being undertaken on the former Devenish College site at Algeo Drive. The works are expected to be completed prior to the Autumn half-term to facilitate the decant of Enniskillen Royal Grammar School from the Lough Shore site.The school aim to have staff occupy the premises during the autumn mid-term break, with pupils attending the Algeo Drive site from 3 November. Pupils will then access facilities between both sites, Cooper Crescent and Algeo Drive for the duration of the planned new build and listed refurbishment works on the Lough Shore site. To maintain Enniskillen Royal Grammar school’s full complement of sports activities, the existing playing fields at the Lough Shore site, (Eisenhower playing fields) will remain operational.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail what measures will be introduced to reduce the current red flag breast screening waiting times.
answer_text: While your question refers to ‘breast screening,’ we have interpreted this to mean breast assessment in cases of suspected breast cancer, and our response has been framed accordingly.To address longstanding inequalities in red flag waiting times, a single regional waiting list for breast cancer assessments was introduced on 8 May 2025.  This system, ensures patients are offered the earliest available appointment, regardless of home Trust.  This change has led to improvements in waiting times in some Trusts while others have experienced delay. However, it has successfully eliminated disparities in access, helping to ensure that every patient is treated fairly and consistently across the region.  I fully acknowledge that the regional approach, while promoting fairness in access does not yet meet the 14-day target for red flag breast assessment services.  Our current focus is on reducing overall waiting times so that every patient across Northern Ireland can be seen within the recommended timeframe. Achieving this requires a broader, coordinated effort to address the gap between demand and available capacity. To support this, £5 million in recurrent funding has been allocated through my Department’s Elective Care Framework (ECF) Implementation Plan.  My Department is working closely with Trusts to increase capacity.  In the short-term waits will be addressed with additional waiting list initiatives clinics and increased use of independent sector capacity. I acknowledge that the current waiting times for red flag breast assessment patients remain lengthy and the deep concern regarding this for patients across Northern Ireland.  This situation reflects a combination of funding shortfalls of objectively assessed need and missed opportunities for system reform.   However, I want to assure you that improving access to breast cancer assessment services remain a key priority for the Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the waiting times for red flag breast screening in each week since the introduction of the regional scheme.
answer_text: While your questions refer to ‘breast screening,’ we have interpreted this to mean breast assessment in cases of suspected breast cancer, and our response has been framed accordingly.  Red Flag breast assessments are equivalent to urgent breast cancer referrals.AQW 30002/22-27Waiting times for ‘Patients seen following an urgent breast cancer referral’ for each of the last two years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust prior to the introduction of the regional waiting list, are published on my Departments website:https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/articles/cancer-waiting-timesAQW 30004/22-25The most recent Accredited Official Statistics on median waiting times are available for the quarter ending June 2025.  Please refer to Table 5 in the ‘Patients seen following an urgent breast cancer referral’ section of the Cancer Waiting Times bulletin published on 2 October 2025:https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/articles/cancer-waiting-timesPlease note that Table 5 excludes Southern or Western HSC Trusts, as validated data for these Trusts were not available at the time of publication due to Encompass implementation. As the regional breast cancer service was launched on 8 May 2025, breakdowns by individual Trust are no longer applicable and will not be provided. The next quarterly publication of official statistics for quarter ending September 2025 is scheduled for 15 January 2026. I have introduced a new bespoke Waiting List Initiative for breast assessment and this has enabled significant additional capacity. 718 additional evening and weekend appointments have been made available across Trusts in October and November with further additional clinics planned in December. The waiting list has reduced from 12 weeks on 10th September to 6 weeks and 5 days on 1st December 2025. All Trusts remain focused on reducing regional breast assessment waiting times and ensuring timely access to breast assessment services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he has given the Western Health and Social Care Trust a set timescale for the completion of the vision plan he has asked it to carry out in respect of the South West Acute Hospital.
answer_text: I did not give the Trust a deadline for the completion of the plan as I would much rather this process be carried out correctly rather than be dictated by a timeframe.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail what engagement (i) he; and (ii) his officials have had with the Western Health and Social Care Trust following his intervention to pause the public consultation on the permanent change to emergency general surgery at the South West Acute Hospital.
answer_text: The pause to the public consultation on the permanent change to emergency general surgery at the South West Acute Hospital was taken by the Trust, but at my urging. Since then, my officials continue to work with Trust colleagues. I continue to have regular engagement with all Health and Social Care Trusts.The Trust’s Board will meet on 2 October 20205, to make decisions on the current paused consultation, and the work ahead on a “Vision” for SWAH.  The Trust will work closely with staff, community leaders, elected representatives, the public and my officials as they prepare the Vision.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the breast screening waiting times in each of the last two years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust, prior to the introduction of the regional waiting list for breast screening.
answer_text: While your questions refer to ‘breast screening,’ we have interpreted this to mean breast assessment in cases of suspected breast cancer, and our response has been framed accordingly.  Red Flag breast assessments are equivalent to urgent breast cancer referrals.AQW 30002/22-27Waiting times for ‘Patients seen following an urgent breast cancer referral’ for each of the last two years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust prior to the introduction of the regional waiting list, are published on my Departments website:https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/articles/cancer-waiting-timesAQW 30004/22-25The most recent Accredited Official Statistics on median waiting times are available for the quarter ending June 2025.  Please refer to Table 5 in the ‘Patients seen following an urgent breast cancer referral’ section of the Cancer Waiting Times bulletin published on 2 October 2025:https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/articles/cancer-waiting-timesPlease note that Table 5 excludes Southern or Western HSC Trusts, as validated data for these Trusts were not available at the time of publication due to Encompass implementation. As the regional breast cancer service was launched on 8 May 2025, breakdowns by individual Trust are no longer applicable and will not be provided. The next quarterly publication of official statistics for quarter ending September 2025 is scheduled for 15 January 2026. I have introduced a new bespoke Waiting List Initiative for breast assessment and this has enabled significant additional capacity. 718 additional evening and weekend appointments have been made available across Trusts in October and November with further additional clinics planned in December. The waiting list has reduced from 12 weeks on 10th September to 6 weeks and 5 days on 1st December 2025. All Trusts remain focused on reducing regional breast assessment waiting times and ensuring timely access to breast assessment services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the Review of General Surgery, published by his Department in June 2022.
answer_text: Due to increasing demands in general surgery service increasing surgical specialisation and capacity gaps within the structure, a review of General Surgery was undertaken. A new approach was needed to ensure that general surgery (unscheduled and elective) can be provided safely and sustainably across NI. Following the completion of the review, the General Surgery Network now sits under the Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) of the Department of Health.  This is an important next step for collective working and to address defined priorities through activity. A number of areas have been taken forward, most recently detailed in the updated Elective Care Framework.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any assessment that has taken place within her Department in relation to the stop-drive instruction from Citreon manufacturer, Stellantis UK, in respect of Citreon C3 and Citreon DS3 vehicles, including the impact it will have on (i) garages in Northern Ireland; (ii) vehicle owners; and (iii) potential increased volume on public transport.
answer_text: My officials are still considering the implications of the recent ‘stop-drive’ action announced by Stellantis UK in respect of Citroën C3 and Citroën DS3 vehicles.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail his engagement in relation to Emergency General Surgery Services within the Western Trust since he took up office.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail the timescale for (i) the consultation process; and (ii) his subsequent decision, in relation to the permanent change to Emergency General Surgery Services at the South West Acute Hospital.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline (i) when she will make publicly available the settlement paid to Worldwide Environmental Products, following recent court action; and (ii) where will the settlement be published.
answer_text: Detail of the negotiated settlement figure paid to Worldwide Environmental Products Inc. is included on pages 53 and 76 in the Driver & Vehicle Agency (DVA) 2024-25 Annual Report and Accounts. The DVA 2024-25 Annual Report and Accounts (ARA) was laid before the Assembly and published on my Department's website on the 4 July 2025. The ARA can be found at the following link: DVA Annual Report and Accounts 2024-25 | Department for Infrastructure.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether agricultural land that has been vested and had construction works carried out on it along the route of the A5 Western Transport Corridor, will now be classified as developmental land for any future vesting orders.
answer_text: Any determination of whether land is considered development land is primarily made by local councils through the planning system. This is done through local development plans which zones land for specific uses or suitable for future development. Landowners can also seek planning permission for specific development purpose through the formal planning process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline (i) when he was presented with the proposals to permanently cease Emergency General Surgery at the South West Acute Hospital; and (ii) when he signed off on the proposals going out to public consultation.

To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline how revenue derived from extended producer responsibility fees will be ring-fenced to support local councils to enhance their recycling infrastructure.
answer_text: In relation to the use of the EPR payments, we have been clear across the four nations that the EPR payments are being introduced in order to meet the costs associated with the efficient and effective management of packaging waste by local authorities, recognising the specific legal purposes for which these payments are being introduced. When local authority payment allocations are confirmed in July, the Scheme Administrator PackUK will write directly to local authority chief executives establishing clear accountability measures. In the event that a local authority does not spend the funds as specified, the regulations provide PackUK with powers to recalculate costs and deduct funds accordingly.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on discussions his Department has held with the Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs on the introduction of a deposit return scheme.
answer_text: Following their appointment in May 2025, UK Deposit Management Organisation (DMO) is now focused on establishing itself as the organisation with responsibility for running the Deposit Return Scheme (DRS) on behalf of industry in England, Northern Ireland and Scotland, including providing businesses with the information needed to prepare for DRS launch.My officials are in ongoing contact with representatives from the Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs, on a weekly and adhoc basis while DRS progresses. Regular engagement with stakeholders is also a priority, along with working closely with UK DMO supporting the implementation of DRS.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on discussions his Department has held with the Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs on the introduction of extended producer responsibility following the release of the confirmed baseline fees.
answer_text: Discussions between officials in the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs of the Department of Environment, Food and Rural Affairs around this issue are ongoing, with frequent meetings occurring on a four nation basis. This will continue following the release of the confirmed base fees on 27 June 2025.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the dates on which he held discussions with the Minister for Infrastructure regarding the A5 Western Transport Corridor scheme since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024; and (ii) the themes discussed during each meeting.
answer_text: On 8 May 2024, I met the then Minister for Infrastructure John O’Dowd to discuss Climate Action Plan Development. At this meeting we discussed the need for strategic road projects such as the A5 and the benefits in terms of road safety and addressing regional imbalance.I engaged with the DfI Minister via correspondence ahead of the Executive’s agreement to the decision made by the DfI Minister to proceed with part of the A5WTC scheme. On 25 November 2024, I met the DfI Minister to discuss a range of issues including the A5 Western Transport Corridor.  Tully Bog Special Area of Conservation and proposed mitigation measures were discussed, as well as the potential implications for existing farm buildings on vested land.I met Minister Kimmins on 24 June 2025 to discuss the High Court judgment. I will continue to work together with Minister Kimmins and other Executive Ministers to deliver our climate responsibilities.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his Department will assist in providing a redress scheme for landowners following the ecological damage that has taken place along the vested land of the A5 Western Transport Corridor scheme.
answer_text: I am conscious of the work that has taken place along the vested land as part of the preparatory works for the A5 Western Transport Corridor Scheme, including the cutting or removal of some natural features.I am aware that DfI officials have written to the affected landowners setting out its plans to manage the process for reinstatement of the vested lands and that DfI and LPS have met with key stakeholders to outline the proposed process for this.  I understand also that DfI, in conjunction with LPS, has had discussions with land agents on how to progress the reinstatement, use and occupation claims relating to the advance works on their land while it was in the possession of the DfI.  My officials are continuing to liaise with their DfI counterparts to ensure that, working together, we find an appropriate way forward.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline the steps he is taking to ensure that landowners are fairly compensated for land vested and works taken place, following the A5 Western Transport Corridor scheme High Court judgment.
answer_text: The judge quashed the decision and the orders on the 27 June 2025. The Department will work with all landowners.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the acreage of lands vested; and (ii) the potential cost of reinstating the lands, as a result of the A5 Western Transport Corridor scheme.
answer_text: The total acreage of lands vested was 787ha (1945 acres).The Department are in discussions with landowner agents on the extent of restoration works required. As a number of options are available to landowners it is too early in the process to establish an estimate for the potential costs associated with reinstating the lands.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the business case for the vesting of land and associated contracts, for the A5 Western Transport Corridor scheme.
answer_text: Business cases are not required for the vesting of land. This is a statutory process; Vesting Orders are the legal instruments used to compulsorily acquire land for public works projects. This process involves a draft order, a period of public consultation and ultimately the formal making of the Vesting Order, which transfers ownership of the land to the Department.Business Cases for the advance works were subsequently developed, and approval received from the Department of Finance.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail whether the £88.5 million of funding from the Irish Government, ring-fenced in the Budget 2024-25, can be used to compensate and reinstate landowners land along the route of the A5 Western Transport Corridor scheme.
answer_text: AQW 29321 22-27DATE FOR ANSWER:   Friday 11 July 2025Deborah Erskine MLA (Fermanagh and South Tyrone)To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail whether the £88.5 million of funding from the Irish Government, ring-fenced in the Budget 2024-25, can be used to compensate and reinstate landowners land along the route of the A5 Western Transport Corridor scheme.ANSWER I am carefully considering the implications of the judgement in the A5 case. The judgement is lengthy and complex and raises a number of issues that we need to deal with. At this stage, it is too early to identify the budgetary implications of the ruling, but my priority is to find the right solution that sees the A5 built as quickly as I can so that no more lives are lost.Signed:	___________________________________________________________		LIZ KIMMINS MLA		Minister for InfrastructureDate:		___________________________________________________________

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when she will publish details of a compensation scheme, including a timeframe for delivery, for vested land along the route of the A5 Western Transport Corridor scheme.
answer_text: The judge quashed the decision and the orders on the 27 June 2025. The Department will work with all landowners.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail who signed off on proceeding at risk with the enabling works of the A5 Western Transport Corridor scheme, whilst the High Court case was ongoing.
answer_text: Having considered the various options available, my predecessor, Minister O’Dowd agreed to progress with certain advance works.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the amount spent by her Department on the challenge to the A5 Western Transport Corridor High Court case.
answer_text: It is too early to establish the amount spent on the recent A5WTC legal challenge.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the amount spent by her Department, to date, on the A5 Western Transport Corridor scheme.
answer_text: The updated cumulative spend on the A5 to March 25 is £153.7million

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the next steps she will take, following the NI Water Forensic Accountant Investigation, which outlines a breach of Managing Public Money (NI) by NI water.
answer_text: My decision to appoint a forensic accountant was a necessary step, that took a measured approach, to help us work in partnership with NI Water and enable better planning going forward in these times of constrained budgets.The report sets out that any direction by the Board of NI Water to overspend without the prior agreement of the Department is technically a breach of Managing Public Money (NI). Had I not taken my decision on 27 March 2025, to allocate an additional £3m to NI Water, just four days before the end of the financial year, then NI Water would have most definitely incurred a breach of Managing Public Money (NI).As a result of the investigation, I now have a better understanding of the issues that led to this overspend and I will work with NI Water to help prevent such an occurrence in future years.I am determined that the learning from this investigation will be useful for both parties, but it is essential that NI Water takes every opportunity where it can to live within its budget.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the capital funding allocated for the A32 Cornamuck Road Scheme.
answer_text: On 20 June 2025 I was pleased to announce the progression of the A32 Cornamuck scheme.As part of the work associated with the Major Projects Prioritisation, my predecessor was able to commit to the progression of the A1 Junctions Phase 2 project, followed by my announcement of the A24 Ballynahinch Bypass in March of this year and now the A32 Cornamuck project.The upgrade to this road, at an estimated cost of £8 – 9m, will provide greater connectivity to both acute hospital services and educational facilities for local communities as well enhancing safety measures and reducing journey times.These important infrastructure projects will be delivered sequentially to manage the funding impact on other departmental priorities, with capital funding for the Cornamuck scheme not required until later financial years.It is anticipated that procurement could commence Spring 2030, with the construction contract awarded Spring 2031.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for a timeline on the progression of the A32 Cornamuck Road Scheme.
answer_text: On 20 June 2025 I was pleased to announce the progression of the A32 Cornamuck scheme.As part of the work associated with the Major Projects Prioritisation, my predecessor was able to commit to the progression of the A1 Junctions Phase 2 project, followed by my announcement of the A24 Ballynahinch Bypass in March of this year and now the A32 Cornamuck project.The upgrade to this road will provide greater connectivity to both acute hospital services and educational facilities for local communities as well enhancing safety measures and reducing journey times.These important infrastructure projects will be delivered sequentially to manage the funding impact on other departmental priorities.It is anticipated that procurement could commence Spring 2030, with the construction contract awarded Spring 2031.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline other models of funding to be explored to enable the progression of the York Street Interchange scheme.
answer_text: As part of the Major Projects Prioritisation which I announced on 19th June 2025, Placemaking work commissioned by my predecessor has been completed, and my officials are in the process of presenting this work to local elected representatives.  When complete, the report will be published and any further work in relation to the scheme will be dependent on the emerging Transport Plans and the availability of funding.The estimated cost of the York Street Interchange scheme is in the region of £120m - £160m (based on 2015 prices) however this would need to be brought up to date and would be subject to the high levels of inflation and cost increases experienced by the construction sector in recent years.The scheme is not funded through City and Growth deals and is not identified as an Executive Flagship.  Therefore, I have asked my officials to review possible alternative funding methods which could facilitate scheme progression.This work is at an early stage.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the adoption of Kesh footbridge.
answer_text: To enable adoption of the footbridge the Department requires title to the land on which the footbridge and the adjacent footway link sits. Officials are liaising with our Departmental Solicitor’s Office (DSO) and Lands & Property Services to secure the transfer of the necessary land and completion of the adoption process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the actions of the Mobile Action Plan for Northern Ireland.
answer_text: As you are aware, the Mobile Action Plan (MAP) was published on 23 June 2022. The delivery vehicle for the actions identified within the MAP is the Barrier Busting Taskforce (BBT). The DfE led BBT Communication Subgroup has held a webinar to inform elected members, published a frequently asked questions document and a guide to digital connectivity for councillors. Development of a wider communication piece around perceived public health concerns, advantages, opportunities and benefits of mobile technologies is underway. The Local Government led Planning Subgroup are organising a cross sectorial workshop in August/ September to address the outstanding planning related issues within MAP.I understand the importance of the delivery of the MAP actions and my officials will continue to provide support and information as this work progresses.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for a breakdown of the (i) number; and (ii) location of unsuccessful applications for mobile infrastructure upgrades under the Shared Rural Network programme, broken down by local council area.
answer_text: The Shared Rural Network (SRN) is a project developed by Britain’s four Mobile Network Operators (MNOs) and the British Government.My Department had no role in the design or monitoring of the SRN project. DfE’s role, along with other local Departments, was to engage with the British Government and MNOs on the SRN Working Group to receive project updates and to help address localised issues. Therefore, DfE does not hold the information you have requested.Ofcom have reported that SRN coverage targets have been met in the North. My officials will remain in contact with British government and Ofcom during the post project evaluation of the project.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for a breakdown of the (i) number; and (ii) location of successful applications for mobile infrastructure upgrades under the Shared Rural Network programme, broken down by local council area.
answer_text: The Shared Rural Network (SRN) is a project developed by Britain’s four Mobile Network Operators (MNOs) and the British Government.My Department had no role in the design or monitoring of the SRN project. DfE’s role, along with other local Departments, was to engage with the British Government and MNOs on the SRN Working Group to receive project updates and to help address localised issues. Therefore, DfE does not hold the information you have requested.Ofcom have reported that SRN coverage targets have been met in the North. My officials will remain in contact with British government and Ofcom during the post project evaluation of the project.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail a timescale for each stage of the new Enniskillen MOT test centre.
answer_text: The Driver and Vehicle Agency (DVA) is finalising the design, costs, and a planning application for a new MOT test centre on the Coa Road, Enniskillen. This stage is due to be completed by the end of September 2025. Approval to proceed with the proposals is subject to further consideration and funding availability. On approval the DVA estimates it may take up to three years to complete the procurement and construction phases of the project.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the anticipated costs for the proposed new Enniskillen MOT test centre.
answer_text: The Driver and Vehicle Agency (DVA) is finalising the design, costs, and a planning application for a new MOT test centre on the Coa Road, Enniskillen. This stage is due to be completed by the end of September 2025. Subject to confirmation once the design stage is complete, the DVA estimates the cost will be around £13-15m. Approval to proceed with the proposals is subject to further consideration and funding availability.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline any plans she has to review the size of the court estate across Northern Ireland.
answer_text: ORAL QUESTION No 7 - AQO 2219/22-27			   ANSWER The Northern Ireland Courts and Tribunals Service currently operates from an estate comprising 24 properties, including 20 freehold assets, 3 leaseholds, and one held under a PFI contract. The publication of its Estate Strategy in December 2023 was an important milestone for NICTS, as it provides a long-term framework for investment over the next decade and beyond.   The Estate Strategy isn’t predicated on reviewing the size of the estate.  Instead, NICTS is using data and evidence to assess the condition, performance and functionality of its buildings against the strategic aims of improving user experience, estate resilience, value for money and sustainability.    These assessments will in turn inform investment plans and will be developed through a rolling programme of work between now and 2030. Any proposed changes to the size of the estate following these assessments would of course be subject to public consultation.  In addition to the Estate Strategy, NICTS has also implemented enhanced digital audio and video technology across its courtrooms and hearing rooms. This provides the capability to display digital evidence, use e-bundles and operate remote and hybrid hearings within our Courts and Tribunals. While this technology was particularly helpful during the covid pandemic, the continuation of remote and hybrid hearings provides another way to access services for those who are unable to attend in person.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he has plans to introduce specialist GPs for specific health issues.
answer_text: In line with the Elective Care Framework Implementation and Funding Plan (Department of Health, May 2025), I have recently committed recurrent funding to support the continued development of General Practitioner Specialist Services in Northern Ireland. This strategic investment recognises the effectiveness of outpatient assessments delivered by GP Federations in primary care settings and aligns with the broader goal of shifting appropriate elective care activity closer to patients’ homes. By embedding specialist care within general practice, this initiative aims to alleviate pressure on secondary care, enhance patient access, and foster a more sustainable and integrated health system.To date, the GP Federation Primary Care Elective Service has the available staff to upscale their capacity in dermatology, minor surgery, and gynaecology.  I recently confirmed a 2025/26 allocation of £2.9m.There is also the potential to expand the scope of primary care elective services into other areas where there are long waiting routine patients waiting for assessment. These areas include neurology and urology. Expansion of the Primary Care Elective Service, to include a Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC) model, is also to be progressed in 2025/26 which will release capacity in hospital dermatology services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update of the progress and planned timeline for the delivery of the Deposit Return Scheme.
answer_text: UK Deposit Management Organisation (DMO) were appointed on 06 May 2025 as the operator of the Deposit Return Scheme (DRS) in England and Northern Ireland. They have also been designated, subject to Parliamentary approval, as the DRS scheme administrator in Scotland.UK DMO is an independent not-for-profit, business-led organisation appointed by the government to develop, and ultimately deliver the scheme day-to-day. Importantly the DMO role is to create the necessary infrastructure and to run the Deposit Return Scheme (DRS). This means managing producer registrations, flow of deposits, financial obligations, effective collection of drinks containers and ensuring that recycling rates improve over time.This appointment proceeds from the Deposit Scheme for Drinks Containers (England and Northern Ireland) Regulations 2025 being made on the 23 January 2025. These regulations outline the framework for implementing the DRS which is scheduled to commence in October 2027.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the final budget allocations for 2025-26 in relation to road maintenance.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail how Health and Social Care Trusts prioritise those who are on a waiting list for an autism diagnosis.
answer_text: Health and Social care Trusts follow the Integrated Elective Access Protocol (IEAP), to manage autism referrals. IEAP is a regional guidance document issued by the DHSSPS, updated in June 2020.The purpose of the Protocol is to define the roles and responsibilities of all those involved in the elective pathway, to set out how data should be recorded and reported, and to outline good practice to assist staff in the effective management of outpatient appointments, diagnostic, elective admissions and Allied Health Professional (AHP) bookings, including waiting list management. Referrals are managed in line with clinical and chronological need.IEAP allows for clinical discretion to be made as to whether to discharge or reappoint patients following non-attendance or repeated cancellations. Flexibility can be exercised by administration staff for unforeseen circumstances which result in non-attendance e.g. family bereavement.IEAP guidelines encourage the timeliness, equity and appropriateness of treatment for all patients/clients. Referrals for autism assessment are defined as routine, and therefore under IEAP are managed in chronological order by referral date.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health,  in light of the difficulty recruiting consultant psychiatrists, whether consideration has been been given for alternative staffing arrangements such as medical staff grade or nurse practitioner roles to work alongside consultant psychiatrists to ensure assessment waiting times are carried out within agreed targets in the Western Health and Social Care Trust Dementia and Older People’s Community Mental Health Team.
answer_text: The Western Health and Social Care Trust acknowledge that they have previously experienced difficulty recruiting medical staff, and that this has had a significant impact on waiting times for patients waiting for a formal diagnosis of dementia. The Western Trust’s Older People’s Mental Health team are currently working at fully funded levels of Medical staffing. The Trust have considered the use of alternative posts within their service and currently have a trainee Advanced Nurse Practitioner in post who will aim to complete their training this year. My officials meet regularly with all of the HSC Trusts to monitor performance and to agree plans to address issues relating to underperformance.  The Western Trust have reported that they are seeing an improvement in their waiting times as a result of improved staffing levels. This will continue to be reviewed through the performance monitoring process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she is planning to introduce digital driving licences similar to those which will be available in the rest of the UK as a result of the GOV.UK Wallet project.
answer_text: Whilst this matter is under consideration by my Department, there are no immediate plans to introduce digital driving licences for licence holders in the North. DVA has already introduced a digital service which enables licence holders to view their driving record online. Customers can also create a licence ‘check code’ to share their driving record with someone, such as a car hire company.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps his Department is taking to reduce combined waiting lists for endometriosis surgery in the Western Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: There is currently considerable work underway across the health and care system to try to reduce waiting times and develop pathways for gynaecology specific conditions such as endometriosis in Western Trust and across Northern Ireland.The Department has established a Regional HSC Gynaecology Services Forum, with Trusts, the Public Health Agency, and General Practitioner representation. This forum is overseeing ongoing service improvement, and the implementation and development of gynaecology service pathways at primary, intermediate, secondary and tertiary care levels. as well as overseeing the implementation of the “Getting It Right First Time” (GIRFT) Gynaecology Report. A Task and Finish group has been established which is working to develop a pathway for endometriosis, for patients that have minor, intermediate or severe diagnosis.  The development of a pathway will ensure that services are available to meet the needs of women at specific aspects of the condition’s progression.My officials are also working to establish a Regional Engagement Forum, this will allow the Department to garner expert user feedback from stakeholders.As outlined in my statement to the Assembly on 6 May 2025, £215m of the ringfenced allocation for Health in this year’s budget will be invested across three areas for waiting list activities; to expand core capacity to reduce the red flag/time critical capacity gap (£85m), increase core routine capacity (£80m) and non-recurrent funding (£50m) to start tackling the backlog of patients waiting. The package of measures which will be taken forward to tackle waiting lists include targeting long waiting patients, expansion of elective services in Primary Care, investment in Mega Clinics and a new Waiting List Reduction Reimbursement Scheme. Reducing waiting lists will not be a quick fix and it will not be resolved in a year or two. Investment at this level or higher will need to be sustained for up to five years to bring hospital waiting times down to acceptable levels. My officials will continue to explore options to strengthen pathways to improve care for women suffering from endometriosis and other gynaecology conditions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he has given consideration to introducing a regional clinical pathway for complex endometriosis surgery which requires an additional surgeon from another discipline, in order to reduce waiting times for surgery.
answer_text: There is currently considerable work underway across the health and care system to try to reduce waiting times and develop pathways for gynaecology specific conditions such as endometriosis. The Department has established a Regional Health and Social Care Gynaecology Services Forum, with Trusts, the Public Health Agency, and General Practitioner representation. This forum is overseeing ongoing service improvement, and the implementation and development of gynaecology service pathways at primary, intermediate, secondary and tertiary care levels. as well as overseeing the implementation of the “Getting It Right First Time” (GIRFT) Gynaecology Report. A Task and Finish group has been established which is working to develop a regional standardised pathway for endometriosis, for patients that have minor, intermediate or severe diagnosis.  The development of a pathway will ensure that services are available to meet the needs of women at specific aspects of the condition’s progression. Currently, for more complex surgeries for patients with severe endometriosis, other non-gynaecology consultants may be involved in the patient’s pathway and may be present at the patient’s surgery providing dual operating. These consultants will also be part of the patient’s multidisciplinary team discussion while planning the surgery. Patients with this level of complexity are managed in either Belfast or Western Trust.My Officials will continue to explore options to strengthen pathways to improve care for women suffering from endometriosis and other gynae conditions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when the next tranche of the safer routes to schools 20mph speed limit will be announced.
answer_text: Road safety is a high priority for my Department and I am committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and address the needs of all road users. A review of the recent initiative introducing part-time 20mph speed limits at schools has been undertaken and is available on my Department’s internet site https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/review-part-time-20mph-speed-limits-schools. The review recommends the provision of a third tranche of part-time 20 mph speed limits at schools, however this will be more modest in size and is dependent on the availability of funding. The report also concludes that a more targeted approach is needed to ensure that the correct safety intervention is selected and work to update our policy document is underway.  I have asked officials to prepare the programme of schools to be treated in the next financial year based on the existing scored listing, but with preference given to rural schools which are located on roads where the national speed limit applies and obviously considering the safety of introducing such measures.  Funding for progressing the next tranche of part-time 20 mph speed limits at schools is included within my budget plans for the next financial year and I intend to make an announcement on plans for the next tranche in the coming months.I would also like to see what can be done to improve attitudes to speeding, not only for road safety purposes, but to make places cleaner and greener.  I have therefore asked my officials to prepare a paper on the use of permanent 20mph speed limits particularly near schools, in predominately residential areas and places such as town centres where there is a higher number of those walking, wheeling and cycling.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 25318/22-27, whether this will involve any increase in cost to the proposed funding provided by her Department.
answer_text: As this matter is the subject of legal proceedings it would not be appropriate to comment further at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 25321/22-27, why she is not able to (i) list the engagement she had with Translink before making her decision on Irish language signage; and (ii) detail when Translink were made aware of her decision.
answer_text: As per my previous answer, I am committed to equality, inclusivity and respect and in that context to increased visibility of the Irish Language across public services and in our society. As legal proceedings have commenced it would not be appropriate to comment further.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of planning (i) approvals; and (ii) refusals on a protected route within town boundaries in Fermanagh and South Tyrone in the last five years.
answer_text: DfI Planning has responsibility to process regionally significant and called in planning applications. On a review of decided applications within the last 5 years there are no applications that fall with the specifics of your question.DfI Roads role is as a statutory consultee to the planning process and provide advice to planning authorities on such matters as road safety, traffic progression and adherence to policy.Fermanagh and Omagh and Mid Ulster District Councils should hold the planning approval and refusal information on protected routes in the Fermanagh and Southern Tyrone area as decision makers on local and major planning applications, you may wish to contact them directly to request this information.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of public transport provision in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: In 2024-25 Translink were allocated £162.9m of resource funding representing 27% of the Department’s total budget to date.  Capital funding received was £248.5m, 29% of a total budget of £858.1m.Translink are currently delivering a number of major projects that will greatly improve public transport across the island of Ireland, including the recently opened Grand Central Station.My Department monitors the performance of Translink by using a series of Key Performance Indicators that were established as part of the Public Service Agreement with Translink.The majority of standards are being met across all metrics. Key areas of note are the 100% achievement of punctuality targets set across all bus and rail services prompting discussion on increasing previously lowered targets to ensure KPI’s remain aspirational. Services provided by community transport provide a lifeline for individuals and communities, connecting people to essential services that underpin their health, well-being, and independence across both our rural and urban communities.  I recognise the important role they play for those who have limited transport opportunities and the value that users, their families and their wider support networks attach to these services.In relation to Taxis, my department increased the maximum fare for taxis in December 2023 and since the introduction of the test for taxi drivers, the Driver Vehicle Agency has made a number of changes to the theory test including lowering the overall pass mark for the theory test and providing revision guides.In working with the taxi industry, to address the range of issues it currently faces, I was also pleased to announce a phased review of taxi policy and legislation, commencing with a review of Class C Taxi Licensing.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure of her assessment of whether Translink is commercially independent of her Department.
answer_text: The relationship between my Department and Translink is detailed in the Partnership Agreement and the Public Service Agreement. Northern Ireland Transport Holding Company, trading as Translink, is referred to as a Public Non-Financial Corporation. This effectively means they are a market body with a degree of commercial independence that is governed in policy terms by my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the business case for Enniskillen Library.
answer_text: The addendum to the business case for the redevelopment of Enniskillen Library was approved in November 2024 subject to the availability of capital funding. I am pleased to confirm I have allocated capital funding of £756k in 2025/26 to progress the project.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether any other language will be removed to facilitate Irish language on ticket vending machines at Belfast Grand Central Station.
answer_text: Translink have advised that no other language would need to be removed in order to add an Irish language option, to the ticket vending machines

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of roads per division which are (i) closed; and (ii) severely damaged as a result of Storm Éowyn.
answer_text: My officials advise that one road, U5803 Carrickmore Road in Ballycastle, remains closed following Storm Eowyn.I can confirm that while some roads suffered edge/verge/footpath damage, no roads were severely damaged apart from Carrickmore Road.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any engagement she had with Translink (i) before making her decision on Irish language signage; and (ii) when she made Translink aware of her decision.
answer_text: I am committed to equality, inclusivity and respect and in that context to increased visibility of the Irish Language across public services and in our society. My recent announcement is to provide physical signs in Irish which will be in addition to the existing signage at Belfast Grand Central Station. The precise details of the signs will be determined during the design process. Consideration was given as to whether this would benefit from conducting a full Equality Impact Assessment (EQIA). However, based on the anticipated positive impact, the Department has made the decision that a full EQIA is not required.NITHC, trading as Translink, is referred to as a Public Non-Financial Corporation. This effectively means they are a market body with a degree of commercial independence that is governed in policy terms by my Department. I am also answerable to the Assembly for the overall performance and delivery of the Translink Group.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the timescales for the phases of construction for the A4 Enniskillen Southern Bypass.
answer_text: Major Road schemes are an important part of the work that is delivered by my Department as we seek to reduce journey times, increase reliability and improve road safety.Procurement for the construction of Enniskillen Southern Bypass is at an advanced stage, with tender assessments well underway.  Following completion of this, it is hoped that a contractor will be appointed to begin construction in early summer 2025.Once a contractor is appointed my officials will engage with them to determine the phasing of construction. The project is planned to take 24 months to complete.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail those private street inspections that have taken place within the target of 16 weeks for unadopted development sites in the last 12 months, broken down by DFI Roads division.
answer_text: My Department does not hold the information requested in a format that is easily interrogated and any attempt to do so would require an extensive exercise to be conducted which might only be expected to produce data with a limited degree of exactitude, and this exercise itself would very likely involve disproportionate costs.However, my department is currently considering amendments to the Private Streets Database which may allow us to report more quickly and efficiently on this activity in the future.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail why a goodwill payment will not be available for NIE Network customers who experienced power outages following Storm Éowyn.
answer_text: Following disruption and damage caused by Storm Éowyn on 24th of January, a working group involving the Department for the Economy, NIE Networks, and the Utility Regulator, was established to consider compensation payments for electricity customers most affected by power outages. The working group was established at the request of the Executive to consider options where compensation payments would be recovered through customer electricity bills. The working group has now updated the Executive of its findings that there is currently no existing route to make payments to customers. NIE Networks has applied a severe weather exemption provided for in legislation under which customers can apply for compensation if they have been off electricity supply. The working group concluded that any other options involving recovery of payments from network charges would require legal or regulatory modifications which cannot be applied retrospectively for those customers impacted by Storm Éowyn.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any measures introduced by the administrators of the Troubles Permanent Disablement Payment Scheme to reduce the long time frame applicants are experiencing until a decision is made.
answer_text: As of week commencing 10 March 2025, the Victims’ Payments Board has received 10,885 applications and made 2,696 determinations, with 1,752 of these recommended for a payment.  Almost £81million has been paid to eligible applicants since the Scheme opened for applications on 31 August 2021.  This is a novel scheme, and each application is unique and with its own complexities. For example, approximately one in three applications include multiple relevant incidents, with up to fifty relevant incidents listed in a single application. With the historical nature of many of the relevant incidents, this can make the gathering of evidence extremely challenging.Following a recent Innovation and Consultancy Services review, the Board are now taking forward work to transform and streamline processes to offer improved services to applicants to the Scheme.  This includes:the elimination of duplication in processesthe introduction of new Performance Management Frameworks and Targets to streamline processes and introduce efficiencies in how the VPB manage applications; andthe design and implementation of key IT systems for managing and reporting on applications throughout all stages of the process.The Board will also continue working with Capita to explore improvements to the disablement assessment process, and with victims’ groups to keep the Scheme under review and look for ways to improve the throughput of cases.Additionally, the Board has processes in place with a range of partners to assist in retrieving evidence on behalf of applicants, and a regional model for the gathering of secondary care evidence is working well.We recently met with the President of the Board to hear directly from him on the progress of the Scheme and the current challenges. We discussed the concerns that have been raised on the length of time it can take to process an application.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any actions in place to reduce the number of young people waiting longer than nine weeks for an initial mental health assessment.
answer_text: Child and Adolescent Mental Health Services are experiencing significant pressures as a consequence of increased demands upon services, increased complexity and acuity of people presenting to the service as well as significant workforce pressures due to recruitment and retention issues. The Mental Health Strategy (MHS) sets the future strategic direction of mental health services in Northern Ireland for the next decade. There are four specific actions relating directly to Child and Adolescent Mental Health Services (CAMHS) whilst other actions will have an impact on CAMHS. Action 32 recognises the staffing challenges across CAMHS and a comprehensive workforce review of statutory roles was completed in March 2022. This review considered; existing workforce, the need to adopt new models of working and the importance of increasing the focus on training, recruitment and retention issues. The current financial constraints within which departments are operating mean that the ability to secure additional investment in our mental health workforce is severely constrained. The report makes clear that implementation of the recommendations in the report will be subject to future budget availability, and phased implementation of those recommendations will therefore need to consider cost and deliverability implications. The Mental Health Strategy also acknowledged that CAMHS requires additional investment and Action 10 of MHS gives a commitment to increase funding to 10% of the Adult Mental Health budget. Currently CAMHS receives approx. 8% of the Adult Mental Health Budget.My Department is committed to work closely and collaboratively with Health and Social Care Trusts and other key stakeholders to develop services that meet not only current but emerging needs of this population.I will continue to work with executive colleagues to secure the necessary resources required to meet all of the Actions within the Mental Health Strategy.The HSC Support and Intervention Framework was issued in October 2024.  The new Framework sets out the Department’s approach for gaining assurance from HSC organisations in relation to the delivery of ministerial priorities and other key deliverables and the approach to support and intervention where there are matters of concern that need to be addressed.  It promotes a ‘no surprises’ approach and supports early identification of emerging issues and concerns, so that they can be addressed before they have a material impact or performance deteriorates further.The framework includes five levels of escalation that provide a model for support and intervention from Level 1 (Area of Concern Identified) to Level 5 (Intervention at HSC Board level). Belfast Trust is at Level 1 and Western Trust is at Level 2 due to their current CAMHS waiting list position.Like many HSC services, Child and Adolescent Mental Health Services are experiencing significant pressures as a consequence of increased demands upon services, increased complexity and acu

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the number of adolescents currently waiting for a mental health assessment.
answer_text: Child and Adolescent Mental Health Services are experiencing significant pressures as a consequence of increased demands upon services, increased complexity and acuity of people presenting to the service as well as significant workforce pressures due to recruitment and retention issues. The Mental Health Strategy (MHS) sets the future strategic direction of mental health services in Northern Ireland for the next decade. This review considered; existing workforce, the need to adopt new models of working and the importance of increasing the focus on training, recruitment and retention issues. The current financial constraints within which departments are operating mean that the ability to secure additional investment in our mental health workforce is severely constrained. The Mental Health Strategy also acknowledged that CAMHS requires additional investment and Action 10 of MHS gives a commitment to increase funding to 10% of the Adult Mental Health budget.
