Belfast Trust is at Level 1 and Western Trust is at Level 2 due to their current CAMHS waiting list position.Like many HSC services, Child and Adolescent Mental Health Services are experiencing significant pressures as a consequence of increased demands upon services, increased complexity and acu

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department will consider aligning its provisions with Section 17(6) of the Children Act 1989 to facilitate the provision of services, including accommodation, for children and families in need.
answer_text: Whilst the provisions of Section 17(6) of the Children Act 1989 do not apply to Northern Ireland, currently, under Article 18 of The Children (Northern Ireland) Order 1995 (the Order), a Health and Social Care Trust has a duty to provide social care for children in need, their families and others.When commenced, section 121 of the Adoption and Children Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 will amend Articles 18(6) and 25 of the Children Order to enable Trusts to provide accommodation to a disabled child who resides normally at home, outside of the looked after children system.  Amendment of Article 18(6) of the Order will allow the Department to prescribe, by way of regulations, other categories of children to whom accommodation may be provided outside of the Looked After Children system, that is, in addition to disabled children.  The power to provide accommodation is in addition to the provision of assistance in kind, or in cash.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the discussions she has had with broadband and telecoms companies, since taking up office, in relation to the restoration of services following Storm Éowyn.
answer_text: The operation of telecoms networks is a reserved matter overseen by the regulator Ofcom. However, I met with senior officials from Fibrus Networks Ltd. who are delivering Project Stratum, which is managed by my Department, on 18 February to discuss their response to Storm Éowyn. These discussions included steps they were taking to restore connections, their compensation scheme and their suggestion that telecoms networks needed to consider responses to extreme weather events on an all-island basis, which is already the case for electricity.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) any consideration he has given to expanding the categories of children eligible for short-term breaks under Article 18 of the Children Act 1989; and (ii) whether his Department will introduce legislation or regulations to prescribe the inclusion of other categories of children who would benefit from such support.
answer_text: The Children Act 1989 does not extend to Northern Ireland. Northern Ireland’s equivalent legislation is the Children (Northern Ireland) Order 1995 (the Children Order). Article 18 of the Children Order places a general duty on an HSC Trust to provide social care for children in need, their families and others. Section 17 of the Children Act 1989 is the broad equivalent of Article 18 of the Children Order, which was brought more into line with Section 17 by way of the Adoption and Children (Northern Ireland) Act 2022 (the Act).The Act received Royal Assent in April 2022. When commenced, section 121 of the Act will amend Articles 18(6) and 25 of the Children Order to enable Trusts to provide accommodation to a disabled child who normally resides at home, outside of the looked after children system. Amendment of Article 18(6) of the Children Order will also allow the Department to prescribe, by way of regulations, categories of children for whom an HSC Trust may provide accommodation outside of the Looked After Children system, that is, in addition to disabled children. To determine which (if any) other categories of children this provision might be extended to in the future, my Department will consult publicly on any proposals. Section 121 of the Act also amends Article 18 of the Children Order to remove the phrase “exceptional” in relation to cash payments, again aligning Children Order provision more closely with that of the Children Act.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for an update on the Maggie's Call initiative between the Northern Ireland Fire and Rescue Service and the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service; and (ii) whether discussions have taken place at the National Joint Committee to address the challenges that have arisen about the future roll-out of this initiative.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the total funding held by her Department of bonds in place for housing developments that are currently unadopted.
answer_text: The total value of all bonds for Private Streets on 25th February 2025 is £143,700,919.It should be noted that the vast majority of bonds take the form of an agreement or guarantee by third party surety such as the National House-Building Council (NHBC), and the monies are not held by my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the number of housing developments that have been issued with an Article 11 enforcement notice in the last ten years, broken down by local council area; and (ii) the action that has been taken in relation to these cases.
answer_text: My Department does not hold this information in a format that is easily interrogated.However, I can confirm that since 2021, 28 Article 11 enforcement actions have been initiated by DfI Roads. The majority of these sites are now adopted, with enforcement ongoing on the remining sites.I appreciate your interest in this issue.  To help inform any future enquiries my officials would be happy to demonstrate what information is available on our database.  If you wish to take this forward, please contact WesternManager.PS@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk to arrange a demonstration.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of enforcement proceedings invoked against developers who have been in breach of (i) the Private Streets (Northern Ireland) Order 1980; (ii) the Private Streets (Amendment) (Northern Ireland) Order 1992; and (iii) the Private Streets (Construction) Regulations (NI) 1994, since the enactment of the legislation.
answer_text: My Department do not hold the information on all enforcements in a format that is easily interrogated. However, we are currently seeking to make amendments to the Private Streets Database which will include the incorporation of new information on enforcements which will in turn allow us to more quickly and efficiently report on this activity.I can confirm that since 2021, my Department have issued enforcement proceeding under article 11 of the Private Streets (NI) Order 1980 on approximately 28 sites.My Department have not taken enforcement action specifically under the Private Streets (Construction) regulations, because the Private Streets (NI) Order 1980 covers construction and all other aspects of the adoption process.I appreciate your interest in this issue.  To help inform any future enquiries my officials would be happy to demonstrate what information is available on our database.  If you wish to take this forward, please contact WesternManager.PS@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk to arrange a demonstration.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of completed housing developments that remain unadopted by her Department under the Private Streets (Northern Ireland) Order 1980, broken down by local council area.
answer_text: The list detailing the currently unadopted phases within developments broken down by council area has been deposited in the Assembly Library.The developments listed have greater than 80% occupancy and are therefore deemed complete but unadopted.  It should be noted that an individual housing development may contain multiple phases.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of housing developments that have been rejected for adoption by her Department in the last 20 years, broken down by the grounds by which they were rejected.
answer_text: All development phases contained in the response to AQW AQQ 22978/22-27 have been refused for adoption.Private Streets developments may remain unadopted for many reasons.  Unfortunately, my Department do not hold this information in a readily available format. However, I can confirm that the most common reasons for not progressing to adoption includeFailure to complete the NI Water infrastructure to their adoptable standardFailure to provide development access arrangements to the required standardsFailure to gain technical approval for structuresFailure to attain geotechnical approvalsConstruction not in compliance with the Private Streets Determination (PSD) layoutInfringement into service strips on shared surface developmentsDeveloper not in control of the required landsConstruction and materials not in compliance with the Private Streets Construction Regulations.I appreciate your interest in this issue.  To help inform any future enquiries my officials would be happy to demonstrate what information is available on our database.  If you wish to take this forward, please contact WesternManager.PS@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk to arrange a demonstration.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any action being taken to ensure financial support is available for children and young people with cancer who face significant travel costs to access treatment, in light of the Young Lives vs Cancer 2022 Under-16 Cancer Patient Experience Survey stating that 71 per cent of families report that they are struggling to meet these costs.
answer_text: I recognise that the unexpected costs for patients undergoing hospital treatment can add additional financial pressure at an already difficult time. There are existing schemes in place to help support individuals and their dependants in Northern Ireland who need access to healthcare. The Help with Healthcare Costs Scheme provides individuals and their dependants, primarily on low incomes, with financial help towards the cost of dental treatment, eyesight tests, glasses or contact lens, and travel costs for health and social care treatment on referral by a doctor or dentist. The eligibility criteria are set out in legislation and certain benefits automatically passport on to the scheme.  		There are currently no plans under development for an additional support scheme at this time. In the absence of an agreed multi-year budget for health and a significant projected overspend, such a scheme would not be currently affordable and would have to be considered in the future alongside the many other competing funding demands across the Health and Social Care system.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any action being taken (i) to ensure that children, adolescents and young people under the age of 25 with cancer have access to timely, age-appropriate mental health support, in line with Action 42 of the Northern Ireland Cancer Strategy 2022-2032; and (ii) to remove barriers to those wishing to access this support.
answer_text: All children and young people under the age of 16 with a cancer diagnosis are discussed at the PsychoSocial multi-disciplinary team (MDT) meeting at, or soon after diagnosis.  The MDT team includes representation from Social Work, Clinical Psychology, Clinical Nurse Specialists (CNS), Clinicians, Occupational Therapy, Physiotherapy, Speech & Language, Play Therapy and Ward Teacher.  Patients are also discussed during times such as relapse events, when undergoing high risk procedures, if travelling for treatment, if complications or  difficulties arise, and at the end of treatment. The team will discuss patients’ needs and make referrals or signpost to relevant services for mental health support.  Each patient will have a named keyworker (CNS when available) and Social Worker, with onward referral within the broader team, when necessary.  Onward referral may also be made to external organisations in the voluntary and community sector for additional mental health support or services, when needed.Adolescents and young adults (AYA) with cancer, aged 13 to 25, are referred to the Regional AYA service. This service includes specialist AYA Clinical Nurse Specialists (CNSs), social workers, and youth coordinators.Each AYA patient is assigned a specialist AYA CNS as their key worker. The CNS conducts a Holistic Needs Assessment (HNA) and makes essential referrals to appropriate support services. Additionally, the CNS refers patients to the Regional AYA Multidisciplinary Advisory Team (MDaT) meeting for a wider comprehensive discussion of their emotional and psychosocial needs. This meeting also facilitates referrals to various psychological, social, and peer support services.The team utilises an AYA Oncology Psychosocial Care Support Tool, developed by CANTEEN, to identify and provide age-appropriate support. This tool covers domains for psychological and social needs. Within these domains, the young person and their key worker discuss any mental health concerns. If concerns are identified, they are escalated through a referral to the mental health services within the treating trust.My Department has recently developed minimum standards for AYA Cancer Services, along with a new regional networked service model. Implementation of these standards and the new service model is well underway. This initiative ensures that all adolescents and young adults with cancer will have their diagnosis confirmed, and their treatment and support planned and delivered by both a Paediatric or cancer site-specific MDT, and an Adolescent and Young Adult MDT.This approach guarantees that all patients have their needs considered by a regional expert team, helping to remove barriers for those who wish to access mental health support services.In addition to assigning an AYA CNS as a key worker and ensuring MDT discussions for every AYA person affected by cancer, a directory of cancer support services has also been developed. This directory enables all those impacted by a cance

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) any assessment made of the number of children and young people under the age of 25 with cancer who receive their treatment outside of Northern Ireland; and (ii) the support in place for the travel costs to access this treatment.
answer_text: Over the past 5 financial years my department has approved 156 referral requests for Paediatric and Young People under the age of 25 for cancer treatments outside Northern Ireland. The Extra Contractual Referral process enables local trust consultants to seek funding approval to transfer a named patient to a provider outside Northern Ireland.  This applies in cases where there is requirement for assessment or treatment, which the consultant considers necessary, but is not available in local health and social care facilities.  This may be either because the treatment needed is of a specialist nature, or because there is a clinical reason why the local service is not appropriate. The Belfast Trust Haematology department has in place a small number of service level agreements. These relate to patients aged 16-25 years of age for stem cell transplant associated treatment, directly with providers outside Northern Ireland. These are monitored and managed through the Trust Contracts Department.The Patient Travel Team provides a front facing service to book and pay for flights and, when required accommodation on behalf of the patient and approved escort(s). The Royal Sick Children’s Hospital provides this service for all Paediatric patients.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail why a bid of £8 million was submitted for NI Water for wastewater services, on top of the £3.4 million resource and £1.8 million capital bids for NI Water resulting from Storm Éowyn.
answer_text: My Department, in its response to the Finance Minister’s request for costs associated with the impact of Storm Éowyn and any other inescapable pressures, submitted a £8m pressure for NI Water, relating to the bid not met in January Monitoring from my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when he expects the pathway at Enniskillen Castle to be fixed, which was damaged due to Storm Éowyn.
answer_text: The roof of the Curved Range of Enniskillen Castle was damaged during Storm Éowyn, displacing a section of high-level guttering and a number of roof tiles.  The storm also loosened additional tiles which remain in place.  A section of the path below this area has been temporarily cordoned off to mitigate any potential hazard to the public.  My conservation works team are scheduled to move to Enniskillen Castle week commencing 24 February 2024 to address the issue and allow the path to be reopened.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will review how local sporting bodies support fully inclusive sports for disabled people.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Sport NI.I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has provided me with a response.Due to the volume of information to be provided I have arranged for the detail to be placed in the Assembly Library.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Sport NI directly.  Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the work he is undertaking to encourage and promote disability sports in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Sport NI.I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has provided me with a response.Due to the volume of information to be provided I have arranged for the detail to be placed in the Assembly Library.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Sport NI directly.  Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the rationale for the bid of £2 million submitted for Translink concessionary fares under Storm Éowyn pressures.
answer_text: My Department, in its response to the Finance Minister’s request for costs associated with the impact of Storm Éowyn and any other inescapable pressures, submitted a £2m pressure for Translink Concessionary Fares, reflective of this inescapable bid not being met in January Monitoring.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to question AQW 19546/22-27, to detail the costs associated to the Department as a result of Storm Bert.
answer_text: Due to the close proximity of Storm Bert and Storm Darragh it is not possible to separately identify the costs arising. Therefore, the total estimated costs for both storms have been provided in the table below. answer_text: Due to the close proximity of Storm Darragh and Storm Bert it is not possible to separately identify the costs arising. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the reasons for delay in the publication of the Regulation and Quality Improvement Authority report Review of the Pathways Associated with the Temporary Suspension of Emergency General Surgery at South West Acute Hospital.
answer_text: Publication took place after the standard process to allow due consideration of the report and its findings and to develop a comprehensive joint Department and Trust response.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) why Reinvestment and Reform Initiative borrowing has been ring-fenced for NI Water in the Draft Budget 2025-26; and (ii) what the funding will be used for.
answer_text: Reinvestment and Reform Initiative borrowing has been ring-fenced for NI Water in the Draft Budget 2025-26 following proposals put forward by the Finance Minister as part of the Draft Budget 2025-26 outcome, which were agreed by Executive colleagues.I have not taken any decisions on the outcome of the Draft Budget 2025-26 for my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of complaints in relation to Driver and Vehicle Agency driving instructors in the last five years, broken down by division.
answer_text: The Driver and  Vehicle Agency (DVA) produces an annual report, which details the complaints they have received in each financial year for each business area, including driver testing.  These complaints reports can be found at DVA Annual Complaints Analysis | Department for Infrastructure.	The information you have requested on complaints in relation to DVA driving examiners constitutes third party personal data within the meaning of the Data Protection Act 2018 (DPA) and is therefore not suitable for publication.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the projected spend on taxi provision for the 2025-26 budget.
answer_text: The Education Authority is unable to provide the requested projected spend on taxi provision for 2025-26 as the budget is not yet agreed. The demand for taxis for 2025-26 is not yet fully known as children are still in the process of having their needs identified.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he plans to introduce a GCSE in Sign Language into the curriculum.
answer_text: I refer the member to my previous answer to AQW 13803/22-27 and AQW 15113/22-27.  A process is in train to develop a GCSE in British Sign Language in England.  Once that qualification is available in England it can be accredited for use in Northern Ireland.

answer_text: The Consequent Written Agreement is a publicly available document, published by the Utility Regulator as part of the price control determination process. question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the work of the bovine TB Partnership Steering Group to date.
answer_text: The first meeting of the TB Partnership Steering Group took place at CAFRE’s Greenmount campus on Wednesday 29 January 2025.  The Chief Veterinary Officer (CVO) chaired the meeting and provided an overview of his recent review of bovine Tuberculosis (bTB) in Northern Ireland.  The CVO facilitated a discussion on how the group might consider his proposals and prioritise actions for inclusion in a new delivery plan.The second meeting of the Group is scheduled for Monday 17 February 2025 where further work on the development of a bTB delivery plan will take place.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether the bovine TB Partnership Steering Group will have developed and published their delivery plan by March 2025.
answer_text: The first meeting of the TB Partnership Steering Group took place at CAFRE’s Greenmount campus on Wednesday 29 January 2025.  The second meeting of the Group is scheduled for Monday 17 February 2025 where further work on the development of a bTB delivery plan will take place.   The group will firstly focus on identifying the immediate priorities for action and the steps needed to put them in place. It will then work through the remaining proposals and report to me by the end of March.It is my intention that this plan, which will draw upon the Chief Veterinary Officer’s proposals alongside actions within the 2022 bTB Eradication Strategy for Northern Ireland, will clearly set out our priorities to be taken forward in the time ahead to tackle the unacceptable levels of bTB in Northern Ireland.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of times the bovine TB Partnership Steering Group has met since its establishment.
answer_text: The first meeting of the TB Partnership Steering Group took place on Wednesday 29th January 2025.  The Group held its second meeting on Monday 17th February 2025. It is anticipated that the group will meet again within the next two weeks.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether the Sentencing Bill will include (i) reviewing; and (ii) increasing penalties for speeding.
answer_text: I received Executive approval to the proposed content of my Sentencing Bill on the 19th December 2024.The Bill does not include a review of or an increase to the maximum penalty for speeding.  My Department is not responsible for road safety matters.  Any change to the maximum penalty for speeding would be a matter for the relevant Department’s consideration.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of general anesthetic dental extractions carried out on children in each year since 2020-21, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Children, young people and adults receive dental care across a range of settings in Northern Ireland: General Dental Services (GDS), Community Dental Services (CDS), and the Hospital Dental Services (HDS). The majority of children receive all their dental care, including extractions, in the GDS. Extractions in the GDS are carried out under Local Anaesthetic (LA).The CDS in each of the Health and Social Care Trusts in Northern Ireland provide a range of dental services for children and young people with additional needs, this includes extractions under General Anaesthetic (GA).GA is only used in dentistry when other options are not feasible. All dental GAs requires justification and the majority of children on GA waiting lists have experienced infection or pain due to decayed teeth.The information requested is outlined below. While the reasons for the extractions will be detailed in the patient’s records, they are not included in the monitoring returns.Service which undertook extractionsExtractions by Trust Area over 12 month periodBelfast Northern South EasternSouthern WesternNI Total2022/231,5264,0324,9283,1462,38616,0182021/221,4431,8254,3922,3522,98113,0332020/217889603,0032,2232,0679,041Total37576,81712,8637,7217,43438,092The most recent annual CDS data is for 2022-23.There were a small number of children and young people aged 13-18 years with highly complex needs in the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust (BHSCT) for which the numbers of teeth extracted were not included in the monitoring returns.The CDS waiting lists for dental treatment under general anaesthetic were severely impacted by the COVID-19 pandemic and associated infection prevention and control restrictions. Regionally, in 2023-24, the number of children receiving dental treatment under general anaesthetic was approximately 90% of the 2019-20 figure.

answer_text: The table below outlines the number of general anaesthetic dental extraction sessions, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.The data requested is outlined below for the number of Paediatric Dental General Anaesthetic Extractions session.  The most recent complete data are for 2022-2023. The Trusts are unable to provide us with a breakdown of these patients.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of children currently on a waiting list for general anesthetic dental extractions, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of (i) permanent teeth; and (ii) deciduous teeth extracted in each of the last five years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The table below outlines the number of permanent and deciduous teeth extractions from 2018/2019 until 2022/2023 (the most recent year for which figures are available) under General Anaesthetic by Health and Social Care Trust area: Belfast Northern South EasternYear Perm Deciduous Total PermDeciduoustotalPermDeciduousTotal2022/231781348152660734254032680427849582021/221581325148324615791825631376143922020/21757137881438179602732,73030032019/201731,60417775303,68742176585,40060582018/19156146016166224309493165651605816Total740645071902148138171596528982132924227Southern WesternYear PermDeciduousTotalPermDeciduousTotal2022/2347626703146306208023862021/2234620062352390259129812020/213391,88422232591,80820672019/208135,00158144493,40538542018/196824,82755094123,6564068Total2656163881904418161354015356Source: Annual data returns from Trusts to HSCB/ SPPG.The table shows that there were significantly more teeth extracted in the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust than in any other Trust. question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, in light of work being paused at the Boyne Bridge and Durham Street, to detail (i) how this situation has arisen; and (ii) the immediate steps his Department is taking to find a solution to protect businesses and mitigate traffic congestion in Belfast.
answer_text: Several conditions attached to the original planning permission for the transport hub required written approval of certain matters before development on particular phases could commence. Although submissions were made in respect of these conditions, development proceeded before receiving that written approval. The submissions have subsequently been assessed and my department has concluded that the necessary approval can be granted that allows the development works to continue. The conditions include measures to mitigate against impacts on the road network. Alleviating congestion in Belfast as a result of vehicular traffic can of course be assisted by individuals choosing more sustainable options, for example using public transport, walking or cycling. My department has taken a number of measures to mitigate traffic congestion in Belfast through the Strategic Oversight Group.  Traffic signals at key junctions including Durham Street/Grosvenor Road and Fisherwick Place/Great Victoria Street have been reviewed and optimised, including an amendment to traffic arrangements at Glengall Street/Great Victoria Street junction. My officials have increased communications through the use of the Trafficwatch X account with a reach of 80,000 follows and additional road signage and variable messaging to promote the use of alternative travel, including park and ride.  In line with this messaging my department has also improved public transport services on key strategic routes from Ballymartin, Cairnshill and Sprucefield Park and Ride sites.The PSNI have increased targeted visibility and engagement in relation to traffic infringements in yellow boxes at key junctions identified as exacerbating traffic congestion.  My department has also taken steps to refresh a number of yellow box markings in Belfast city centre.From 18 December Class A and Class C taxis are permitted in certain bus lanes in and around the city centre on a trial basis in recognition of the important service the industry provides within our community ensuring people, including the most vulnerable in society, have door to door access to work, education, socialising and medical appointmentsMitigating the congestion issues will need a huge collective approach, with everyone considering how they can play a part to help everyone better share out the limited road space available in a smarter way.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any measures his Department will introduce following the detection of a case of Avian Influenza near Dungannon.
answer_text: I can advise that my Department is currently implementing a number of measures to ensure the poultry sector in Northern Ireland (NI) is prepared and protected against the potential of any highly pathogenic avian influenza (HPAI) incursion here.My officials have been undertaking a comprehensive communications campaign across DAERA social media channels and other external media to highlight the three key messages of reporting dead wild birds, registering kept birds and the utmost importance of biosecurity.  The key messages have been disseminated to stakeholders, local councils and other public bodies including NICS Departments.  Furthermore, detailed biosecurity guidance and a checklist has been provided on the DAERA website. In addition, as of 12.00 noon on Saturday 18 January 2025, I took the decision to introduce an Avian Influenza Prevention Zone (AIPZ) in Northern Ireland following confirmation of HPAI H5N1 in three wild birds. The AIPZ places a legal requirement on all bird keepers in Northern Ireland to follow strict biosecurity measures.  This applies if you keep pet birds, commercial flocks or just a few birds in a backyard or hobby flock.  A copy of the AIPZ declaration which details the requirements that must be adhered to can be found on the DAERA website at https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/publications/disease-control-zone-declarations My officials continue to work closely with their counterparts across the UK and the Republic of Ireland to monitor the situation to determine next steps as appropriate. The last case of avian influenza in kept birds in NI was confirmed during October 2022.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department is considering prescribing weight loss injections, as is currently available to NHS patients in England and Wales.
answer_text: My Department has a formal link with the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) under which NICE Technology Appraisals are reviewed locally for their legal and policy applicability here, and where found to be applicable, they are endorsed for implementation within Health and Social Care (HSC) organisations. In practice, this means that treatments that have been recommended by NICE for routine use in the NHS in England are also available in Northern Ireland.NICE has published TA664 (liraglutide) and TA875 (semaglutide) which advises that these treatments are recommended as an option for weight management alongside a reduced-calorie diet and increased physical activity in adults, however it can only be prescribed in secondary care by a specialist multidisciplinary tier 3 weight management service.Unfortunately, there is currently no specialist multidisciplinary tier 3 weight management service available in Northern Ireland.As you may be aware, NICE recommended the use of tirzepatide (Mounjaro®) as an option for managing overweight and obesity on 23 December 2024. My Department has reviewed the NICE guidance and has formally endorsed it as applicable in Northern Ireland. The Department recognise that full implementation of this guidance will require consideration of financial and service requirements including clinical capacity, specialist support and the development of coordinated and sustainable service models. My Department is currently considering how this treatment option could be prescribed in Northern Ireland to patients for obesity management, with rollout carefully managed in a phased manner to ensure that HSC healthcare professionals can continue to meet the full range of health needs for all their patients.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of ambulance waiting times within the Western Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Reducing ambulance handover delays at EDs remains a key priority due to the impact this has on NIAS’s capacity to respond to calls in the community, particularly Category 1 and Category 2 emergency calls.Handovers between the ambulance service and ED should take place within 15 minutes.  For the Western HSC Trust, only 5.8% of handovers were completed in under 15 minutes in Q3 2024/25.  This was below the regional performance of 6.3%.  For patients arriving at Western Trust EDs by ambulance, 8.2% of arrivals waited more than 2 hours before being transferred into the hospital.At the South West Acute Hospital (SWAH) 8% of handovers were completed in under 15 minutes and 88 patients waited more than 2 hours in Q3 2024/25.  To reduce long waits outside EDs and release ambulances more quickly, a 3-hour ambulance handover backstop was introduced in December 2022 and amended to a 2-hour backstop in February 2023. I have also asked that all HSC Trusts work in collaboration with NIAS to develop new pathways as alternatives to conveying patients to the Emergency Department.My Department provided funding to Western HSC Trust to open a Minor Injuries Unit at Altnagelvin hospital in March 2024 and the Western Trust Phone First service was established in January 2021, providing clinical triage for patients seeking urgent care services. Between April and November 2024 there were over 15,000 calls to the service, of which 35% were directed to attend ED, 41% were directed to the Minor Injuries Unit and 24% were given advice for self-care or directed to their own GP or other service.  Ambulance handover delays are monitored on a daily basis, including average delays and longest delays at each hospital.  This information is circulated widely within the HSC system including to the Trust Chief Executives.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on when details will be provided on the review of the Prioritisation of the Major Roads Programme.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when the Kesh Footbridge will be fully adopted by his Department.
answer_text: To facilitate full adoption of the footbridge a number of documents are required by my department including the Health and Safety Manual, As Built drawings and a Maintenance Manual. The collation of these records are at an advanced stage and it is anticipated that adoption will proceed in the coming months.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline the challenges to improving good relations.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the amount the Driver and Vehicle Agency has received from motorists as a result of fines or court action due to not having a valid MOT certificate.
answer_text: The Driver & Vehicle Agency does not receive any income from motorists resulting from enforcement action for failing to have a valid MOT test certificate. All court fines and income generated from fixed penalties issued in the North are paid by the Department of Justice into the UK Consolidated Fund, which is managed by HM Treasury.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) to list when gritting was carried out on the main arterial roads of (a) the A5; (b) the A4; and (c) Dungannon town between 6 January and 7 January 2025; and (ii) whether the regularity was reduced on these roads compared to any other yellow weather warning.
answer_text: All gritting operations undertaken by my Department are determined using forecast data, real time weather station readings and scouting operations undertaken by trained DfI Roads staff in accordance with Policy & Procedure Guide: RSPPG E022 – Winter Service. Using this information the Duty Controller then determines what, if any, action needs to be undertaken. Details of the salting actions for the A5, A4 and Dungannon town are provided in the table below:Date of ActionTime of Action6th January 202518:00 hrs7th January 202502:30 hrs7th January 202505:00 hrs7th January 202519:00 hrs8th January 2025*01:30 hrs*included for information as it was part of the same weather eventIn addition to the above a further salting action of Dungannon Town was actioned at 11:15 hrs on 7th January 2025. A yellow snow and ice warning is quite common over the length of the Winter Service period and does not warrant a change in the Departments response to wintery weather as the resources required are available over the entire winter season at short notice and the utilisation of that resource is at the discretion of the Duty Controller who monitors the condition of the treated network 24hrs a day. A yellow ice/snow warning serves as a useful tool to warn road users of potential hazards on the road network. Yellow weather warnings for other weather related events such as heavy rainfall or high winds will result in additional resources being put on standby or brought in through term contractors working for the Department as these are one off events with no standard dedicated resource.As road users we all need to be mindful of the road conditions and adjust the way we drive, ride or cycle and during particularly wintry conditions people should only travel if absolutely necessary.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when the Memorandum of Understanding between his Department, the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs and the Department for the Economy in relation to renewable energy projects will be signed off .
answer_text: Officials from the Department for the Economy, Department for Infrastructure (DfI) and Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (DAERA) are working closely to finalise the MOU by February 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to list the dates his Department has met with the (i) Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs; and (ii) Department for the Economy to discuss offshore wind energy since February 2024.
answer_text: My planning officials have met with DAERA and DfE to specifically discuss offshore wind energy on the following dates:6 February 20249 May 20247 August 20243 October 20245 December 2024

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 19794/22-27, to detail (i) the dates of the four consequent written agreements between his Department and the Utility Regulator since 2007; and (ii) the funding levels agreed for each.
answer_text: The publication of the Consequent Written Agreement coincides with the Utility Regulator’s Price Control Final Determination process. There have been four Price Controls since 2007 as follows:PC10: 3 February 2010; PC13: 14 December 2012;PC15: 10 December 2014; andPC21: 13 May 2021.The Consequent Written Agreement does not represent an agreed funding level as NI Water’s funding forms part of the overall budget for my Department as agreed by the Executive. The Agreement however outlines a process to be followed should the investment in water and sewerage services vary from the level recommended by the Utility Regulator as part of the Price Control Final Determination.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline the ongoing work to upgrade the custody suites within the PSNI estate.
To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether any of the herd at the College of Agriculture, Food and Rural Enterprise have been fed Bovaer.
answer_text: Milking cows, dry cows and replacements in the CAFRE Dairy Herd were fed Bovaer 3-Nitrooxypropanol (3-NOP) from September 2023 until March 2024 and again from September 2024 until 29 November 2024.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) to detail the rationale behind the areas awarded NI Water capital funding in the October Monitoring Round, as outlined in his written statement on 13 December 2024; and (ii) whether these projects are in line with PC21 priorities in order of need.
answer_text: (i) I am committed to playing my part to address the challenges of housing supply across the North.  The £19.5million of capital funding I allocated to NI Water in the October Monitoring round will be put into the delivery of projects to enable housing connections to over 2,300 new properties across five council areas in Antrim, Derry and Tyrone. These projects are currently in progress as part of NI Water’s PC21 plan, and for which delivery can be accelerated to ensure that the additional funding is fully invested within the current financial year.  (ii) The investment is in line with the established need as determined in NI Water’s PC21 investment plan and informed by the water regulatory bodies. I will continue to make the case for additional funding for essential public services to enable the upgrade and modernisation of this vital infrastructure.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will consider implementing a part-time 20mph speed limit zone outside Devenish College, Enniskillen, which is currently a 50mph road with major safety concerns.
answer_text: One of the best ways to ensure safety on our roads and fulfil our long-term goal of eliminating death and serious injury by 2050, is by changing road user behaviour.  As road users we all have a personal responsibility to behave in a way that keeps ourselves and others safe. The sad reality is that if we do not change our own attitudes, particularly to speed when we are using the roads, our death toll will continue to increase. As part of my consideration of the recently published review of the part-time 20mph speed limit at schools initiative I asked my officials to prepare me a paper on 20mph speed limits in general, which is in progress.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of (i) whether Bovaer and other additives will reduce emissions; and (ii) how much emissions will be reduced in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: There is a consensus among scientists that Bovaer and other methane suppressing feed products can reduce methane emissions from cattle. Food Standards Agency and the European Food Standards Agency considered efficacy of Bovaer during their authorisation processes and concluded that Bovaer had potential to reduce methane emissions when used as recommended. The potential of methane suppressing feed products for emissions reductions in Northern Ireland is dependent on a number of factors including the efficacy and the adoption rate of the methane suppressing feed products selected.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of times a written consequential agreement was in place between the Utility Regulator and his Department in relation to the funding needs of NI Water since 2007.
answer_text: There have been 4 consequent written agreements in place between my Department and the Utility Regulator since 2007.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) how widespread is the use of the Bovaer in Northern Ireland; and (ii) whether any analysis has been carried out by his Department into food from animals that have been fed the Bovaer additive.
answer_text: Bovaer is a commercially available feed additive and my Department does not hold information on its use.  All authorised feed additives undergo rigorous safety assessment by the Food Standards Agency (FSA) and Food Standards Scotland (FSS). This ensures products are safe and appropriate for the UK market.  Bovaer has been authorised for use in the UK for all milk producing and breeding ruminant animals by Ministers across the nations based on the FSA/FSS recommendations and supporting evidence.  All feed additives that are authorised for use within the EU and Northern Ireland, are subject to a scientific assessment by the European Food Safety Authority (EFSA) on the safety and efficacy of that additive specific to the proposed terms of use.  EFSA has concluded that use of the Bovaer additive in the feed of dairy cows poses no concern for the safety of consumers of dairy products and hence is also authorised for use in the EU for dairy and breeding cows.Further information is available on the FSA website: https://food.blog.gov.uk/2024/12/05/bovaer-cow-feed-additive-explained/
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the road capacity figures of transport in Belfast city centre for the last five years.
answer_text: My Department does not record traffic metrics by capacity but measures by traffic volume.  The figures for traffic volumes are published in the Annual Traffic and Travel Information Report. The latest published report is dated 2023 and can be found on the Department’s website - Traffic and travel information (incorporating annual traffic census and variations in traffic flow) | Department for InfrastructureMy officials are currently developing a bespoke growth forecasting tool called TEMPRO-NI. This tool will provide a standardised approach to forecasting travel demand for use in transport planning studies across the region. The level of traffic growth predicted in this forecasting will be specific to each location and is generated from the forecasted changes in planning data (population, employment), car ownership rates and trip purposes. This TEMPRO-NI will be available for use in early 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of surgeons employed by the Western Health and Social Care Trust who carry out orthopaedic surgery.
answer_text: The Western HSC Trust has funding for 15 Consultant orthopaedic surgeons and there has been one full time post vacant since November 2024, but temporary cover should be in place from February 2025.  The Trust has transformed the elective outpatient model using extended scope AHPs and ICATS staff to increase outpatient capacity. Since March 2021 there has been a 60% reduction in the number of patients waiting over 52 weeks for a new outpatient assessment.  This is despite fracture demand increasing by 18% since 2019/20. The elective inpatient and day case service has taken time to re-establish after the pandemic. As with other surgical specialities, treating time critical patients took priority and the rebuild of the orthopaedic inpatient service has only fully recovered since August 2024. All job planned sessions have run in both inpatient and day case services since August 2024 and are expected to be maintained going forward. The Trust inpatient activity however has been affected by the complexity of patients being treated and the increase in inpatient fracture surgery demand. However, progress has been made and patients waiting for inpatient surgery longer than three years has reduced by 44% since August 2022.  The Trauma & Orthopaedic team in the Western HSC Trust is fully utilising all resources to meet the rising trauma demand and prioritise elective services. Additional funding is required to address the capacity gap in both the unscheduled and planned areas of care.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the status of orthopaedic surgery in the Western Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The Western HSC Trust has funding for 15 Consultant orthopaedic surgeons and there has been one full time post vacant since November 2024, but temporary cover should be in place from February 2025.  The Trust has transformed the elective outpatient model using extended scope AHPs and ICATS staff to increase outpatient capacity. Since March 2021 there has been a 60% reduction in the number of patients waiting over 52 weeks for a new outpatient assessment.  This is despite fracture demand increasing by 18% since 2019/20. The elective inpatient and day case service has taken time to re-establish after the pandemic. As with other surgical specialities, treating time critical patients took priority and the rebuild of the orthopaedic inpatient service has only fully recovered since August 2024. All job planned sessions have run in both inpatient and day case services since August 2024 and are expected to be maintained going forward. The Trust inpatient activity however has been affected by the complexity of patients being treated and the increase in inpatient fracture surgery demand. However, progress has been made and patients waiting for inpatient surgery longer than three years has reduced by 44% since August 2022.  The Trauma & Orthopaedic team in the Western HSC Trust is fully utilising all resources to meet the rising trauma demand and prioritise elective services. Additional funding is required to address the capacity gap in both the unscheduled and planned areas of care.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) his plans; and (ii) a timeline for delivery in relation to bowel cancer screening, as announced in his Health and Social Care Three Year Plan.
answer_text: The three-year strategic plan for health and social care was published on 10 December 2024 and includes an outcome to expand the bowel screening programme, subject to additional funding, by April 2027. Currently, in Northern Ireland, men and women aged 60-74 years old, who are registered with a GP, are eligible for bowel cancer screening. The Northern Ireland Cancer Strategy (2022-32) has committed to reducing the sensitivity levels used in the Faecal Immunochemical Test (FIT) tests and lowering the age range in the NI Bowel Cancer Screening Programme, in line the UK National Screening Committee recommendation. The NI Bowel Screening Programme reduced the screening detection threshold to 120 micrograms of blood per gram of stool sample, in April 2023. It is important to note that screening is a pathway, not just a ‘test.’ As such, where a Screening Programme makes changes to its eligibility, it is important that the associated infrastructure, to include diagnostic capacity and any treatments that may be required, are aligned.Work to meet the commitments to lower the age range and further reduce the sensitivity level is ongoing but must be viewed within the context of wider financial and capacity challenges within the supporting services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he is committed to establishing a practitioner health service to address the mental health needs of the medical and dental workforce.
answer_text: My Department is currently engaging with stakeholders, including colleagues from NHS Practitioner Health, with a view to gaining an informed understanding of Practitioner Health Services and how they may potentially work in Northern Ireland.The financial position my Department is currently facing makes the introduction of new services particularly challenging at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) his plans; and (ii) a timeline for delivery in relation to lung cancer screening, as announced in his Health and Social Care Three Year Plan.
answer_text: The former Minister of Health, Minister Swann, accepted the recommendation by the UK National Screening Committee (UK NSC) to introduce a targeted lung cancer screening programme for asymptomatic people with a history of smoking, aged between 55-74 years old, in tandem with the enhanced integration of smoking cessation services.We are at the early stages of scoping out what the requirements of this new programme for Northern Ireland will be.  We will be working with colleagues across Northern Ireland and the rest of the UK to establish the resources required and the likely timeline. This will be a very significant service development encompassing new screening services and a range of additional diagnostic and treatment services. This work will require input and collaboration from multiple partners.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the work his Department is undertaking to introduce a practitioner health service for doctors and dentists.
answer_text: My Department is currently engaging with stakeholders, including colleagues from NHS Practitioner Health, with a view to gaining an informed understanding of Practitioner Health Services and how they may potentially work in Northern Ireland.The financial position my Department is currently facing makes the introduction of new services particularly challenging at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he has considered involving (i) Retail NI; (ii) Hospitality Ulster; and (iii) the Belfast City Centre Night Czar in any planning or steering groups in relation to traffic issues in Belfast.
answer_text: I have previously established a Strategic Oversight Group, comprising Departmental officials, Belfast City Council, PSNI and Translink to critically examine what more can be done to ease congestion that is occurring at peak times.I have also held a number of meetings with a range of stakeholders to discuss the city’s traffic challenges and solutions.  Most recently, on 16 December 2024, I had round table discussions with stakeholders from: Belfast City Council; Translink; PSNI; Belfast Chamber of Commerce; Belfast Business Improvement Districts; Retail NI; Hospitality Ulster; and Belfast City Centre First Night Czar  to strengthen engagement; to improve communication and to drive collective action.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the cost of the clear up to damage caused by Storm Darragh.
answer_text: The Department is currently undertaking an assessment of the estimated cost of clear up after Storm Darragh and Storm Bert which required a significant response and recovery efforts, over a wide geographical area including a multi- agency response.  Consequently cost estimates are not available at present.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of groups he has engaged with in relation to traffic congestion in Belfast since taking office.
answer_text: The ongoing work in and around Belfast represents a significant investment, which will ultimately improve and enhance the cityscape, however I fully appreciate that this work is causing delay and frustration for motorists travelling through or to the city centre.I can advise that my Department has engaged with a range of groups regarding the ongoing works in Belfast city centre and the surrounding area.These include but are not limited to Belfast City Council, PSNI, Translink, BID companies, Chamber of Trade, Retail NI, NI Water, Pheonix Energy, outside consultants, Taxis Associations and effected businesses.Mitigating the congestion issues will need a huge collective approach, with everyone considering how they can play a part to help everyone better share out the limited road space available in a smarter way.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the cost of the clear up from Storm Bert incurred by his Department.
answer_text: The Department is currently undertaking an assessment of the estimated cost of clear up after Storm Darragh and Storm Bert which required a significant response and recovery efforts, over a wide geographical area including a multi- agency response.  Consequently, cost estimates are not available at present.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the ambulance cover at weekends in the Fermanagh and South Tyrone constituency in the last 12 months.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he has reconsidered his position on plans to allow drivers, who passed their driving test from 1 January 1997, to tow a trailer with a maximum weight of 3,500kg without undergoing a separate test, similar to the rest of the United Kingdom.
answer_text: The position has not changed since my original response on this matter, AQW 7567/22-27. Road Safety continues to be of paramount importance and therefore I have no plans to introduce similar changes to those introduced in Britain in 2021.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on AQW 14387/22-27.
answer_text: An updated response for AQW 14387-22-27 issued on 9 December 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on work carried out by his officials to commission a research exercise into future service provision for childhood and adult attention deficit hyperactivity disorder.
answer_text: My Department is in the process of commissioning a research exercise with a view to bringing forward recommendations to inform a commissioned ADHD service, subject to future budget availability. The Terms of Reference for an ADHD research exercise have now been finalised and are with the Leadership Centre to identify a suitable individual or individuals to take it forward. It is hoped, therefore, that researchers with the requisite skills, knowledge and experience will be appointed shortly. This research, which would cover both child and adult ADHD, will aim to determine the level of demand for a commissioned service, as well as recommendations for what such a service might look like, including potential implementation and staffing costs.In line with the usual process for commissioning of services, any decision to commission adult ADHD services in the future will be based on an assessment of the level of demand for services, and in the context of future budget availability.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) all Active Travel Schemes carried out in the Fermanagh and South Tyrone constituency in 2024; and (ii) the amount spent on those schemes.
answer_text: Monitoring of Active Travel project spend within my department is not broken down by parliamentary constituency. The recorded spend on active travel projects for the 2023-2024 financial year within the Western Division area, which includes Fermanagh and South Tyrone, was £1m. A breakdown of this spend is below:Western Division schemes 2023/24  £kDublin Road, Omagh (Phase 2) - Shared Use Cycleway100Glencam Road, Omagh - Shared Use Path 100Cornagrade Road, Enniskillen Footway20Granville Cycleway50Clabby Road, Fivemiletown Footway170Drumenny Road, Ballinderry Footway30Draperstown Pedestrian Crossing20A505 Killyclogher Road180Beragh Footways35Cross Street, Lisnaskea Footway Extension15Loughmacrory Footway40Railway Street, Strabane Toucan Crossing45Clooney Road, Derry Shared Footway50Foreglen Road, Claudy Shared Footway100Ballynagarde Road, Culmore Footway45Total1,000The direct active travel project spend in Western Division from 1 April 2024 to 30 September 2024 is approximately £0.8m. This figure is mid-year and therefore unaudited and subject to change. Details of active travel schemes in delivery in the Fermanagh and Omagh Council area and Mid Ulster Council area are available in the local council reports: https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/articles/list-council-reports-202324.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of pensioner households that are expected to receive the £100 fuel payment in the Fermanagh and South Tyrone constituency.
answer_text: The member is referred to the response to AQW 19245/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the schools in the Fermanagh and South Tyrone constituency that will benefit from his announcement on funding for (i) outdoor play equipment; and (ii) new desktop computers.
answer_text: Every grant-aided pre-school, nursery and primary school in Fermanagh and South Tyrone and across Northern Ireland will receive funding for outdoor play equipment.  The level of funding received will be dependent on pupil numbers and details of the scheme will be provided to schools and settings over coming weeks.All post primary schools in the Fermanagh and South Tyrone constituency will receive an allocation of desktop computers to replace those which have reached their end of life to ensure that students have access to secure, modern technology.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for an update on the work his Department is undertaking to scope out introducing a practitioner health service for doctors and dentists; and (ii) whether he is committed to establishing such a service to address Northern Ireland’s outlier status, as being important to support the mental health needs of the local medical and dental workforce.
answer_text: My Department is currently engaging with stakeholders, including colleagues from NHS Practitioner Health, with a view to gaining an informed understanding of Practitioner Health Services and how they may potentially work in Northern Ireland.The financial position my Department is currently facing makes the introduction of new services particularly challenging at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the amount his Department has spent on bovine TB compensation in the Fermanagh and South Tyrone constituency in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Information relating to the Northern Ireland bovine Tuberculosis (bTB) Programme is held according to the areas covered by each of the Divisional Veterinary Offices, not be constituency.  The Fermanagh and South Tyrone constituency comprises the Enniskillen office area along with parts of the areas covered by Dungannon and Armagh offices.  The compensation paid to herd owners for cattle slaughtered for bTB disease control in each of these three office areas in the last five financial years is set out in the table below: 2019-20Enniskillen£2,384,425.00 Armagh£2,205,305.00 Dungannon£2,056,385.002020-21Enniskillen£2,702,905.00 Armagh£1,895,310.00 Dungannon£2,165,560.002021-22Enniskillen£3,486,035.00 Armagh£2,356,305.00 Dungannon£2,919,185.002022-23Enniskillen£6,312,380.00 Armagh£3,194,745.00 Dungannon£2,913,810.002023-24Enniskillen£5,153,530.00 Armagh£4,565,734.78 Dungannon£3,697,605.00
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 12688/22-27, whether he has received further evidence from his officials about the possibility of introducing a bursary scheme, similar to the NHS Wales Bursary Scheme, to ensure that students studying healthcare-related degrees will return to work in Northern Ireland after receiving their qualification.
answer_text: The introduction of a scheme to ensure that students on pre-registration healthcare programmes commissioned by my Department take up a post in Northern Ireland upon completion of their studies, is being considered as part of the work currently being taken forward by my officials. I anticipate that evidence from the NHS Wales Bursary scheme will be considered as part of this work.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department has any emergency funding avenues available that DfI Roads Division can draw upon to carry out emergency road repairs in the case of severe roads subsidence.
answer_text: There are no emergency funding streams available to my Department other than through the in-year monitoring rounds whereby departments can bid for additional funding. The funding secured by my Department in the most recent October monitoring round allowed me to allocate almost £19m for essential and structural road maintenance and street lighting work.My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government. My Department’s annual programme of resurfacing and reconstruction works is dictated by the availability of funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether there are plans to update the current childminder ratios in Northern Ireland to reflect the revised childminder ratios in England.
answer_text: As part of the forthcoming review of the Minimum Standards for Childminding and Day Care I will be seeking views on adult:child ratios through a public consultation exercise early in 2025.
