question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for a breakdown of roads maintenance projects that will take place in the Southern Division as a result of the October monitoring Round.
answer_text: The additional funding allocated to my Department through the October Monitoring Round includes almost £19m towards essential and structural maintenance of our roads and street lighting work to progress existing and new schemes.Work has started on the planning and delivery of new schemes for delivery this financial year. However, the development of the programmes is on-going so full breakdown of these is not yet available. In addition, I have also funded a £1m targeted intervention for gully emptying given the potential impact of severe weather and resultant risk of flooding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when he will launch the consultation for the provision of taxis in bus lanes pilot scheme, as per his announcement on 11 October 2024.
answer_text: Officials are working on the development of my proposal and I will announce the commencement of the consultation on the legislation to enable the pilot in the near future.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for a breakdown of roads maintenance projects that will take place in the Western Division as a result of the October Monitoring Round.
answer_text: The additional funding allocated to my Department through the October Monitoring Round includes almost £19m towards essential and structural maintenance of our roads and street lighting work to progress existing and new schemes.Work has started on the planning and delivery of new schemes for delivery this financial year. However, the development of the programmes is on-going so full breakdown of these is not yet available.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for a breakdown of the £1 million for gully maintenance announced as part of the October Monitoring Round, broken down by Division.
answer_text: The additional funding secured by my Department through the October Monitoring Round has enabled me to fund a £1m targeted intervention for gully cleaning and drainage maintenance by means of cleaning and desilting of lines and manholes.Work has started on the planning and delivery of this work for delivery this financial year. Initially, £200k has been allocated to each Division however, the development of the programme is on-going and so the remaining £200k is being held to fund high priority sites as they are identified.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of developments that are unable to be connected to NI Water and are currently being held within the planning system.
answer_text: My Department’s Planning Group is unable to advise on the number of developments currently held within the planning system as it does not possess or collate the figures requested. It would be for each council to provide the figures for their own respective area.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether the NIEA have any powers in creating policy in conjunction with NI Water in relation to NI Water’s wastewater modelling, capacity policies or mapping.
answer_text: I would advise that the Northern Ireland Environment Agency (NIEA) does not have any powers to create policy in conjunction with NI Water.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) given the budget allocated, to detail the number of developments that will be connected to NI Water under the Price Control 2021 to 2027 in the 2024-25 financial year; and (ii) how this number compares with a fully funded price control.
answer_text: This is an operational matter for NI Water however I have sought the information as requested:(i) NI Water estimates it will connect 5,520 new properties in 2024-25.(ii) The Price Control does not quantify the number of properties to be connected to the NI Water’s network each year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the establishment of a taskforce to consider area planning and development proposals.
answer_text: I outlined in the annex to my statement to the Assembly on 21 October 2024, my intention to establish a Taskforce to develop a new approach to Area Planning.  As stated, revised arrangements are needed to reconfigure the current network of schools to create thriving, sustainable schools that support higher standards of education and promote inclusion for all children and young people.Further details in relation to the way forward for the Taskforce will be set out in my Strategy for Education which will be published in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 11234/22-27, to detail any further engagement his officials have had with colleagues in other jurisdictions to evaluate a number of pilot schemes being progressed in relation to cross pavement electric vehicle charging solutions.
answer_text: My officials have been engaging with a number of Local Authorities in England in relation to how they have been progressing cross pavement electric vehicle charging solutions. Discussions are also continuing with other key stakeholders and the Office of Zero Emission Vehicles which provides advice, guidance and grants for the installation of such solutions.This work has focused on two solutions which are widely used; an engineered solution which requires the opening of the footway and cable protectors which are already widely used to prevent tripping hazards and are designed not to obstruct the footway for users including those with disabilities. This work is now at an advanced stage and my officials will be consulting locally with the Inclusive Mobility Transport Advisory Committee on this issue given the potential impact of any pavement solutions which may affect the mobility of disabled and older people.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of convictions relating to livestock worrying as per the Dogs (Northern Ireland) Order 1983 in the last ten years, broken down by local council area.
answer_text: My Department is not responsible for prosecuting incidences of livestock worrying, so does not hold the data sought by the Member.  It is the statutory responsibility of local councils to investigate all breaches of the Dogs (Northern Ireland) Order 1983 and the Member may wish to contact local councils directly for any statistical or other detail that each may hold.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance what engagement she has had with the Financial Conduct Authority regarding access to cash and banking hubs in Northern Ireland, since new access to cash rules came into force on 18 September 2024.
To ask the Minister for Communities whether he plans to develop equal levels of funding through Sport NI for grassroots sports across all sporting organisations.
answer_text: In line with the vision of the Northern Ireland Strategy for Sport and Physical Activity to get more people, more active, more of the time, it is important that we continue to encourage greater participation across all grassroots sports.  Any funding disbursed through Sport NI on behalf of my Department is subject to an approved business case and an equality impact assessment to ensure fairness of opportunity.  Funding programmes will have relevant criteria set out, to which applicants must demonstrate how they meet the requirements of the programme.I believe that provision of funding must be needs-based to create equality of opportunity so that people can participate and excel in the sport of their choice.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the street lights that have been removed in the last five years, broken down by DfI Division.
answer_text: As part of its ongoing capital replacement programme to replace street lights that have reached the end of their lives, my Department removes and replaces many street lights each year, depending on the available funding.In considering those lighting installations which are deemed to have reached the end of their serviceable life and can no longer be maintained, a survey is carried out of the location and lighting is replaced only in those adopted areas which meet my Department’s policy for the provision of lighting.This policy helps ensure that the limited resources available for the provision of street lighting are directed at those areas where they can provide the maximum benefit to the public, particularly where road or infrastructure safety is concerned. It also serves to balance the demand for more rural lighting against the unwelcome effects of increased urbanisation of the countryside and the environmental impact of night sky light pollution on people, wildlife and fauna.This can result in the removal and non-replacement of lights in areas such as back pathways, unadopted roads and footways, or rural areas with insufficient density of housing.Whilst my keeps detailed records of the number of street lights across the network, details of lights which have been removed and not replaced are not recorded separately and would not be easily attained.  To attempt to provide details of these removals would require an extensive exercise to be conducted across the department which might only be expected to produce data with a limited degree of exactitude, and this exercise itself would very likely involve disproportionate costs.Whilst details of the street lights that have been removed in the last five years, broken down by DfI Division are not available, the table below presents details of the total number of street lights on the network in the last five years, broken down by DfI Division.  It is highlighted that these figures include the street lights that have been added on to the network as new private streets developments are completed and adopted.  In general, the number of street lights added to the network exceeds the number being removed, so the total number of lights increases each year.Total Number of Street Lights per DfI DivisionYear01/04/202001/04/202101/04/202201/04/202301/04/2024Eastern67,13367,17967,40167,61267,901Northern72,57172,48372,66473,89473,695Southern83,19083,22083,35384,30484,290Western65,70265,89866,27566,98967,124Totals288,596288,780289,693292,799293,010

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the bus lanes in Belfast city centre that (i) Class A; and (ii) Class C taxis are permitted to use whilst the road works associated with Belfast Grand Central Station are taking place.
answer_text: In order to alleviate some of the disruption as a result of the construction of the Belfast Grand Central Station, and to support the taxi industry, I have asked my officials to urgently bring forward proposals which will permit Class A and Class C taxis to use certain bus lanes in the vicinity of the works.My Department is currently working on the legislation, and I will announce further details on the commencement of the pilot in the near future.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for a breakdown of the funding that will be spent on the roll-out of bilingual traffic signs in West Belfast.
answer_text: On 10 October 2024, I announced bi-lingual traffic sign pilot for the Gaeltacht Quarter in West Belfast, which will install bi-lingual traffic signs and road markings on the Falls Road between the Springfield Road and Whiterock Road.  The current estimate for this work is £50,000.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, following his assessment of the 17 recommendations in the UK Blood Cancer Action Plan, to detail any action his Department will take to improve and enhance blood cancer services.
answer_text: Whilst my Department acknowledges the helpful 17 recommendations outlined within the UK Blood Cancer Action Plan, it should be stated that it was not consulted or involved in the development of this Action Plan. However, the recommendations around the themes of workforce, diagnosis and care, access to clinical trials and data, are all areas we acknowledge require work and we have been working to address these in Northern Ireland.Haematology services in NI have been under growing pressures for some time amidst serious workforce concerns for haematological services UK wide.  In order to address the severe regional pressures within haematology services, in 2023 the DoH NI Cancer Policy and Projects Team initiated a project for the redesign of Haematology Treatment Pathways across NI to include the three main haematological pathways: Myeloma, Lymphoma and Leukaemia. The Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) of my Department were asked to implement the recommended actions in April 2024 and have engaged with Trusts on the reports’ recommendations.  While some recommendations are cost neutral, others require investment, and resource to implement.  Some progress has been made on early stage actions including baseline assessment and identification of nursing workforce gaps, maximising virtual clinics, identifying appropriate time and process for holistic needs assessment, weekly multi-disciplinary Team (MDT) meetings, adoption of NICE guidance on bispecific antibodies in Multiple Myeloma and development of a haematology cancer nurse career pathway. Progression of some of the actions will be dependent on consultant capacity and ongoing recruitment. Many actions across all 3 reports are dependent upon reform and greater skill mix within haematology service including haematology specialist and advanced nursing roles.  These roles are key to releasing consultant capacity and provide alternative clinical options for managing patients via nurse led clinics and follow up, releasing limited consultant / specialist doctor resource for assessment and treatment of more complex patients.  Investment in the nursing workforce (action 39 of the NI Cancer Strategy) and Advanced Nurse Practitioners (ANPs) in particular will help release the resource required to take forward a significant number of the recommended actions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline how the justice elements of the Programme for Government 2024-2027 will be funded.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of watercourses that have met the criteria for the designation of watercourses to be maintained by his Department in the last (i) 12 months; and (ii) five years.
answer_text: The Drainage Council determines which watercourses and sea defences should be maintained by my Department at public expense.  Watercourses designated by the Drainage Council are recorded in the minutes of the Council's meetings which are available on my Department's website at: https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/articles/what-drainage-council. question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on discussions with Treasury regarding the pause in funding for the Mid South West and Causeway Coast and Glens Growth Deals.

To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his Department would consider working in conjunction with local councils to develop a centralised, publicly accessible list of all registered and licensed breeders and sellers in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: At present, there is no legal basis to collate and publish this information on a Northern Ireland wide basis. However, there may be scope, under the current legislation, for councils to modify the way they record and process licence applications from dog breeders and publish this information on a council-by-council basis. It is for councils to determine if this was feasible in the first instance.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the redevelopment of Enniskillen library.
answer_text: A new public library facility at Enniskillen remains a priority for my Department and Libraries NI.  An addendum to the original business case for the library was recently submitted to my Department by Libraries NI and this is being progressed. The future contribution of the Department to the project will be considered alongside the Department’s other priority capital projects and will be dependent on prioritisation of capital investment in a very difficult budgetary situation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when the findings of the review of the part-time 20mph speed limits at schools will be published.
answer_text: A review of the initiative introducing part-time 20mph speed limits at schools has been undertaken and I recently discussed the draft review report with my officials.  I have asked for the report to be shared with the Infrastructure Committee, following which it will be made publicly available.One of the best ways to ensure safety on our roads and fulfil our long-term goal of eliminating death and serious injury by 2050, is by changing road user behaviour. As road users we all have a personal responsibility to behave in a way that keeps ourselves and others safe.  The sad reality is that if we do not change our own attitudes when we are using the roads, our death toll will continue to increase.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline the discussions between his Department, the Regulation and Quality Improvement Authority and the Western Heath and Social Care Trust regarding the Review of the Pathways Associated with the Temporary Suspension of Emergency General Surgery at South West Acute Hospital.
answer_text: My Department requested the RQIA to undertake a focused review of the pathways developed as a result of the temporary suspension of Emergency General Surgery in South West Acute Hospital (SWAH), and to consider whether any improvements might be made. The Western Health and Social Care Trust has welcomed this review.  Both my Department and the Western Trust are aware of the importance of maintaining the independence of the review.  Discussions with the RQIA throughout the course of the review have focussed on provision of information pertinent to the review and updates on progress.  I can advise that information gathering for the review has finished and a report detailing its findings is nearing completion.  I expect to receive and consider the report in the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of children who are home educated, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has provided the table below which details the number of children who are home educated, broken down by council area. The EA are unable to provide this data broken down by constituency. Number of children recorded by the EA as being Electively Home Educated in 2023/24Council AreaPrimaryPost PrimaryAntrim & Newtownabbey2158Ards & North Down4063Armagh, Banbridge & Craigavon5594Belfast44105Causeway Coast & Glens2256Derry & Strabane2655Fermanagh & Omagh2981Lisburn & Castlereagh3151Mid & East Antrim1964Mid Ulster2248Newry Mourne & Down2748Total336723Overall total1059The legislative position in Northern Ireland is that parents are responsible for ensuring their child receives an education, at school or otherwise. Parents may therefore choose to educate their children at home rather than register them at a school and they may choose to deregister their child from school with the intention of educating them at home. Whilst the EA is aware of children who have attended and subsequently been deregistered from a school, there is no duty on parents who have never registered their child at a school to inform the Department of Education or the EA if they have chosen to educate their children at home. Thus, there is no complete record of children being electively home educated in Northern Ireland. This is the case in other jurisdictions.

answer_text: My officials are currently collating this information and will share as soon as practicable.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the actions his Department is taking to raise awareness and understanding of mobile connectivity among local councils, given the increase in attacks on 5G infrastructure.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Barrier Busting Taskforce (NIBBT) was established on 13 December 2022 as the delivery vehicle for the actions identified within the Mobile Action Plan for Northern Ireland (MAP NI). The NIBBT has cross sectoral membership and includes representatives from local councils. A subgroup within the NIBBT is delivering a programme of information, to help inform citizens, elected representatives and the local business community address any perceived threats regarding new mobile technologies and outline the advantages, opportunities and benefits to the local economy.Local councils have representation on this subgroup and it is their responsibility to publicise and to raise awareness and understanding of mobile connectivity within their own organisations. It is my understanding that each council has now nominated a Digital Champion, who may be well placed to assist in this information delivery.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy when the outcomes will be presented as part of the findings of the Mobile Action Plan for Northern Ireland Barrier Busting Taskforce Oversight Group.
answer_text: The Mobile Action Plan for Northern Ireland (MAP NI) was published on 23 June 2022. The delivery vehicle for the actions identified within the MAP NI is the Northern Ireland Barrier Busting Taskforce (NIBBT). The NIBBT first met on 13 December 2022 and is comprised of an oversight group, who have met 7 times, and three subgroups which have been tasked with delivering outstanding MAP NI actions and dealing with specific barrier busting themes.The three subgroups are as follows:The NIBBT Communications Subgroup, who have met 11 times.The NIBBT Planning Subgroup, who have met 2 times (that DfE is aware of). The Publicly Owned Assets Subgroup, who have met 2 times.The NIBBT membership is drawn from central and local government departments, industry and statutory organisations identified as having responsibility for an area of mobile connectivity implementation or policy. Officials from my Department chair the NIBBT Oversight Group and the NIBBT Communication Subgroup. The NIBBT Communication Subgroup is delivering a programme of information to help inform citizens, address any perceived risks regarding new mobile technologies and outline the advantages, opportunities and benefits to the local economy.  I am confident that every effort to develop and deliver all actions that fall within my Department’s remit are being progressed at pace. The work streams ongoing required to deliver other MAP NI actions by the NIBBT, are dependent on participation of all stakeholders who sit outside my Department. I have asked my officials to discuss a delivery timeframe from all NIBBT members at the next NIBBT meeting and will publish this at https://www.economy-ni.gov.uk/articles/map-ni-implementation-progress when it is available.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the costs for submitting a planning application for a renewable energy scheme; (ii) how these costs are calculated; and (iii) how the costs differ on scale of application.
answer_text: Under the Planning (Fees) (Amendment) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2024,  the fees for  renewable planning applications are calculated as follows;	(a) where the site area does not exceed 5 hectares, £425 for each 0.1 hectare of thesite area;(b) where the site area exceeds 5 hectares, £21,250; and an additional £126 for each 0.1 hectare in excess of 5 hectares, subject to a maximum in total of £316,468.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) how his Department intends to support allied health professionals to deliver new National Institute for Health and Care Excellence guidelines for stroke rehabilitation, which now recommend at least three hours of therapy, five days a week for stroke survivors; and (ii) what consideration his Department will give to the use and roll-out of new technologies and innovations, such as virtual reality, to enhance intensity and frequency of stroke rehabilitation therapies.
answer_text: I recognise the important role of Allied Health Professionals in providing rehabilitation services for stroke patients. I understand that the recent changes in National Institute of Health and Care Excellence (NICE) will further impact on existing pressures within the stroke rehabilitation service. In the context of these pressures, my Department intends to take forward a Stroke Workforce Review later this year. The Review will identify the future workforce required to deliver a high-quality stroke service and will be informed by best practice guidance including the revised NICE guidelines on stroke rehabilitation.In the interim, my Department is supporting innovative ways of delivering stroke rehabilitation services, including a pilot of multidisciplinary team stroke rehabilitation groups in the South Eastern Trust. In addition, a pilot has also been introduced in the Belfast Trust to support the use of virtual reality in the delivery of Occupational Therapy. An app to support the delivery of Speech and Language Therapy is also currently in development. It is important to note that the introduction of such technology beyond the pilot phase is subject to additional funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the number of renewable energy planning applications currently under consideration by the Planning Appeals Committee; and (ii) the average length of time for a decision to be taken.
answer_text: The Department currently has 4no. renewable energy planning applications currently referred to the Planning Appeals Commission (PAC) to hear and report on.In all four cases no recommendation has yet been made by the PAC. The PAC advises on their website that all these applications are being held in abeyance pending availability of Commissioners.The Department does not hold a record of what other renewable energy planning applications are subject of current planning appeals with the PAC.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the average time for a renewable energy planning application to proceed through the planning system from submission to completion.
answer_text: Based on the latest published provisional figures, the average processing times (weeks) for renewable energy applications this year to date 2023/24 up to Quarter 3 2023/24 is 30.8 weeksThe time taken to process a decision/withdrawal is calculated from the date on which an application is deemed valid to the date on which the decision is issued or the application is withdrawn. The median is used for the average processing time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the current waiting time from referral to appointment for Child and Adolescent Mental Health Services in the (a) Western Health and Social Care Trust; and (b) Southern Health and Social Care Trust; and (ii) the number of children currently on both waiting lists.
answer_text: Completed wait times (waits from referral to assessment/appointment) for the Child and Adolescent Mental Health Service (CAMHS) is not routinely collected by my department.My department does publish CAMHS active (current) wait time information broken down by waits over nine weeks, including total number waiting in each Health and Social Care Trust on a quarterly basis. Children and adolescent mental health service (CAMHS) statistics | Department of Health (health-ni.gov.uk)

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the (i) actions; and (ii) strategies in place to reduce waiting times and access to Child and Adolescent Mental Health Services in the (a) Western Health and Social Care Trust; and (b) Southern Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The Mental Health Strategy (MHS) sets the future strategic direction of mental health services in Northern Ireland for the next decade. There are four specific actions relating directly to Child and Adolescent Mental Health Services (CAMHS) whilst other actions will have an impact on CAMHS. Action 32 recognises the staffing challenges across CAMHS and a comprehensive workforce review was completed in March 2022. This review considered; existing workforce, the need to adopt new models of working and the importance of increasing the focus on training, recruitment and retention issues. My Department is committed to create a workforce that will meet the needs of the population and which is fit for the future.  The Mental Health Strategy also acknowledged that CAMHS requires additional investment and Action 10 of MHS gives a commitment to increase funding to 10% of the Adult Mental Health Budget. My Department will continue to work closely and collaboratively with Health and Social Care Trusts and other key stakeholders to develop services that meet not only current but emerging needs of this population and work with executive colleagues to secure the necessary resources required to meet demands.In relation to the effective management of diagnostic waiting lists, the Western and Southern Trust’s Child and Adolescent Mental Health Services adhere to the Integrated Elective Access Protocol (IEAP). All referrals accepted by CAMHS Step 2 and Step 3 services should be seen within a 9-week target.     (a) 	Western Health and Social Care Trust  As of July 2024, the Western Trust is reporting a 23% deficit in staffing across its Step 2 and Step 3 CAMHS service. This deficit is impacting upon its ability to meet demand and ensure children are assessed within IEAP targets.   CAMHS senior management continue to work in close partnership with Senior Trust Managers, Trust Quality Improvement and Performance colleagues to improve the current situation. Current waiting times will not improve until all staff are in post. (b)	Southern Health and Social Care Trust The Southern Health and Social Care Trust is operating a waiting list initiative consisting of staff across CAMHS offering additional new patient assessments. It is anticipated that by September the service will meet IEAP targets.  All young people who are currently on the waiting list have an appointment booked and have been offered group-based programmes during the summer period to provide support.  Information has been sent to families whilst they are waiting, in the form of online resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether she plans to introduce an equivalent reduction in rates for retail, hospitality and leisure business, similar to those in England and Wales.
answer_text: Over 75% of all non-domestic ratepayers here currently receive some form of rates support. In the last rating year this cost over £250 million. We have a markedly different taxbase here that simply makes an approach, such as that introduced in England and Wales, unaffordable.The Barnett consequential that the Executive received in respect of that scheme would fall well short of funding the reduction in rates for retail, hospitality and leisure business provided in England. A conservative estimate would suggest that we would need an extra £40M on top of the Barnett consequentials to fund such a scheme. Decisions on Barnett allocations are determined by the Executive based on funding priorities. Scotland does not have the retail, hospitality and leisure scheme at all, and Wales tried to do it in 2023/24 and then had to cut the relief provided under it for 2024/25. My focus is on looking at how our system can be realigned to reflect and fund the Executive priorities in conjunction with wider Budget sustainability work.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail the treatments that are not currently available to under 16-year olds under NHS dentistry.
answer_text: The range of dental treatment available under General Dental Services (GDS) is set out in the Statement of Dental Remuneration (SDR).  Within the SDR there are three treatments which are normally only available to those aged 17 and over, i.e. are not available to those aged 16 and under.  They are:Porcelain veneers. These are custom-made facings used to cover the front surfaces of teeth in order to conceal tooth discolouration and other cosmetic imperfections.Inlays and crowns. Inlays are laboratory made fillings which are used to restore cavities on the biting surfaces of back teeth. Crowns are tooth shaped caps that cover broken or weakened front or back teeth.Bridges. These are artificial teeth which are attached to natural teeth and are used to replace missing teeth.Many items in the SDR have conditions which must be satisfied when considering provision of the treatment. In some cases, the conditions relate to the patient’s age or the stability of the teeth and their supporting structures.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the steps he is taking to improve access to NHS dentists in Fermanagh and South Tyrone, including those patients who have relocated there and were previously registered with an NHS dentist in another area.
answer_text: The commissioning of General Dental Services is undertaken on a regional basis, rather than within HSC Trust boundaries.Dental Practices are private businesses, and as such, the Department has no role in placing patients; it is the decision of the practice whether or not to accept new patients, whether Health Service or private. In March 2024, a 6% pay uplift for General Dental Services for 2023/24, in line with recommendations contained in the 51st Doctors and Dentists Review Board report, was announced. This uplift comes with a 7.3% uplift to non-staff expenses for 2023/24, with a 3% recurrent award in future years. Practices in Fermanagh and South Tyrone will benefit from this additional investment.In March 2024 the Department also announced a £9.2m investment package for General Dental Services to include:£1m for newly registered child patients through the reestablishment of the Enhanced Child Examination Scheme. This scheme provides children aged 0-10 who have not been registered with a dentist with an examination, individualised oral health advice and age-specific fluoride application to teeth to assist with preventing dental decay;£4.3m to fund a 30% enhancement to fees paid to dentists for health service fillings, extractions and root canal treatment for 2024/25, to support public access to priority treatments; and£3.9m for the treatment of high priority unregistered patients through a Dental Access Scheme.Practices in Fermanagh and South Tyrone will benefit from all three of these new funding streams.Until the Dental Access Scheme is implemented, a pilot scheme will continue to run to provide appointments to unregistered patients who require emergency dental treatment during the working week. If a patient is not registered with a dentist, requires emergency dental treatment, and has been unable to access an appointment, they can contact their local Strategic Planning and Performance Group office for advice.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will request a review of the Education Authority's procurement of goods and services.
answer_text: The Education Authority is designated as education’s Centre of Procurement Expertise and is responsible for the procurement of goods and services on behalf of schools. The EA is responsible for ensuring that effective procurement practice is embedded across the education sector to provide best value for money. Controlled and maintained schools are required to comply with the EA’s purchasing and contracting procedures, using EA contracts where they exist.All grant funded schools are also required to operate within the procurement legal framework and to adhere to Northern Ireland Public Procurement Policy and the relevant Northern Ireland Procurement Guidance Notes.  The EA has developed a new procurement strategy for its new maintenance and minor works contracts, based on defined geographical areas which will allow schools greater freedom to engage locally approved suppliers.  The EA has undertaken a review of schools’ tenant/ landlord responsibilities and plans to introduce new arrangements, with the aim of providing greater flexibility for the delivery of Local Management of Schools low-value maintenance works and cost certainty across the EA schools’ estate. The EA will also explore the application of this type of delivery model across the range of goods and services which it currently procures on behalf of schools.As the Minister of Finance has recently commissioned a review of Public Procurement, in line with recommendations made by the Northern Ireland Audit Office in its April 2023 Review of Public Procurement, I do not intend to request a review of the EA’s procurement of goods and services at this time.  I anticipate that procurement within education will form part of this review and to that end, both my Department and the EA look forward to contributing to the review.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) whether he is aware that the midwifery course at Queen’s University has received its full quota of places allocated by his Department for the upcoming academic year; and (ii) whether he plans to increase the quota of places for the course to meet the demand.
answer_text: My Department has commissioned 120 pre-registration midwifery training places for the 2024/25 academic year, from Queen’s University Belfast - the highest ever number of midwifery pre-registration training places. I will continue to consider opportunities for expansion across all pre-registration programmes commissioned by my Department,  however, this process will be determined by the availability of funding and the assessment of priorities.

The Villas have been unoccupied for the whole of the relevant period and Stormont House has been unoccupied since the Northern Ireland Office vacated the building in April 2022.	The cost of maintenance in each of the last 5 years is in the table below. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the cover in GP out-of-hours services in each Health and Social Care Trust in the last two years.
answer_text: SPPG commissions GP Out of Hours (OOHs) services across Northern Ireland from five GP OOHs Providers.  The GP OOHs service is available across the region Mon-Fri from 6pm to 8am the next morning, as well as 24 hours at weekends and during public holidays.  All Patients can contact the GP OOHs service in their area via telephone and will be triaged by a telephone or video call by clinical staff.  Following telephone triage, the healthcare provided by GP OOHs will be in the form of advice, a base attendance, or a home visit.  All calls to the service are prioritised based upon their clinical urgency and those patients who are assessed as urgent will be triaged and allocated appointments first.  In 2023/24 19% of contacts across the region required a base visit.  In the last two years the GP OOHs service has been available to patients at all specified service opening times.  The majority of OOHs bases across Northern Ireland are located in close proximity to an Emergency Department.Clinical cover for the provision of GP OOHs services is provided by a multidisciplinary workforce including Advanced Nurse Practitioners, Nurse Advisors, Pharmacists, Paramedics and Mental health workers. GPs are not contractually obliged to work in GP OOHs and the majority of the clinical workforce is on an ‘as and when required’ basis.  Clinical cover secured by OOHs Providers in 22/23 and 23/24 has increased on 21/22 cover levels.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will consider extending access to the Degree in Social Work - Student Incentive Scheme to students from Northern Ireland who have been unable to secure a university place in Northern Ireland and have had to pursue their degree elsewhere in the United Kingdom.
answer_text: The Social Work Student Incentive Scheme is available for eligible students, domicile in Northern Ireland, who study to achieve a NISCC approved Degree in Social Work at undergraduate level here. The rationale for restricting the Scheme to NI is based on evidence which found that more than 70% of students funded to study courses outside of Northern Ireland did not return to work here, whereas, the majority of local graduates remain and join the workforce in NI.Unfortunately, this means that individuals who study social work outside of NI place themselves outside the remit of the Scheme.Regrettably, given the extremely challenging budgetary situation, the Department is unable to provide any more assistance to students on professional training programmes than it does at present.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department plans to improve endometriosis care, to include a multi-disciplinary and a dedicated hub with specialist nurses that can provide advice and support.
answer_text: Covid, and general overarching pressures within the Gynaecology Service in Northern Ireland have delayed the development of Endometriosis Services. As you may be aware, my Department recently launched the Women’s Health Action Plan for Northern Ireland and we have also published the Getting It Right First Time (GIRFT) Report for Gynaecology – this signals our commitment to working towards improving services for women which includes those suffering from endometriosis. The GIRFT report has identified 20 general recommendations to improve services through coordinated regional action, as well as a list of specific recommendations for each Health and Social Care (HSC) Trust to take forward. These recommendations have been accepted in principle with a view to implementing the required improvements as soon as it is feasible; however, it is acknowledged that some recommendations may require additional funding and lead-in time to deliver fully.It is our intention to progress the development of Endometriosis Services in the context of the GIRFT implementation plan which will be developed in the coming months.  This will include further development of the role of specialist nurses, and consideration of a multi-disciplinary approach to the management of patients.The Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) have established  a regional forum, with Trust, GP and Public Health Agency (PHA) representation, to oversee ongoing service improvement and implementation at a local and regional level, to take forward the recommendations, detailed in the GIRFT report, funding permitted.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 5422/22-27.
To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 5551/22-27.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when he will inform Community Transport of its budget position from 1 June 2024 onwards.
answer_text: I fully recognise the importance of community transport and how both rural Dial-a-Lift (DAL) and urban Disability Action Transport Scheme (DATS) services continue to help improve access to local health appointments, shops and facilities, voluntary groups, friends and family. These services enable people to be independent and remain connected to their local communities.In the absence of an agreed budget at the beginning of the financial year, I have approved interim funding for both DAL and DATS for April to June 2024 based on 2023/24 funding levels to support continued provision of community transport.  My officials provided this update to those organisations on 1st May 2024.  The overall budget allocation for 2024/25 is currently being considered.My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government.  The value of our infrastructure cannot be overstated, and I will continue to work with Executive colleagues to ensure appropriate investment is made.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department has considered the dualling of the A4 between Ballygawley and Enniskillen.
answer_text: My Department is in the process of developing a new Transport Strategy that will help to shape the vision and strategic priorities for transport through to 2035.  Once this is published, it will help to inform the Regional Strategic Transport Network Transport Plan, which looks at key transport corridors linking our communities.  At this stage, I have not taken any decisions in relation to the Plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he plans to reintroduce the Republic of Ireland Cross-Border Reimbursement Scheme.
answer_text: In my reply, dated 4 March 2024, to your Assembly Question (AQW 6663/22-27 refers) regarding the reinstatement of the cross border healthcare reimbursement scheme, I outlined the background and closure of the Republic of Ireland Reimbursement Scheme (RoIRS). Following the success of the scheme I introduced in June 2021, I had hoped to introduce a new reimbursement scheme this year. I included a bid for funding for a new replacement, which could have treated over 4,000 patients during this year’s budget setting process. Unfortunately, under the 2024/25 Budget recently approved by the Executive, precisely zero additional funding has been provided for tackling our waiting lists.Unfortunately, instead of now being able to invest in such waiting list initiatives, my Department’s budget has been cut by over 2%. This is a deeply regrettable and concerning position.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will bring forward a plan of investment for the reduction of waiting lists.
To ask the Minister of Health whether he plans to regulate the hourly rates of agency care staff employed by Health and Social Care Trusts in order to encourage recruitment to the sector.
answer_text: My Department, working in conjunction with HSC Trusts through the Regional Agency Reduction Implementation Group (ARIG), has undertaken significant work to date and is continuing at pace to attempt to address the issues of the rising trend in agency expenditure.It is important to note that the agency rate has to cover all employer pay related costs that would be associated with the cost of Trust employed staff such as pension contributions, annual/sick leave, therefore the differential between Trust rates and those of agencies is significantly lower than those previously pertaining to off framework rates.The implementation of the new nursing and midwifery framework has eliminated the use of off framework agency in delivering health and social care services across HSC in Northern Ireland with only two exceptions. It is the expectation of the ARIG that this approach will be replicated with the proposed new Medical/Dental and Non-Medical frameworks.The ARIG will continue to monitor all opportunities in which to reduce agency expenditure and encourage the progression of recruitment to HSCNI organisations.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of electric vehicle charge points in the Fermanagh and South Tyrone constituency, broken down by town and village.
answer_text: The provision and operation of publicly accessible electric vehicle charge points is a commercial operation. Responsibility for the maintenance and installation of those charge points rests solely with the charge point operators (CPOs). In this commercially led market, officials continue to engage with EV owners and provide advice to CPOs on operational matters and my Department is leading an EV Infrastructure Task Force, bringing together representatives from government, consumers, energy providers, industry and EV drivers to consider the barriers to enhancing our EV charging infrastructure requirements.  My Department does not hold information detailing electric vehicle charging points or their locations.  Relevant up to date charge point locations can be found on online platforms, such as https://www.zapmap.com.  The relevant CPOs are best placed to advise you of their plans for the installation of charge points in your constituency.   Local Councils have also been able to avail of funding for the installation of charge points under the Office for Zero Emissions’ On Street Residential Charge Point Scheme which my Department match funded. Derry City and Strabane District Council are leading a consortium on this work having been successful in securing funding to install 127 charge points across nine Council areas, and may be in a position to outline their plans within your constituency should you wish to contact them. It may also be of interest that The British Government provide statistical updates quarterly on the number of publicly available charging points available in the UK.  The latest figures published in April 2024 indicate that there are now over 545 publicly available charge points, 53 of which are within the Fermanagh and South Tyrone Constituency. This represents a 40% increase in overall charge point numbers since April 2023. The statistics can be found here: Electric vehicle public charging infrastructure statistics: April 2024 - GOV.UK (www.gov.uk).

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what consideration his Department has given to provide a cross pavement charging solution for homeowners without a driveway, who wish to be able to charge their electric vehicles at home.
answer_text: Over 80% of houses in the North have access to off street parking. In these situations, charging solutions are already available. However, for those who rely on on-street parking at their homes, home charging solutions are more problematic, primarily due to the issue of the charging cable needing to cross the footway. My Departmental officials are engaging with colleagues in other jurisdictions to evaluate the benefits of a number of pilot schemes being progressed in relation to cross pavement charging solutions.  Engagement is also taking place with key stakeholders and the Office Zero Emission Vehicles (OZEV), which has recently announced the availability of grants for on street charging solutions. Guidance for applicants, local councils and Highway Authorities is currently being prepared by OZEV.  Once available this will be used to assess if the arrangements for such installations at particular locations can satisfy the conditions of the grant and legislative arrangements contained in the Roads (Northern Ireland) Order 1993 where a street works licence is required to allow works to be carried out to facilitate the safe channelling or suspension of a cable. This legislation is in place to protect public health and safety and to address ownership and liability issues.Currently across the North there are around 545 publicly available charge points provided commercially by a range of Charge Point Operators which allow convenient access to charging for EV owners who are unable to charge their vehicles at home. This represents a 40% increase in total numbers since April 2023.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of requests for an ambulance in the Western Health and Social Care Trust in the first quarter of 2024.
answer_text: Information on the number of requests for an ambulance and the number of times when an ambulance was requested but was not available for attendance in the Western Health and Social Care Trust (HSCT) in the first quarter of 2024 is not held centrally. However, the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service (NIAS) has provided a response in relation to both these questions.Table 1, below, shows the total number of requests for an ambulance in the Western HSCT in the first quarter of 2024, broken down by emergency and routine incidents. Table 1: Total number of requests for an ambulance in the Western HSCT in the first quarter of 2024, broken down by emergency and routine incidentsMonth/YearNumber of Emergency IncidentsNumber of Routine IncidentsTotalJanuary 202428241142938February 20242522992621March 20242798862884Total81442998443In response to the number of times when an ambulance was requested but was not available for attendance in the Western Health HSCT, NIAS advised that it is committed to ensuring that every call for an ambulance receives a prompt and appropriate response. In line with this commitment, NIAS ensures that no call from a patient requesting an ambulance will go unanswered. NIAS advised that each call is assessed and addressed, either through telephonic consultation with a qualified clinician or by dispatching the necessary ambulance resource to the scene. Patients' calls are systematically queued in the ambulance stack, prioritised based on urgency, and responded to as soon as an ambulance or clinician becomes available.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the response times by Natural Environment Division to feedback requests from planners in relation to wind farm applications, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Officials in the Natural Environment Division (NED) do not hold this information.Staff in the local planning authorities will be aware that NED has not been providing detailed updates in relation to timeframes for issuing consultation responses, including those associated with wind farm applications.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) to detail the actions he is taking to improve response times by Natural Environment Division to feedback requests from planners in relation to wind farm applications; and (ii) whether additional staffing is being considered.
answer_text: (i)  Staff in the local planning authorities will be aware that NED has not been providing detailed updates in relation to timeframes for issuing consultation responses, including those associated with wind farm applications.  		NED took the decision to cease provision of updates & prioritisation in November 2021 in order to improve planning consultation response timeframes.  At that time the total outstanding caseload was close to 600 cases.  		Provision of detailed updates significantly reduced the time officials had for processing consultation case work responses. This measure was therefore initiated to maximise the time case officers spent on responding to planning consultations as opposed to providing such updates.  		As of 1 May 2024, NED’s total outstanding caseload is approximately 220 cases.  		(ii)  An additional member of staff has been redeployed to planning teams within NED on a temporary basis to respond to wind farm consultations whilst new avenues to secure additional resource are explored.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for her assessment of the method used for collecting rates from businesses on high streets in towns and villages.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) why the response to the consultation on the Review of Regional Strategic Planning Policy on Renewable and Low Carbon Energy has yet to be published, despite the consultation closing in June 2023; and (ii) when he intends to publish it.
answer_text: (i) I was briefed by officials on the progress made on the review, including the outcome of public consultation on draft policy proposals, at the earliest opportunity following the restoration of the political institutions; (ii) the Departmental Response to the Public Consultation report will be released at the same time as publishing the revised policy in its final form which the Department is aiming to do later this year, subject to Executive Committee agreement.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the role the planning system can have in facilitating the statutory requirement to achieve 80 per cent renewable electricity by 2030; and (ii) the plans in place to accelerate decision making on renewable applications.
answer_text: My Department has and continues to engage with DfE, the Renewables Industry and local authorities in terms of the challenges that lie ahead given these ambitious targets with a view to encouraging better quality of submissions on appropriate sites.I am committed to ensuring that our tier planning system operates in an efficient and effective way. My Department is working with local government to take forward a Planning Improvement Programme (PIP) which aims to improve current processes and increase the efficiency of the current system. The Programme includes actions across a number of key themes including Governance; Policy & Legislation; Local Development Plan; Development Management; performance, engagement and financial sustainability.To achieve an efficient and effective planning system and to further progress the commitment to achieve an efficient and effective planning system will however be dependent on appropriate resourcing to key areas of the system. My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government. The value of our infrastructure cannot be overstated, and I will continue to work with Executive colleagues to ensure appropriate investment is made.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 5422/22-27.
To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 5551/22-27.
To ask the Minister of Health when an allocation of multi-disciplinary team funding will be received by the South West GP Federation.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) an update on progress made in developing the replacement facility for the Sydenham Pumping Station; (ii) the budget established for the proposed new facility; and (iii) when work will commence on the replacement facility.
answer_text: The query you have raised is an operational matter for NI Water. You should therefore direct your question to NI Water through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any alternatives available to tenants of Housing Associations who would have previously benefitted from the Right to Buy Scheme, broken down by Housing Association.
answer_text: When the Right to Buy scheme ended for registered housing associations, a provision was included in the Housing (Amendment) Act (NI) 2020 to enable the Department to make grants to registered housing associations in respect of voluntary right to buy schemes. There have been no such requests from Housing Associations. The introduction of a voluntary sales scheme is for each individual housing association to decide upon. The terms and conditions for any payment of grant would be for the Department to determine, and subject to budget being available.Co-Ownership may be considered as a more general alternative to help people into home ownership. Further information on the applicant and property criteria for Co-Ownership is available at https://www.co-ownership.org/

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the number of apprenticeship opportunities available in the last 12 months, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: My Department’s ApprenticeshipsNI and Higher-Level Apprenticeships (HLA) programmes are demand-led, with apprentices recruited and retained by employers in line with business needs. Data is gathered at different times across both programmes so it is not possible to provide published participant data for the last 12 months as requested.  However, the attached table provides the latest position across both programmes, broken down by constituency. It should be noted that constituency data is based on an apprentice’s home postcode.  Although the apprentice may live in a particular constituency, they may attend work elsewhere.All participants on ApprenticeshipsNI and Higher Level Apprenticeships by Parliamentary Constituency  ApprenticeshipsNI participants (January 2024)HLAs in Further Education (Academic year 2022/23)HLAs at Higher Education Institutions (Academic year 2021/22)Belfast East3604025Belfast North5023520Belfast South2564550Belfast West4532525East Antrim5316035East Londonderry5486015Fermanagh and South Tyrone81817515Foyle6585035Lagan Valley5028040Mid Ulster91016550Newry and Armagh84017545North Antrim6986535North Down3364025South Antrim6036545South Down74215050Strangford5466020Upper Bann67912555West Tyrone80911030Not known40750Total11,1981,530620NotesThe ApprenticeshipsNI programme is delivered at Level 2-3 by the six Further Education Colleges and over 20 private training organisations.  The Higher Level Apprenticeship programme is delivered by six local Further Education Colleges, plus CAFRE (Levels 4-7) and local Higher Education Institutions (Level 6-7).  "Not known" includes those participants for whom postcode is not known/incorrect or where postcode cannot be mapped to a particular Constituency.Data Rounding. To prevent the identification of individuals, figures are rounded to the nearest 5, with 0, 1, and 2 rounded to 0. Due to rounding, the sum of numbers in each row or column may not match the total shown. Additional statistical information on apprenticeships can be accessed at: https://www.economy-ni.gov.uk/topics/statistics-and-economic-research/higher-and-further-education-and-training-statistics

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the Rural Micro Capital Grants Scheme.
answer_text: My officials have completed an evaluation of the 2022 Rural Micro Capital Grant Scheme.The findings, lessons learned and recommendations from the 2022 evaluation and the independent evaluation of the schemes delivered between 2016 and 2021 have been considered and a Business Case proposing to launch the Scheme in 2024/2025 is at an advanced stage.Once the Executive have agreed a budget for 2024/2025, I anticipate being in a position to make an announcement regarding the Rural Micro Capital Grant Scheme.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for a breakdown of non-Covid related funding provided by his Department to sports clubs, broken down by constituency, in the last five years.
answer_text: Due to the volume of information to be provided, I have arranged for the information requested to be placed in the Assembly Library.I can also advise that all funding information is available on the Government Funding Database. This website is publicly accessible and holds records of applications and funding to voluntary and community sector organisations including funding disbursed to the sports sector.  I have included the link for future convenience - https://govfunding.nics.gov.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail how often water testing is carried out in the River Blackwater to ensure harmful chemicals and toxins are not present in the waterways.
answer_text: My Department undertakes monitoring on the River Blackwater under The Water Environment (Water Framework) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2017.   A combination of monthly and quarterly monitoring is undertaken for 29 chemicals and trace metals.  During each six-year River Basin Plan cycle, additional monitoring is undertaken each month for a twelve month period. This additional testing looks for a wide range of pollutants that are considered potential threats to water quality. Verification of reports of potential sightings of blue green algae is undertaken through the Blooming Algae App and notifications issued to Local Councils and water operators.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail how often water testing is carried out in Lough Erne to ensure harmful chemicals and toxins are not present in the waterways.
answer_text: My Department undertakes monitoring across seven sites on Upper and Lower Lough Erne under The Water Environment (Water Framework) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2017.   A combination of monthly and quarterly monitoring is undertaken for 29 chemicals and trace metals.  During each six-year River Basin Plan cycle, additional monitoring is undertaken each month for a twelve month period. This additional testing looks for a wide range of pollutants that are considered potential threats to water quality. Verification of reports of potential sightings of blue green algae is undertaken through the Blooming Algae App and notifications issued to Local Councils and water operators.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he has any plans to review the work of Place Shaping teams in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: I have no current plans to review the work of the Housing Executive’s three Regional Place Shaping Teams. The Housing Executive has statutory responsibilities which are delivered by its Regional Place Shaping Teams who undertake a range of activities in accordance with its legislative responsibilities. These are operational matters for the Housing Executive.I have been advised by the Chief Executive of the Housing Executive that its three Regional Place Shaping leads would be happy to engage to discuss any of its areas of business.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for a breakdown of funding provided to grassroots (i) football; (ii) rugby; (iii) hockey; (iv) GAA; and (v) hurling clubs in the last two years.
answer_text: There is no standardised definition of “grassroots” across the various sporting codes. It may be considered that the majority of our local clubs, even where there is an element of professionalism within the main team, make provisions for their grassroots levels which may include children and young people, girls and women and other under-represented groups. Therefore, it is difficult to identify what funding has been specifically allocated for grassroots sport.In addition, hurling is a sporting discipline that is governed by the GAA, with many local GAA clubs including hurling in their activities. As a result of this neither my Department nor Sport NI, an arm’s length body of my Department, are able to distinguish between funding disbursed for hurling from funding provided to GAA.Due to the volume of information to be provided, I have arranged for the information requested to be placed in the Assembly Library.I can also advise that all funding information is available on the Government Funding Database. This website is publicly accessible and holds records of applications and funding to voluntary and community sector organisations including funding disbursed to the sports sector.  I have included the link for future convenience - https://govfunding.nics.gov.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his Department plans to implement the recommendations of the NI Audit Office report Water Quality in Northern Ireland’s Rivers and Lakes.
answer_text: I am aware that water quality is not what it should be and, therefore, I welcome the Northern Ireland Audit Office’s report. While I recognise that this issue needs to be tackled urgently, I will need some time to consider the recommendations in the report and the action that is needed collectively across government, the public and private sectors, and also wider society. My vision is to have an environment where our water bodies are at good status or better, support biodiversity and contribute to health and well-being.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of (i) the number of housing new starts being held up by wastewater capacity limits; and (ii) the areas that are impacted by this.
answer_text: The impacts of wastewater capacity limits and the areas affected, are operational matters for NI Water.  You should therefore direct your question to NI Water, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the assessment criteria of bridges in Northern Ireland; and (ii) how often inspections take place.
answer_text: Our approach to the inspection of bridges is set out in the Design Manual for Roads and Bridges (DMRB), specifically CS450 – Inspection of highway structures.  DMRB requires the following structures to be included in the inspection regime:  all bridges of span 3m and greater; bridges 1.8m to 3m span if cover to road surface is less than 1m; multi-span bridges where the cumulative span is greater than or equal to 5m, if cover to road surface is less than 1m; corrugated metal culverts 0.9m or more in span; and, pedestrian subways.  A General Inspection of each bridge is to be undertaken every two years with a more detailed Principal Inspection carried out every six years. Additional non-scheduled inspections of bridges are often carried out, for example following flood events.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the number of bridges in Northern Ireland; and (ii) those that are assessed and maintained by his Department.
answer_text: My Department does not hold records for bridges which are located outside of the adopted road network or the railway network therefore I am unable to accurately answer the question as worded.My Department routinely inspects and maintains 5870 bridges on the adopted road network. It is estimated an additional 175 bridges are awaiting an initial inspection to enable them to be added to the Structures Management System. A further 436 bridges are maintained by Translink, an Arm’s Length Body of DfI.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to provide a list of schools to receive capital new school build works in the Fermanagh and South Tyrone constituency, in order of priority.
answer_text: There are currently four new school build works in the Fermanagh and South Tyrone constituency.  The schools are:Enniskillen Royal Grammar SchoolGaelscoil Aodha Rua, DungannonHoly Trinity Primary School, EnniskillenSt Ciaran’s College, DungannonWithout additional capital funding the first three projects listed above will not progress to construction, and the St Ciaran’s College project, which was announced in March 2022, will remain paused.  Should capital funding become available projects will be reviewed and prioritised for delivery.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he has considered raising the Personal Expenses Allowance rate for state-funded residents in care homes to the rates available in other UK jurisdictions.
answer_text: The Personal Expenses Allowance (PEA) rate is specified in the annual Charging for Residential Accommodation Guide (CRAG) circular. This amount is reviewed each year and the Department will notify Trusts of any change in the rate of personal expenses. The amount is the same for each resident, whether in a Trust managed home or an independent sector home.Any changes made to the Personal Expenses Allowance rate in other UK jurisdictions do not automatically apply to Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the proposed facility for victims and survivors at the former Lisnaskea High School site.
answer_text: In 2020, officials completed a scoping exercise on the feasibility of developing a new victims’ and survivors’ facility to be co-located on the site of the former Lisnaskea High School, along with a new Irish Medium School and a new Health Centre. This matter is currently under consideration as to the way forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the work of the interim Regional Planning Commission to date.
answer_text: An interim Regional Planning Commission (iRPC) was established in early 2023, comprised of representatives from key stakeholder groups and has met on 4 occasions. The iRPC has provided advice and support to the wider planning improvement programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the list of non-departmental bodies he has met since coming into post.
answer_text: Please see below the list of non-departmental bodies I have met since coming into post:British and Irish GovernmentsTrade Unions, GMB, SIPTU (Services, Industrial, Professional and Technical Union) and UniteCity Of Derry AirportMental Health Champion for NICommunity Places

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 5551/22-27.
To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 5422/22-27.
To ask the Assembly Commission to outline its plans to bring forward maternity provision for elected representatives.
To ask the Minister for the Economy to provide an update on the discussions he has had with the BT Group and Invest NI on the future of the EE call centre site in Enniskillen.
answer_text: Oral Question No 13 – AQO 187/22-27Intense discussions have been ongoing between my department and officials within Invest NI, both with BT and also with local stakeholders including Fermanagh and Omagh District Council and the Unions, since the announcement of the review of the BT Enniskillen Site on the 08 March 2024.Most recently, on Tuesday 27 February, Invest NI met with GB based representatives from EE along with local senior management from BT to discuss options for the staff and for EE in Enniskillen. On Wednesday 28 February I wrote to the Chief Executive of the BT Group to emphasise the importance of regional balance in Northern Ireland and to urge the business to retain the employment in the Enniskillen area.The following day BT confirmed that the deadline for BT staff to express their interest in the Voluntary Leavers Package had been extended from the 08 March to the 28 March. Invest NI will continue to engage with BT to discuss the potential options for retention of employment within BT and within the Enniskillen area. It is not appropriate to pre-empt the outcome of those discussions. BT has continued to state that no decisions have been taken, pending the outcome of the review of the EE site in Enniskillen.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the measures taken to reduce the waiting times for urology operations in the Western Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: My Department has been working to reduce the current waiting times for both urology assessments and treatments. Regional Day Procedure Centres have been established across the region which have increased operating capacity in a number of hospital sites, including in Omagh Hospital and Primary Care Complex. This expansion in operating capacity has helped reduce in the Western Trust’s urology day case waiting list which has decreased by 10% between March 2023 to December 2023. A new regional Extra Corporeal Shockwave Lithotripsy (ESWL) service has also been established in Craigavon Area Hospital, which is having a positive impact for patients waiting for urology stone laser treatment.In addition, regionally to date, £70m has been allocated in 2023/24 for additional waiting list activity. The funding is being used by all HSC Trusts to help reduce waiting times for assessment and treatment across a range of services, including urology in the Western Trust.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he is committed to ensuring Rural Community Transport will have a sustainable level of funding, given its importance within communities.
answer_text: I recognise the importance of community transport to those who have few if any travel options in rural areas and the value they attach to it.  The grant supported Dial-a-Lift transport service improves access to health appointments, to shops and facilitates, voluntary groups, to friends and family and enables people to be independent and remain connected to their local communities.While funding for community transport is not part of my Department’s statutory defined spend, and in the absence of an agreed budget for the coming year, I have approved funding for Rural Community Transport Partnerships for April and May 2024. This is subject to public expenditure processes and submission of completed applications, to support continuance of community transport in rural areas. This funding is based on the overall level of funding provided in 2023/24. I will be engaging with my Executive colleagues on agreeing a budget for the incoming financial year and will be seeking support for this service.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail what discussions he and his officials have had with the Rural Community Transport Partnerships.
answer_text: My officials are in regular contact with Rural Community Transport Partnerships.  This contact is in relation to the monitoring of grant support for the Dial-a-Lift Scheme and the administration of the Assisted Rural Travel Scheme. Officials also meet formally with the Partnerships on a quarterly basis, with the most recent meeting having taken place on 28 February 2024.I have provided an undertaking to meet with the Community Transport Association, which is the body which represents many of the Partnerships, along with representatives of the Partnerships themselves and this meeting has been scheduled for 09 April 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the urgent action his Department is taking following reports of cracks appearing in test centre lifts.
answer_text: In 2020, the Driver and Vehicle Agency (DVA) procured and installed 52 new scissor lifts. 16 scissor lifts to date across DVA test centres have been identified by the equipment provider with small hairline cracks in the welds of the main scissor bushing.  The DVA has a planned preventative maintenance program in place for vehicle testing equipment, including vehicle lifts and the inspection frequency for the affected lifts has been increased.  The equipment provider has advised that all the scissor lifts are still safe to continue using.The DVA also has independent insurance inspectors who carry out thorough examinations of the lifts.  For inspections carried out to date the insurance inspectors have reported their findings on the hairline cracks in their lowest category.A scissor arm from one of the affected lifts has been removed and a replacement arm has been installed. The equipment provider is assessing the cause of the small hairline cracks and the DVA is awaiting information on their findings, which will help inform the timescale for any remedial action required.All lifts remain in use and this issue has not had an impact on the provision of MOT tests

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to provide an update on proposals to amend legislation to help tackle anti-social behaviour.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he plans to review the legislation regarding the use of e-scooters.
answer_text: The position remains that it is illegal to use e-scooters on public roads and public spaces here. You may be aware that the Department for Transport announced its intention to create regulations which will legalise the use of electric scooters in England. The introduction of regulations will only apply in England. However, currently there is no detail on timings for the legislation.
