My officials will continue to monitor developments there and in due course I will consider the way forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether there are plans to allow drivers, who have passed their driving test from 1 January 1997 and have an ordinary category B licence, to tow a trailer with a maximum weight of 3.500kg, similar to that of the rest of the UK.
answer_text: Legislation introduced in Britain on 16 December 2021 removed testing requirements for towing a trailer (BE entitlement), meaning that all drivers who passed a category B (car) driving test from 1 January 1997 are now entitled to tow trailers up to 3,500kg Maximum Authorized Mass (MAM).  This change was made in an effort to address the HGV driver shortage situation in England, Scotland and Wales.At that time testing was not seen as a significant reason for HGV driver shortages here. This position has not changed.In EU Member States BE testing is still a requirement, as it is here. Road safety continues to be of paramount importance and therefore I have no plans to introduce similar changes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy when he will answer AQW 5987/22-27.
answer_text: I answered AQW 5987/22-27 as an urgent oral question that you tabled on Monday 12th February 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether there are any plans to ring-fence money for a rural roads scheme.
answer_text: I am giving careful consideration to a ring-fenced fund for rural roads as part of my deliberations in regards the 2024-25 budget process.The level of resurfacing of rural roads that can be carried out in future financial years will depend on the 2024-25 final budget determination for my Department. The importance of a well maintained road network cannot be understated but this requires investment. I will continue to seek the support of Executive colleagues to enable this to happen.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what plans his Department has to provide speed indicator devices, in identified areas, to help address the issue of speeding.
answer_text: Speed Indicator Devices (SIDs) are not considered to be traffic signs as they tell the driver what is already displayed on the speedometer within their vehicle, however, I do recognise that they offer some road safety benefits and am therefore content that groups such as local Policing and Community Safety Partnerships (PCSP) continue to provide them.  To facilitate this, the Department has developed a Director Of Engineering Memorandum (DEM), see: Speed Indicator Devices DEM 191/23 (infrastructure-ni.gov.uk).  In terms of future provision of SIDs, I would suggest that you make contact with your local PCSP.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of Housing Executive tenants that have been evicted from their property in the last 5 years, broken down by division.
answer_text: The number Housing Executive tenants who have been evicted from their property in the last five years, broken down by division is an operational matter for the Housing Executive.You should, therefore, direct your question to the Housing Executive at its dedicated MLA response team, which can be contacted at public.affairsunit@nihe.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of anti-social behaviour incidents relating to Housing Executive tenants in the last 5 years, broken down by division.
answer_text: The number of anti-social behaviour incidents relating to Housing Executive tenants in the last five years broken down by division is an operational matter for the Housing Executive.You should, therefore, direct your question to the Housing Executive at its dedicated MLA response team, which can be contacted at public.affairsunit@nihe.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether there are plans to reopen the Discretionary Housing Payment.
answer_text: The Discretionary Housing Payment Scheme remains open to new applicants. However, the Housing Executive had to reinstate the two-year maximum payment period in order to manage the DHP budget this financial year which was under severe pressure. Currently there are no plans within the Housing Executive or the Department to remove the maximum payment period. The reinstated two-year maximum payment period for DHPs remains a necessary mechanism to manage the limited DHP budget and ensure it remains available to as many of those who need it.Any claimant whose DHP was stopped has a right to appeal this decision. Whilst it is the case that anyone whose DHP was stopped this year is unable to reapply in the same financial year they can apply to the scheme from the beginning of the next financial year, and their case will be assessed by the Housing Executive on its own merits.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether there are plans to implement a provision to allow the extension of the slurry ban should inclement weather conditions prevail making it more difficult to spread slurry.
answer_text: The closed period for spreading slurry is a key requirement of the Nutrients Action Programme (NAP) Regulations and applies from 15 October to 31 January each year. Outside of the closed period, there are already provisions in the NAP Regulations for when weather and soil conditions are unsuitable for spreading slurry. These provisions are to reduce the risk of nutrient run off and pollution of waterways.Slurry and all other fertilisers must not be spread when soil is waterlogged, likely to flood or flooded, frozen or snow covered, or when heavy rain is falling or forecast within 48 hours. My Department is reviewing the Nutrients Action Programme this year and the review will consider what changes may be needed to improve implementation, effectiveness and to further protect water quality in our rivers, lakes and groundwaters.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for a breakdown of (i) the number of Housing Executive tenants currently in prison, per division; (ii) the number of Housing Executive tenants currently in prison who are currently in rates arrears, per division; and (iii) the cost of rates arrears caused by those in prison, per division.
answer_text: The breakdown of Housing Executive tenants currently in prison and any related breakdown of arrears is an operational matter for the Housing Executive.You should, therefore, direct your question to the Housing Executive at its dedicated MLA response team, which can be contacted at public.affairsunit@nihe.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on what discussions Invest NI has held with BT and EE regarding the announcement of possible job losses in Enniskillen.
answer_text: I can confirm that a meeting between Invest NI executives and BT took place on Tuesday 13 February 2024 regarding the announcement of a review of the future of its EE customer contact centre in Enniskillen. At the meeting, BT confirmed that it is undertaking what it describes as a major modernisation programme across its global business. This includes the introduction of new technologies and innovation, and the consolidation of buildings within its estate. The company confirmed that it had engaged with staff at the Enniskillen location, who have been offered what BT describes as a voluntary paid leavers package. BT again stressed that no decision has yet been taken on the future of the site, which is pending the outcome of the review. Following on from the meeting, I wrote to the Chief Executive of the BT Group on the 28 February 2024, to emphasise the importance of regional balance and to urge the business to retain the employment in the Enniskillen area. My officials at Invest NI are continuing to engage with the business to influence the best possible outcome for the staff concerned.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the implementation of the Review of General Surgery in Northern Ireland, published in June 2022.
answer_text: I published the Review of General Surgery in June 2022 with the aim to develop a model for general surgery that is equipped to deliver equitable, sustainable, and high-quality care in a modern healthcare system. Despite the many pressures currently facing the health service, significant progress has been made on implementing the actions.The Review sets out clinically derived evidence-based standards for emergency and elective general surgery. Implementation of the standards will ensure that people across Northern Ireland who require general surgery receive the highest quality care in the most appropriate environment. My Department is working alongside Trusts as they develop plans to implement the standards.Other key actions include the establishment of Elective Overnight Stay Centres (EOSCs). These are centres for intermediate complexity surgery that may require an overnight stay in hospital.  The Mater Hospital, Daisy Hill Hospital and South West Acute Hospital have been announced as EOSCs with elective overnight surgery taking place in all hospitals.Another action successfully delivered is the establishment of Post Anaesthetic Care Units (PACUs). PACUs will protect critical care capacity and reduce ‘last minute’ cancellation of complex inpatient surgery.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department is considering providing further support to local councils to administer grant schemes for shop owners to upgrade their shop fronts.
answer_text: My officials work closely with local Councils to develop urban regeneration projects including grant schemes for shop fronts through the Revitalisation programme.  My Department will consider providing further support to local councils to administer grant schemes for shop fronts, however, it will be subject to the prioritisation of regeneration projects evidenced by need and availability of funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what support is provided by his Department for those who wish to pursue a career as a classroom assistant.
answer_text: Both Further Education Colleges and Higher Education Institutions deliver training to support people in pursuing a career as a classroom assistant. Stranmillis University College, in partnership with Belfast City Council, has also developed the innovative Up-Skilling Classroom Assistant Academy.		Financial support is available for eligible learners undertaking Further and Higher Education courses and advice is available from the relevant institution’s student support teams.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department will implement a cap on the cost of care home payments for residents, similar as to what has been introduced in England.
answer_text: The cap of £86,000 payable by a care home resident for their personal care over their lifetime, applies to proposals to change the social care charging rules in England outlined by the Department of Health and Social Care  (DHSC) in 2021. Any changes made to the social care charging rules in England do not automatically apply to Northern Ireland. There are no plans to change the current charging arrangements in Northern Ireland at present, pending the outcome of a review of charging approaches. The review will make recommendations for future charging arrangements including any proposed changes to cap and floor thresholds.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what action he is considering to support farmers who are struggling financially due to the current cost of living crisis pressures.
answer_text: In 2023 direct payments continued to be paid earlier than normal and with a 2.32% increase to the Basic Payment component.  My Department continues to bring forward new policies and programmes designed to help farm businesses improve productivity, resilience and environmental sustainability. CAFRE continues to offer relevant training and support also.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether there are plans to introduce a regional approach, inclusive of all Health and Social Care Trusts, to provide universal programmes aimed at those patients identified medically as at risk of cardiovascular disease but have not suffered an event such as a cardiac arrest.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Cardiac Network, whose role is to identify and address key priorities for the prevention, diagnosis, treatment and long-term management of cardiac disease, has developed a three-year work plan, which is currently being progressed.Currently there are lipid clinics in each Health and Social Care Trust which deal with managing the risk posed by genetic lipid disorders, as well as difficult to treat cases, and are also involved in secondary prevention.Secondary prevention rehabilitation programmes are in place for ischaemic heart disease, post myocardial infarction and cardiac surgery/percutaneous coronary intervention. Cardiac rehabilitation is also a priority for left ventricular systolic dysfunction, which is an area of priority and focus for the Cardiac Network.My Department will continue to work closely with the Cardiac Network and other regional networks such as stroke and diabetes to improve outcomes for those at risk from cardiovascular disease.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) whether his Department plans to review the current prices of school uniforms; and (ii) whether he will consider legislative changes surrounding the prices charged for school uniforms.
answer_text: The cost of school uniforms is something that I am keen to address. My officials have been carrying out significant work to understand the issue, including a pre-consultation exercise, engaging with a wide range of stakeholders.Schools governing bodies are responsible for setting their school uniform policies.  My Department has issued guidance which references that in setting their uniform policies, schools should ensure they represent value for money for families. It also recommends that schools enable uniform costs to be as low as possible through minimising branded items and ensuring that, as far as possible, items are available from a number of retailers. It is important to recognise that many schools have followed this guidance in ensuring that the cost of their school uniform is as low as possible, however there are other schools who have not. (ii) I plan to take action to address this issue and a public consultation, launching in late spring, will gather stakeholder views particularly around cost and affordability and the use of branded items and sole supplier arrangements. This will help to inform my decision on the way forward with regards to strengthening my Department’s guidance, including consideration of legislating to put this guidance on a statutory basis.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of temporary social housing properties in Fermanagh and South Tyrone.
answer_text: Figures in relation to the number of temporary social housing properties is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.  You should therefore direct your question to the Housing Executive’s dedicated MLA response team, which can be contacted at public.affairsunit@nihe.gov.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what action his Department is taking to secure the provision of out-of-hours doctors in the Western Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Western Urgent Care (WUC) is commissioned to provide the GP Out of Hours (OOHs) service across the Western area.  The GP OOHs service is for people who need urgent medical treatment but cannot wait until their doctor's practice reopens. The local population is encouraged to utilise the service appropriately to meet any urgent health care needs which cannot wait to be seen by their registered practice when it reopens.  The GP OOHs service is available Mon-Fri from 6pm to 8am the next morning, as well as 24 hours at weekends and during public holidays.  All Patients can contact WUC via a single telephone number, there are no telephone numbers available to contact bases directly.  Patients who contact the service will be triaged by a telephone or video call by clinical staff who could be located in any one of the service’s bases in Derry/ Londonderry, Enniskillen, Limavady, Strabane or Omagh.  Many patients can be safely and effectively clinically triaged by either a Nurse Advisor, Prescribing Pharmacist, an Advanced Nurse Practitioners (ANPs) or GP.  The majority of patients who contact WUC OOHs service do not need to be seen face-to-face.  From Apr-Dec 2023, 71% of WUC OOHs patient contacts were given advice by telephone or video call, 25% of patient contacts required a base visit and 4% of contacts required a home visit. The GP OOH service provided by WUC faces considerable challenges in consistently populating the GP rota, despite best efforts.  Reasons for this include the fact that GPs are not contractually obliged to work in GP OOH; shifting attitudes in relation to achieving work life balance and preparedness or willingness of doctors to cover out of hours’ sessions; and busier and more complex day time general practice reducing the inclination or ability of GPs to work additional out of hours sessions.  When GPs book to work in out of hours clinics, the majority prefer to work within their local area - close to their own practices and homes.  The majority of clinical staff are retained by WUC on an ‘as and when required’ basis and for the reasons outlined above DoH does encourage the development of a multidisciplinary workforce.  Provision of a multidisciplinary workforce allows WUC to meet patient needs and make effective use of the available clinical resource.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what actions are being taken to increase capacity for learning disability residential placements for children in Fermanagh and South Tyrone.
answer_text: The Western Health and Social Care Trust provide services to the Fermanagh and South Tyrone area, and have provided direct information to the Department in respect of the current circumstances for learning disability residential placements for children in Fermanagh and South Tyrone.The Western Health and Social Care Trust is currently commissioned to provide bespoke residential services for one young person with learning disabilities in Heather House residential facility, which is located in the Omagh area. The Trust is also commissioned to provide short break services in Avalon House to children with disabilities in the Omagh area.Avalon House Short Break services are currently paused, due to the temporary placement of three children who require medium/long term residential care, however, the Trust are working towards reinstatement of this short break service within the next few months.There are no residential or short break services commissioned in the Fermanagh area. The Trust continues to work closely with Commissioners to highlight this area of unmet need.With regard to planning for the future, a Task and Finish group has been established by the Department to finalise the Adult Learning Disability Service Model and the Children with Disabilities Framework.  Further the Department has established a Children’s Services Reform Process to reform Children’s Services. This reform process includes Children with a Disability. One of the workstreams from this process will be looking specifically at the regional position in respect of residential care, and this will include a focus on demand and capacity. The outcomes of this work will inform future commissioning across the HSC Trusts.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what provisions have been made to ensure surgery is available for those patients requiring vaginal mesh removal.
answer_text: The NI Mesh Centre at the Belfast City Hospital was established as part of the response to the work of the NI Vaginal Mesh Review that commenced in September 2017. The establishment and work of this centre was seen as an essential component of the response to handling vaginal mesh complications, where reports and lobbying had indicated that local women were not receiving appropriate care in this area. In terms of the treatment, surgeons at the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust, who provide the specialist clinic for mesh complications, continue to collaborate with other surgeons in the UK, developing specialist skills and expertise in the treatment of mesh complications. In July 2022, surgeons undertook a skills exchange weekend with a specialist from Bristol with regard to full mesh removals requiring groin surgery.  Local surgeons at the mesh clinic are now fully trained in this specialist surgery and one theatre session a month (typically 2/3 patients, depending on the level of surgery required) has now been established.  The Belfast Trust also delivers pain/psychology clinics as part of the mesh clinic service, which are the first of their kind in the UK and have received positive feedback from services users and patient representatives.The surgical team is satisfied with how the surgical skill level has developed and of the Belfast Trust’s ability to offer complete mesh removal surgery for both retropubic and transobturator meshes. Informal feedback is positive, with women indicating good levels of satisfaction with the service.In September 2023, my Department confirmed that a recurrent allocation of £530,000 was now being made available to support the provision of a vaginal mesh service in the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust.   This commissioned service is fully funded to deliver: 3 regional multispecialty multidisciplinary outpatient clinics per month. 2 additional regional scan clinics per month (4 patients per clinic); 2 additional regional multidisciplinary pain management clinics per month (2 patients per clinic); 1 additional multispecialty inpatient full day operating list (2-3 patients per list) per month and 108 bed days per year; 2 regional virtual multispecialty multidisciplinary meetings with other Trusts per month

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether there are plans to introduce the Carer's Leave Act in Northern Ireland, to enable carers to avail of 5 days paid leave.
answer_text: The Carer’s Leave Act provides powers for the Secretary of State to introduce regulations to create an entitlement in Britain to provide one-week unpaid leave for eligible employees.Following a co-design and consultation process, it is my intention to bring forward an employment rights Bill within the current Assembly mandate to ensure the employment law framework remains fit for purpose and operates effectively for both businesses and workers. Carer’s Leave is an issue I will be happy to consider in that context.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will retain free bus and rail travel for those of pension age.
answer_text: The 12-week public consultation on changes to the NI Concessionary Fares Scheme closed on 24th August 2023, with the Department receiving over 20,000 responses. The consultation included two options which could affect those of pensionable age: Limiting the use of the SmartPass to bus only; and Limiting the use of the SmartPass to off-peak times only. The consultation also included an option which would remove free travel for those aged 60-64 years of age, however Senior (65+) SmartPass users (i.e, those of pension age) would be unaffected by the implementation of this change.In the coming weeks I will be considering the responses the Department has received to the consultation and, I will do all I can to protect the services and concessions which matter most to people.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what actions are being taken to increase capacity for nursing home residential placements in Fermanagh and South Tyrone.
answer_text: There are 468 registered Care Homes in Northern Ireland, providing a total of 15,836 beds. Nursing Home beds make up approximately two thirds of these beds, with one third being within Residential Care Homes. Independent Sector Care Homes account for 93% of the total Care Home beds registered in Northern Ireland.The constituency of Fermanagh and South Tyrone is covered by the Western Health and Social Care Trust and the Southern Health and Social Care Trust. The Western and Southern Trust areas have fewer registered care home beds, in comparison to the other three Trusts. The Western Health and Social Care Trust have 31 nursing homes and 36 residential care homes, with a total of 2079 care home beds. The Southern Health and Social Care Trust has 48 nursing homes and 60 residential care homes, with a total of 2568 beds. (Census of Bed Availability in Registered Care Homes in Northern Ireland on 27 September 2023 (Dec 2023)).Southern Health and Social Care Trust representatives have advised that the majority of beds in their nursing and residential care homes are provided by the independent sector. The Southern Trust advises that there are currently no plans to increase bed provision within the Statutory Sector. They have advised however that if the Independent Sector were to develop a proposal to increase bed capacity the Trust would engage with them and consider the proposal in line with contractual requirements and tariffs. Western Health and Social Care Trust representatives have advised that the majority of beds in their nursing and residential care homes are provided by the Independent Sector. In order to explore increasing independent care home bed capacity the Western Trust published market engagement notices on “Find a Tender” in May 2023. These market engagement notices were conducted to explore increasing bed stock, particularly dementia care beds. As a result, two independent care home providers have come forward expressing interest in increasing bed capacity. The Western Trust have advised should other providers within the Independent Sector wish to develop a proposal to increase bed capacity the Trust would engage with them and consider the proposal in line with contractual requirements and tariff. The Department of Health acknowledges the need to increase bed availability and to make better use of existing vacant capacity across the region. The Social Care Collaborative Forum is taking forward work to progress a regional digital solution for Care Home bed capacity and availability. It is hoped that this digital solution will improve communication regarding location of available beds and provide an approach for data gathering that supports better identification, individual care planning and provision of services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what engagement he has had with the Community Pharmacy sector to help understand the urgent pressures within the system.
answer_text: I am acutely aware of the pressures on health and social care and recognise the challenges facing community pharmacists. Since returning to the post, I have received numerous correspondence from community pharmacies highlighting pressures, including cash flow and funding issues.An urgent meeting was held this week to meet with representatives of the community pharmacy sector, CPNI to consider these matters further.  I have asked my Department to address some of the pressures that pharmacies are facing within what is available and affordable. I encouraged Community Pharmacy NI (CPNI) to continue to work with Department officials to seek ways to ensure the pharmacy service can develop in line with our shared strategic objectives.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs when cattle movements will be reinstated between Northern Ireland and Great Britain following the outbreak of bluetongue.
answer_text: Movements of ruminants from Great Britain to Northern Ireland can recommence when Great Britain recovers disease free status for Bluetongue. The minimum period required before disease free status can be granted is two years from the date of the last positive case.		Movements may be permitted sooner should regionalisation for Bluetongue be pursued by the Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs and the devolved administrations with approval granted by the European Union. This would then permit the movement of ruminants from areas outside control zones.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department plans to take any measures to reduce the sale of vapes.
answer_text: Whilst vaping may have a role to play in smoking cessation, I share the growing concerns about escalating recreational use of vaping devices by young people. Departmental officials have been working closely with colleagues in GB to ensure NI inclusion in the recent consultation on smoking and vaping. The consultation showed overwhelming support from NI for measures such as restrictions on retail displays and restrictions on flavours and packaging. As a result, I have expressed my support for NI inclusion in the planned UK Government Bill which aims to reduce the appeal of these devices to young people.Furthermore, a new Tobacco Control Strategy for Northern Ireland is in the early stages of development and this will also set strategic direction in relation to e-cigarettes and other emerging nicotine products.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in relation to community pharmacies, whether he has any plans to review and implement an uplift in drug tariffs.
answer_text: My Department undertook a consultation seeking to improve the Drug Tariff arrangements in April 2022 and work is currently in train to take forward improvements.In light of representations from the sector, an urgent meeting was held this week with representatives of Community Pharmacy NI (CPNI) and part of that agenda was the consideration of further changes to the Drug Tariff.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what discussions have taken place to reintroduce the Cross-Border Healthcare Reimbursement Scheme.
answer_text: Reducing waiting times for elective access is a key priority for me. Current waiting times are unacceptable, no one should have to wait longer than necessary for the surgery they need.  While good progress has been made in tackling waiting times in some areas, there remains much more to be done. Within the constraints of existing budgets, I’m committed to taking whatever actions I can to tackle these waiting times and ensure that patients are treated as quickly as possible to ensure best possible outcomes.As you will be aware, I introduced the Republic of Ireland Reimbursement Scheme (RoIRS) on 30 June 2021 as part of the Elective Care Framework to help reduce NI waiting lists. The scheme was a limited 12-month administrative version of the Cross-Border Healthcare Directive (CBHD) which ceased to apply to NI following the UK’s exit from the EU. In June 2022, I announced that the RoIRS would be extended beyond the planned 30 June 2022 deadline, until an additional £5m of new funding had been committed. The RoIRS closed to new applications on Wednesday 21 September 2022 when the additional £5m funding had been exhausted. Given the pressing demands for funding across many areas of health and social care no further funding could be assigned to this scheme.Any future funding allocation to a similar scheme to help reduce waiting lists will now need to be considered as part of a broader review of resource investment in Waiting List Initiatives.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department plans to reintroduce the Village Catalyst Scheme.
answer_text: The Village Catalyst Grant Scheme is still open for applications. It is a partnership between my department, DAERA, the Architectural Heritage Fund and NIHE, aimed at supporting village communities to find sustainable uses for heritage at risk that tackle rural poverty and social isolation. You can access an application form and guidance from my Department’s website at: Village Catalyst Grant Scheme | Department for Communities (communities-ni.gov.uk)

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether his Department plans to bring forward any grant funding for homeowners to make their homes more energy efficient.
answer_text: My department is currently preparing a consultation on evidence-based options for a domestic energy efficiency programme in line with Action 4 of the Energy Strategy Action Plan for 2023. The outcome of this consultation will help to inform options regarding the design, rules, and guidance of future support.  In the interim, the department is working with partners such as the Utility Regulator to assess the options for further scaling of up existing energy efficiency programmes.  Energy Strategy - Path to Net Zero Energy - 2023 Action Plan Report (economy-ni.gov.uk)

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice what discussions her Department has had regarding an improved pay award for prison officers.
answer_text: The Department of Justice Permanent Secretary issued an activation letter to the Prison Service Pay Review Body in April 2023 asking that they make recommendations on the pay of operational prison grades. Following written and oral evidence from NIPS Management and recognised trade unions, a report was provided by the Prison Service Pay Review Body to the Department on 31 January.  A pay remit is currently being prepared for consideration and approval by the Department of Finance.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance what discussions her Department has had in relation to the introduction of banking hubs to rural areas.
answer_text: ANSWERI am deeply concerned about the impact of bank branch closures on local communities here, particularly in our rural towns and villages, and for those customers that rely more heavily on in-person banking.I have therefore written to the main local banks operating here, calling for a roundtable meeting to discuss how access to vital banking services can be maintained. At that meeting, I will be raising the need for increasing, at pace, the roll out of banking hubs.Separately I am meeting with the Financial Services Union to discuss the direction of the banking industry locally.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment on the location of the imaging academy; and (ii) whether he will assess if similar could be implemented in the Western Health and Social Care Trust.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department has considered the introduction of new technology to fix potholes.
answer_text: My Department works closely with the construction industry to monitor new technology and innovations in respect of pothole repairs.The Department keeps informed about new approaches to road maintenance through its engagement with other road authorities, Professional Engineering Institutions, universities and the wider construction and infrastructure industry. We are very open to adopting new approaches and innovative materials that offer an improvement to our current approach to road defect repair and are actively exploring how emerging technology can be used to better maintain the road network.     My Department will continue to liaise with contractors and suppliers to identify any opportunities in the future that could improve the repair of road defects.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of (i) individuals; and (ii) households currently living in temporary housing in the Fermanagh and South Tyrone constituency.
answer_text: Figures in relation to individuals and households living in temporary accommodation is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.  You should therefore direct your question to the Housing Executive’s dedicated MLA response team, which can be contacted at public.affairsunit@nihe.gov.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what discussions his Department has had with the UK Government regarding the implementation of the Project Gigabit scheme in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Following guidance under the Gigabit Infrastructure Subsidy Scheme, my Department, in conjunction with Building Digital UK (BDUK), an executive agency of the Department for Science, Innovation and Technology, carried out an Open Market Review to establish existing and planned coverage of broadband services locally, seeking responses from existing and prospective broadband infrastructure providers. My Department and BDUK also carried out a Public Review to validate the results of the Open Market Review. An outcome report of the Public Review was published in February 2024. My Department has also been engaging with BDUK on the development of documentation for an anticipated procurement under Project Gigabit, and has begun Pre-Procurement Market Engagement. My officials continue to develop a business case and BDUK has confirmed that it will make available any necessary capital funding for Project Gigabit here, subject to the approval of a final business case, State aid / subsidy control assurance and value for money considerations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what plans his Department is putting in place to increase the provision of domiciliary care packages in (i) the Western Health and Social Care Trust; and (ii) the Southern Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Domiciliary care services continue to experience significant pressures; however, my Department remains committed to the provision of a high-quality domiciliary care service. It is clear that the current system is not sustainable, too many people are waiting for all, or part of a care package. There are a range of factors which can impact upon delivery of this service; this includes retention of domiciliary care staff. This significantly impacts on the Health Trusts and the Independent sector’s ability to increase capacity in the context of a competitive wider recruitment market. The Western Health and Social Care Trust annual Domiciliary Care budget amounts to £46.3m this includes an additional £1.353m allocated to meet demographic growth for the last two financial years. The Trust currently deploys all of the Domiciliary Care resources allocated.  This amounts to an average of 39,000 weekly hours of care provided to 3,620 service users.  Despite the resources deployed the Western Trust is not able to meet all of the presenting demand for domiciliary care services.  At present there is unmet need amounting to 652 cases assessed as requiring 5,202 weekly hours.  All Domiciliary Care providers advise that they are encountering workforce challenges which is the predominant factor affecting the Western Trust's ability to increase domiciliary care service provision.  The Trust has embarked on a Homecare Optimisation programme as part of the Delivering Value project.  The reorganisation of domiciliary care services thus far in five  of the Trust's nine  geographical areas has created further efficiencies and has resulted in the release of 4,267 weekly hours allowing a service to be provided to an additional 459 clients.  The Homecare Optimisation programme will be rolled out to the remaining areas of the Trust in the year ahead.The Southern Health and Social Care Trust is currently providing a greater number of Domiciliary Care hours per year than it is funded to do so.  The Trust is working to improve access for service users to domiciliary care, through a number of approaches, including:Working to increase the frequency of service user review, to ensure that over and under provision of domiciliary care services is avoided.Implementation of an “Early Review” Team Pilot, as supported by my departmentContributing to the Regional work in respect of Domiciliary Care Services within the work of the Social Care Collaborative. Working towards a digital live system to manage in-house domiciliary care which will make the most efficient use of the resources allocated to the Trust for Domiciliary Care services Continuing to implement a range of approaches to recruitment, to ensure that there is steady access to new recruits to replace staff retirements.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister what support their Department is giving to the Troubles Permanent Disablement Payment Scheme in relation to (i) the speed of the process; and (ii) communications with survivors.
answer_text: As of week commencing 13 May, almost £41 million has been paid to victims since the scheme opened for applications on 31 August 2021.This is a novel scheme and each application is unique with its own complexities.  Many applications may also include multiple relevant incidents.  The Executive Office has committed to funding a range of support services to enhance the application process to ensure a victim centred approach.To date, funding of almost £5 million has been provided to support victims’ and survivors’ representative groups via the Victims and Survivors Service (VSS).  This has facilitated the recruitment of welfare officers and admin support staff to assist applicants with the application process and additional funding to provide health and wellbeing support. This support will continue in 2024/2025.These groups have been working closely with the Victims’ Payments Board and VSS on improving the process for applicants, and feedback from those discussions will be considered at the Sectoral Engagement Group, which is chaired by TEO.  		TEO also continues to provide additional resources to evidence retrieval partners to assist in the retrieval of evidence on behalf of applicants and the Victims’ Payments Board.  TEO officials also engage regularly with colleagues in the Department of Justice and the Victims’ Payments Board to consider scheme process and communications issues.  In addition, the Victims’ Payments Board are currently engaged with the Department of Finance’s Business Consultancy Service to conduct a high-level review of the scheme administration processes to help enhance the delivery of the scheme.Over two weeks from the 19 February, the Victims’ Payments Board conducted a leaflet drop to each household to raise awareness of the scheme.  The leaflets were also distributed to other relevant stakeholders, including all MLAs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) to detail the number of police officers on (a) long-term sick absence; and (b) short-term sick absence; and (iii) how many of these are a result of being injured while on duty.
answer_text: The number of police officers on either long or short-term sickness absence, and/or the relationship between sick absence and an injury on duty is an operational matter for the Chief Constable to address.I will continue to do all I can to support the Chief Constable to ensure safe and effective policing. In doing so, I remain committed to respecting his operational independence and the accountability role of the Northern Ireland Policing Board.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he will consider introducing legislation to mandate the wearing of life jackets on inland waterways.
answer_text: My Department is not responsible for the legislation relating to the use of life jackets by inland waterway users.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he has considered reopening the process for schools to apply for new school crossing patrols.
answer_text: The Education Authority has advised that the application process for new school crossing patrol sites reopened on 1 February 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many patients in (i) the Western Health and the Social Care Trust; and (ii) the Southern Health and Social Care Trust, are on waiting lists for (a) hip surgery; (b) knee surgery; and (c) shoulder surgery.
answer_text: Data on the number of patients waiting specifically for (a) hip surgery; (b) knee surgery; and (c) shoulder surgery is not available.Reducing waiting times for elective care, including orthopaedics, remains a key priority for me as Health Minister.  Within the constraints of existing budgets, I’m committed to taking whatever actions I can to tackle waiting times and ensure that patients are treated as quickly as possible to ensure best possible outcomes.  In April 2022, my Department commissioned an independent review of the regional elective orthopaedic service in Northern Ireland to support service rebuild.   My officials will continue to work closely with Trusts to monitor performance and support increased service delivery.As at 30th September 2023, there were (i) 6,309 patients awaiting inpatient or day case admission within the Trauma and Orthopaedics specialty in the Western Health and Social Care (HSC) Trust and (ii) 3,876 patients waiting in the Southern HSC Trust.Patients are reported against the HSC Trust responsible for the service providing their treatment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment on neighbourhood policing provision in the Clogher Valley area, following the withdrawal of neighbourhood policing teams from Clogher police station.
answer_text: Neighbourhood policing provision is an operational matter for the PSNI who are accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  It is therefore not appropriate for me to make an assessment on this specific matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what action he will take to ensure that maternity services have the staff capacity, skills and resources required to assess and care for women effectively.

To ask the Minister of Health whether he will support the introduction of a funded research programme to inform the development of effective interventions to address health inequalities during pregnancies.
answer_text: There is currently no budget associated with research and development for a programme to inform effective interventions to address health inequalities during pregnancy.Action to address health inequalities remains a priority for my Department and the Public Health Agency (PHA) with more work planned to focus specifically on social complexity. My Department works closely with national researchers within the MBRRACE-UK programme (Mothers and Babies: Reducing Risk through Audits and Confidential Enquiries across the UK) which is focused on improving the health and outcomes for mothers and newborns. This work has a specific focus on health inequalities and works to support women with complex needs and those of a higher disadvantage.The National Institute for Health and Social Care Excellence (NICE) is a UK based organisation that provides evidence-based recommendations for health and social care providers.  This is a nationally endorsed programme through the Chief Medical Office and Chief Nursing Office in my Department.   Processes are in place to implement NICE recommendations and guidance across Northern Ireland. My Department also work closely with Royal College of Obstetricians and Gynaecologists (RCOG) and the Royal College of Midwives (RCM) in national research. The guidance produced and promoted by RCOG and RCM is designed to help clinicians evaluate and improve their practice as well as provide guidelines and standards for maternity providers. If regionally accepted, the Maternity Collaborative will work to provide support to Trusts to implement this guidance, funding permitted. Following a coronial case in a freestanding Midwife Led Unit in Northern Ireland, Professor Mary Renfrew was tasked with undertaking a comprehensive and independent review. The independent report will draw on evidence reviews, data analysis and on consultation and engagement with users and advocates of maternity services and with the professionals who provide and lead the services, culminating in the development of recommendations for policy, practice, education, and research.  This will help inform a consistent approach to the provision of midwifery services throughout Northern Ireland (NI).UK Research and Innovation have a number of projects ongoing at the moment to tackle entrenched and longstanding inequalities in some of the poorest communities in the UK. They do this by exploring how health systems can collaborate more effectively with communities.As part of the Public Health Agency Research and Development Division and in conjunction with UK Research and Innovation, the University of Ulster has recently received funding of £1.9m to further develop a community consortium to tackle health disparity for people living with mental illness and to help address health and social care inequalities.   This may provide further evidence for perinatal mental health services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will introduce long-term, funded plans aimed at eliminating inequalities in pregnancy loss and baby deaths.
answer_text: In order to effectively manage risk and reduce avoidable harm during pregnancy, it is vital that maternity services are well led, adoptive a culture of safety and have robust governance systems in place to ensure delivery of safe, effective, person centred care. The development of a new Maternity Strategy in Northern Ireland and a Maternity Partnership to shape the planning, delivery and monitoring of HSC maternity services will help achieve this.Maternity services should aim to achieve safe birth outcomes for women and babies. The loss of a baby during pregnancy and following birth can lead to profound trauma and heartbreak for both the woman and the family. Work is under way to provide additional help and support.Improving the health and wellbeing of mothers remains a priority. Work aimed at addressing issues that carry a higher risk of loss such as smoking, substance abuse, obesity, age and other co-morbidities is ongoing within Trusts and a number of programmes are in place regionally to address this. It should be noted that many of the risk factors mentioned above  are more prevalent in higher social disadvantaged groups.Other initiatives such as the family nurse partnership and continuity of midwifery carer enable support for mothers and pregnant women to be available at local level. These evidence based programmes have delivered improvement in care and outcomes across Northern Ireland.A national care bundle called Saving Babies’ Lives (SBL) aims to prevent and reduce stillbirth rates and has been adopted in Northern Ireland.  The care bundle has five elements; smoking in pregnancy, fetal growth surveillance, reduced fetal movement, intrapartum monitoring and premature births.  The third iteration has been published and a regional group has been established to support implementation, additional funding will be required to take this forward.This work is part of an overall action plan for HSC on preventing stillbirths.  This is a culmination of a number of areas of work but was most recently prompted by learning from a coronial investigation involving a stillbirth. Funding constraints continue to be a challenge for this area of work, as well as the challenges in maintaining workforce across all units. A regional pathway for preterm birth and perinatal optimisation has also been developed across the HSC in line with best practice, however, resource will be required to fully implement this pathway. All Trusts in Northern Ireland review each stillbirth using a structured tool called the Perinatal Mortality Review Tool (PMRT).  This is a systematic, multidisciplinary review of the circumstances and care leading up to and surrounding each stillbirth which identifies learning and actions to improve future care.  A proportion of stillbirths each year will be reported as Serious Adverse Incidents (SAIs) where it has been assessed that different care may have made a difference to the outcome.  Stillbirths will also be reported internally within Trust

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will ensure all maternity safety improvement schemes include a focus on tackling inequalities, with action, progress and impact monitored.
answer_text: In light of several recent reports into the safety of services for pregnant women, new mothers and newborn/neonatal babies both locally and nationally, the Department of Health (DoH) has established a Maternity and Neonatal Services Safety Oversight Group to receive assurance on the safety of maternity and neonatal services for the Northern Ireland (NI) population.  As part of this assurance the DoH and Public Health Agency (PHA) are working with Trusts to improve monitoring and oversight of maternity services and improve the systems and pathways for the delivery of maternity care. To inform a consistent approach and to improve safety in the provision of services throughout Northern Ireland, Professor Mary Renfrew is undertaking a comprehensive and independent review of freestanding midwifery led units. The independent report will draw on evidence reviews, data analysis and on consultation and engagement with users and advocates of maternity services and with the professionals who provide and lead the services, culminating in the development of recommendations for policy, practice, education, and research. In order to effectively manage risk and reduce avoidable harm during pregnancy, it is vital that maternity services are well led, adopt a culture of safety and have robust governance systems in place to ensure delivery of safe, effective, person centered care. The development of a new Maternity Strategy in Northern Ireland and a Maternity Partnership to shape the planning, delivery and monitoring of HSC maternity services will help achieve this.My Department and the PHA have taken forward a piece of work to consider compliance against national standards and recommendations in each unit in Northern Ireland. This piece of work also included:•	Compliance against CMO and CNO endorsed standards and guidance with an Action Plan for Trusts;•	Reviewing reports developed through SAI learning reviews in relation to maternity and neonatal care were also considered as part of the workload; and•	The Regulation and Quality Improvement Authority (RQIA) Review of governance arrangements in place to support safety within maternity services in Northern Ireland.Maternity services should aim to achieve safe birth outcomes for women and babies. The loss of a baby during pregnancy and following birth can lead to profound trauma and heartbreak for both the woman and the family. Work is under way to provide additional help and support in this area.Improving the health and wellbeing of mothers remains a priority. Work aimed at addressing issues that carry a higher risk of loss such as smoking, substance abuse, obesity, age and other co-morbidities is ongoing within Trusts and a number of programmes are in place regionally to address this. It should be noted that many of the risk factors mentioned above  are more prevalent in higher social disadvantaged groups.Other initiatives such as the Family Nurse Partnership and Continuity of Midwifery Carer (CoMC) enable support

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the turnaround time for medical assessments when applying for a (i) driving licence; and (ii) HGV licence.
answer_text: The Driver & Vehicle Agency (DVA) is required to be satisfied that driving licences are issued only to those drivers who meet the relevant standards in relation to medical fitness to drive.The time taken to determine an application where medical assessment is required will depend on the nature of the medical condition and whether any further information or medical examination is required as part of the assessment process.  In February 2023, DVA awarded a new contract for the provision of professional medical services.  The contract requires each stage of the medical assessment to be completed within 14 days.  It is important to note that after the initial assessment of a case the medical advisor may require further information from the driver’s GP or determine that a specialist medical assessment is required.  Therefore, some applications will take longer to process than others.  A driver can apply for renewal of their car or motorbike licence two months in advance of their licence expiry and for a lorry or bus licence, the application for renewal can be submitted three months in advance of licence expiry.In most cases, a driver is permitted to drive pending medical assessment, where certain conditions are met; those conditions include that the DVA is in receipt of a qualifying application, that the driver was not previously refused a licence on medical grounds, that this is not the first application following disqualification and that they have not been advised by a medical practitioner that they should not drive.  This cover exists even where the current licence has expired and remains in place for a period of one year from the date of receipt of the qualifying application.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many hospital patients in the Western Health and Social Care Trust are awaiting a care package prior to being discharged.
To ask the Minister of Health what action he is taking to support and encourage the recruitment and retention of GPs in rural practices.
answer_text: I am committed to building the GP workforce across Northern Ireland and recognise pressures in rural practices.  Against the background of a challenging financial climate, my Department continues its focus on building the workforce this includes funding the Attract, Recruit and Retain scheme, the GP Retainer Scheme and the GP Induction and Refresher Scheme.The Attract, Recruit and Retain Scheme promotes recruitment in hard to recruit areas. It aims to support practices in attracting and recruiting new GPs as well as retaining those who join their team.  GP practices can apply for a number of packages under the Scheme including a Golden Hello, relocation costs, recruitment costs, management costs, reimbursement Costs for applying to the NI Performers List and funding for international GPs.The Scheme was launched on 4th January 2023 and, as at the end of December 2023, 41 GP Practices have applied to the Scheme and received approval in principle, of these 41 GP practices, 18 Practices have already recruited new GPs.The annual number of GP training places has been increased  from 65 in 2015/16 to the current level of 121 (an increase of 86%) and steps have also been taken to streamline the recruitment processes for GPs who qualified in the ROI, Australia, Canada, New Zealand and South Africa.My Department will be working with the NI Medical and Dental Training Agency (NIMDTA) to undertake a review of the GP training programme to consider innovations which will enhance the recruitment and retention of trainees in Northern Ireland.The Ulster University’s School of Medicine at Magee Graduate Entry Programme places a very significant emphasis on primary care placements, with a high concentration of clinical placements in rural settings in the west of Northern Ireland.  It is hoped that this will help ensure a supply of local students who wish to pursue a career in General Practice in these areas.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what discussions he and his Department are having with BT, following the announcement of the planned closure of its call centre in Enniskillen.
answer_text: I answered this question as an urgent oral question that you tabled on Monday 12 February 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on plans for the old Devenish College site, Enniskillen.
answer_text: The vacated Devenish College site is to be used by Enniskillen Royal Grammar School who will decant to the site during the construction phase of their major capital project.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department is considering introducing a standardised approach across all GP surgeries in relation to appointment booking systems.
answer_text: GPs are independent contractors who contract with the Department of Health to provide primary medical services to their registered list of patients under the terms of the General Medical Services Contracts Regulations.  As independent contractors, GPs are responsible for the day to day running of their practice, including how consultations for individual patients are delivered and how appointments are requested/booked.  I recognise the importance of access to GP services for patients.  My Department has established a GP Access Working Group to explore issues around accessing services and to oversee work to improve the experience for patients.  Work is ongoing to develop guidance for practices in the management of patient demand and optimising workflow within practices to maximise efficiency.  The Group is also exploring how telephony and related technologies can be best deployed to improve experience for patients and practices alike.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what action his Department is taking following the discovery of blue-green algae at Mill Lough, Bellanaleck, County Fermanagh.
answer_text: Northern Ireland Environment Agency (NIEA) staff confirmed the presence of blue-green algae at Mill Lough on 15 January 2024. Staff from NIEA visited the lough on 18 January 2024 and 8 February 2024 and confirmed that algae was still present. NIEA have informed Fermanagh and Omagh District Council who plan to erect signage. NIEA will continue to monitor the lough for the presence of algae and report findings through to the council.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether a budget has been set aside to fund resurfacing works on (i) C roads; and (ii) unclassified roads, which his Department class as low priority.
answer_text: My Department has a statutory duty to maintain all public roads in a safe and serviceable condition and an annual programme of resurfacing works is undertaken, the extent of which is dictated by the availability of finance.In the absence of a fully funded allocation for the structural maintenance programme, my Department aims to prioritise work on the basis of the indicators including the road usage, general surface condition surveys, information from maintenance inspections, public enquiries and public liability claims as well as on the professional engineering judgements of our local engineers. Those schemes not included in a particular year’s programme as result of funding constraints will routinely be considered in future years’ programmes.For a number of years now my Department has also allocated budget to a Roads Recovery Fund, which is primarily targeted towards the lower tier network. These improvements are targeting many short lengths of mainly rural roads that are in particularly poor condition. I have also recently announced the immediate investment of an additional £1m in road maintenance to address the growing backlog of pothole repairs. This much needed funding will be used between now and the end of the financial year to target areas of particular need with small scale resurfacing schemes designed to repair areas that have suffered the most and where localised repairs are less effective.The level of resurfacing that can be carried out in future financial years will depend on the 2024-25 final budget determination for my Department. I hope that critical services and infrastructure such as our roads are provided with the investment we need and I will continue to seek the support of Executive colleagues to enable this to happen.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he will commit to working with stakeholders, including those with science, veterinary and environmental experience, when developing policies and delivery programmes.
answer_text: My department is leading the development of Northern Ireland’s first Climate Action Plan, in collaboration with all departments. This Plan will set out Northern Ireland’s approach to delivering the emissions reductions required to achieve our first carbon budget. I intend to seek agreement from Executive colleagues for Northern Ireland’s first three carbon budgets. I will then bring forward draft regulations to the Assembly for its consideration and approval.I am fully committed to delivering a Climate Action Plan that is transformational, evidence-based and informed by stakeholders. The draft Climate Action Plan will need to be agreed by the Executive. The timeframes involved mean that it will not be possible to meet the dates for final publication as set out in the Climate Change Act. However I am determined that we will publish the draft for consultation as soon as possible.The Climate Action Plan will be considered by the DAERA Assembly Committee, and other Committees may also wish to scrutinise their departments’ contributions.As the member will be aware, budgets for the next financial year have not yet been confirmed, however the cost of implementing the Climate Change Act is being considered as part of the ongoing budget exercise for next year.  Work is ongoing to scope and refine these costs. Some of these will fall to DAERA but other departments will bear costs too. Normal budgeting processes will apply and it is also important that the costs and opportunities of addressing climate change are embedded within the economic appraisal processes. Finally, I want to emphasise that these up front costs represent investments in an improved society for future generations and that the cost of delaying action will ultimately be much greater. We must act now and we must act decisively.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice what provisions have been made to support policing teams in Fermanagh following the (i) permanent closure of the custody suite in Enniskillen PSNI Station; and (ii) temporary closure of the Omagh custody suite.
answer_text: Decisions relating to the support of policing teams as a result of the closure of Enniskillen custody suite and the temporary closure of Omagh custody suite are operational matters for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Northern Ireland Policing Board. You may, therefore, wish to direct your question to the PSNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what actions are being taken to increase capacity for respite placements for adults with a learning disability in Fermanagh and South Tyrone.
answer_text: The Western Health and Social Care Trust (WHSCT) provides short breaks via multiple arrangements in line with strategic direction and includes beds and building-based provision in both Trust and Private/Independent Sector provision.  In addition, the use of Direct Payments under Self-Directed Support, enables carers to determine more bespoke arrangements deemed appropriate to their particular needs.Across all Trusts, short breaks provision has been identified as an area of increasing need with demand extending beyond the existing capacity within relevant facilities.  These pressures have been raised with the Department’s Strategic Planning and Performance Group as one of a number of commissioning priorities within the Learning Disability programme of care.  Throughout 2023, the Department has led on an exercise to finalise a draft service model for learning disability, which includes several outcomes and actions to expand day services and short breaks in Northern Ireland.  The current draft has been shared with key stakeholders to inform the next phase of this work and wider consultation.  In parallel to this work, the Department’s Strategic Planning and Performance Group are working closely with Trusts to undertake a financial review of adult learning disability services.  The outputs of this work will inform decisions on the service model and long-term planning for learning disability services.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what plans he has to address the unavailability of MOT appointments for applicants at their local test centre.
answer_text: In 2022-2023, the Driver & Vehicle Agency (DVA) conducted over 1.15 million vehicle tests which is the highest number ever recorded in a year. This increase in capacity has been achieved by adopting a range of measures including the recruitment of additional vehicle examiners, the use overtime to provide cover for leave and offering vehicle test appointments on Sundays and bank holidays, when testing is not normally available.  Disappointingly, the number of vehicles that failed to attend a booked appointment in 2022-2023 was also the highest on record at 50,772.A further vehicle examiner recruitment competition will launch on 19 February 2024 to fill staff vacancies across the DVA’s network of test centres.  The DVA appreciates that, in some cases, customers may not be able to have their vehicles tested before their current MOT certificate expires and has taken steps to ensure that motorists are still able to use their vehicles when they are unable to secure an MOT appointment before their current MOT expires.  The latest information on MOTs is available on NI Direct at https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/articles/guide-booking-your-mot-vehicle-test and is also included as an advice note with every MOT reminder letter issued to customers.Vehicle test appointments are released on a daily basis, when resources become available, so customers are encouraged to regularly check the booking system as they may be able to change their existing appointment to a different test centre and time if a slot becomes available.  Information on how a customer can change, cancel or view their MOT appointment online is available on NI Direct at https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/services/change-cancel-or-view-your-mot-test-appointment-online.To further increase vehicle testing capacity, the DVA is investing in new test centres.  The construction of a new test centre at Hydebank, in Belfast, is nearing completion and will open later this year.  The construction contract for a second new test centre at Mallusk has also been awarded and this new test centre is expected to open in 2025.  Once fully operational, these two new centres will provide the capacity to test over 200,000 additional vehicles per annum, which will help the DVA meet the future demand for this service.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the new school building for Enniskillen Royal Grammar School.
answer_text: Enniskillen Royal Grammar School was announced to advance in planning under the Major Capital Works Programme in January 2013.The final technical design and pre-tender estimate work for the new school building is currently being undertaken by the appointed Integrated Consultant Team.  This work is anticipated to complete in July 2024.A planning application for the new build project was submitted in March 2023, however no decision on the application has been taken at this time.  For this project to progress beyond the current design stage additional capital funding, for which I will be bidding, will be required to enable progression of this and other capital projects which are currently at a stand still.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of in-patients unable to be discharged from hospital due to a care package not being in place in the (i) Western Health and Social Care Trust; and (i) Southern Health and Social Care Trust, in each of the last two years.
To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the roll-out of Project Stratum in Fermanagh and South Tyrone.
answer_text: A total of 12,842 premises in the Fermanagh and South Tyrone Constituency will benefit from funding allocated under Project Stratum. To date, 12,183 premises have been addressed, and now have access to a gigabit-capable broadband service. The remaining 659 will be addressed by June 2025. Further details will be provided at https://hyperfastni.com/.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the Brookeborough Shared School Campus.
answer_text: Work is ongoing to finalise the business case for the project.  Until the business case is approved and a preferred option is identified, it is not possible to provide an accurate timeline for the works, which will be dependent on various factors, including achieving planning permission and acquiring any necessary land.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) what priority his Department is giving to plans for the new Enniskillen Library; and (ii) to provide a timeline for delivery.
answer_text: A new public library facility at Enniskillen remains a priority for my Department and Libraries NI.  A timeline for delivery cannot be provided at this stage as a decision on whether the project can be progressed must take account of the Department’s capital budget position for 2024/25.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on a proposed pathway for adult patients to receive diagnosis of ADHD and access to ADHD services.
answer_text: Attention deficit hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) provision for adults has grown organically across Health and Social Care Trusts in response to population demand and expertise within HSC teams. While this has meant a level of provision is available, it is recognised that this has also led to a gap between what can be provided by Trusts in terms of demand and the level of prevalence of ADHD, leading to individuals waiting for assessment and support.The Health and Social Care Trusts are committed to working closely and collaboratively with commissioners to develop innovative, cost effective, co-produced, quality, and evidenced based services that can meet the needs of adults with ADHD within Trust areas. This work would include the development of clinical pathways.DoH Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG), alongside policy colleagues in the Department, are keeping the situation regarding the commissioning of Adult ADHD services under review, recognising that the demand for ADHD diagnosis has risen in recent years.  In line with the usual process for commissioning of services, any decision to commission Adult ADHD services in the future will be based on an assessment of the level of demand for services, and in the context of future budget availability.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he has considered the inclusion of Devenish College, Enniskillen, in the 20 mph speed limit scheme for schools.
answer_text: Part-time 20mph limits near schools are implemented based on assessments considering collision history, speed, traffic volume, infrastructure, and site constraints.  Schools have been assessed in accordance with DfI’s policy ‘Road Safety at Schools’ which can be viewed at: https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/road-safety-schools-rsppg-e070The program to date has been rolled out in tranches, with the most recent completed in November 2022.  Over the coming weeks, I will be considering my priorities, including the potential for a further tranche of the 20mph schools programme, in the context of the funding that is currently available to my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the proposed introduction in 2019 of bariatric surgery in the South West Acute Hospital.

To ask the Minister for the Economy whether he will review the financial support available to students in Northern Ireland, including increasing the threshold for student loans in light of the cost of living crisis.
answer_text: Prior to my taking office, the higher education student support package for academic year 2024/25 had already been determined. I am, however, acutely aware of the impact that cost of living increases are having on students and I will be engaging with my officials to consider the support available in future years.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for an update on the A4 Southern Bypass scheme; and (ii) to outline the timescales for the project going forward.
answer_text: Major Road schemes are an important part of the work that is delivered by my Department as we seek to reduce journey times, increase reliability and improve road safety.In August 2022, I committed to progress the development of contract documents for this strategically important scheme in preparation for future procurement. This work is now at an advanced stage of completion, and I am in discussion with my executive colleagues to establish funding to allow the procurement process to commence for the construction of the project.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether the decision to temporarily suspend emergency general surgery at the South West Acute Hospital is in line with the Review of General Surgery which he brought to the Assembly in June 2022; and for his assessment of this review.
answer_text: The decision to temporarily suspend emergency general surgery at SWAH was taken by the WHSCT on the grounds of patient safety due to inability to sustain the consultant rota required. Any decisions on a permanent change will be taken in line with Guidance on Change and Withdrawal of Services and will be subject to approval by me as Minister.The NI Review of General Surgery, published in June 2022, sets out the fragility of the current configuration of general surgery services and contains evidence-based standards for the delivery of elective and emergency general surgery.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to provide a list of housing developments which are still to be adopted in Northern Ireland, broken down by Council area.
answer_text: A list of unadopted roads for which bonds exist, arranged by Council areas, has been compiled and reflects developments progressing through the various stages of construction.  This list will be placed in the Assembly Library for ease of reference.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to confirm that there are no threats to maternity services at the South West Acute Hospital following the publication of the Getting it Right First Time report.
answer_text: Following the publication of the Getting it Right First Time (GIRFT) report  on gynaecology services and the reference to maternity South West Acute Hospital Maternity Services, I would like to emphasise the following:•	My Department agree that gynaecology waiting lists are unacceptably high.•	I would like to reassure the public that the Western Health and Social Care Trust has advised that there are no threats to Maternity Services at South West Acute Hospital and they remain entirely committed to full maternity service provision at the Hospital.•	As part of that commitment to first class maternity care to mums and babies, Western Health and Social Care Trust have further strengthened both their medical and midwifery permanent workforce in recent months. The GIRFT report states “At Enniskillen (SWAH) there are maternity, neonatal, elective and emergency gynaecology, day case and outpatient facilities in a relatively new build with extensive distances between services.
