Although a relatively small maternity unit (1100-1200 births per year), there are no plans for reconfiguration and centralisation of maternity services in the Western Trust”. Within the recommendations from the report – “Western Trust should consider developing a gynaecology/women’s health unit on ward 43. Western Trust should promote SWAH gynaecology unit as the elective day case hub and develop ward 43 for ambulatory treatments further”.It is estimated in 2023/24 that there will be around 19,500 births across Northern Ireland, births numbers have been reducing year on year. Health and Social care Trusts need to ensure that their services are safe and sustainable and meet national standards.  South West Acute Hospital is referenced in the GIRFT report as being one of the three smallest units in Northern Ireland with 1,130 births. The Western Trust has been asked to provide assurance of the safety and sustainability of the Gynaecology and Obstetrics Services and ensure that it meets the needs of women within its catchment population.   It is SPPG’s expectation that any changes to services by Trusts will include wider engagement with the local population.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what efforts his Department is taking to prevent the discharge of untreated sewage into Lough Erne.
answer_text: Unfortunately my officials were unable to gather together the information sought by the member in the short amount of time I had remaining in office.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the efforts made by his Department and NI Water to identify and quantify all discharges of raw sewage into Lough Erne.
answer_text: Unfortunately my officials were unable to gather together the information sought by the member in the short amount of time I had remaining in office.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of whether the significant pay disparity with other UK nations is having an adverse impact on recruitment and retention of Hospital dental trainees in Northern Ireland; and (ii) what efforts are being taken to address this.

To ask the Minister of Health (i) what structures exist to take forward oral health Improvement in Northern Ireland following the standing down of the Northern Ireland Oral Health Improvement Group; and (ii) to outline what role the Public Health Agency has in relation to oral health.
answer_text: The Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) commissions a range of evidence based oral health improvement programmes from Health and Social Care Trusts, and also commissions primary dental care, including prevention, from General Dental Practitioners (GDPs).While the oral health of the NI population has lagged behind that of England, it has improved markedly over the last decade. The current Northern Ireland Oral Health Strategy (OHS) was published in 2007 and, despite its age, the main oral health problems described in the document and the approaches to prevention advocated by it remain valid today.Prior to the Covid 19 pandemic my Department decided that the two sections of the Northern Ireland population that would benefit most from updated oral disease prevention programs where children and the older population. Unfortunately, the pandemic delayed the establishment of the two planned working groups, but significant progress has been made by two oral health groups - one dedicated to improving the oral health of children and the other specifically looking at improving the oral health of older people.In addition, my Department has funded a dental health survey of five year old children this financial year, and a survey of twelve year old children is planned for 2022/23.  These surveys will provide key information for future planning of oral health improvement in Northern Ireland.The Public Health Agency (PHA) does not have direct responsibility nor funding for oral health. However, oral health promotion is integral to wider public health activities undertaken through the PHA, including SureStart programmes.  Recent PHA workstreams include Swallow Aware project work and mouth cancer awareness. Officials in my Department are currently involved in reviewing oral health related information for Healthy Child Healthy Future- the Framework for the Universal Child Health Promotion Programme in Northern Ireland and updating the oral health advice included in the Birth to Five Book which is given to every new mother having their first baby.  The PHA also promotes partnership working with local government and the voluntary and community sectors for the purpose of improving oral health.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what reassurances can he give to the dental profession in response to British Dental Association's survey findings that morale among Health Service committed dentists is at rock bottom, and that there is significant uncertainty and a crisis of confidence among dental practitioners in Health Service dentistry at this time.
answer_text: My Department acknowledges the challenging environment that dental practices have endured in recent years.  A key element of that has been uncertainty around investment and financial support. To help sustain GDS during the Covid-19 pandemic, my Department provided £76m in financial support to independent General Dental Practitioners over the last 2 years.  Further funding was committed for the first half of 2022/23 to support dental practices, with dental fees being enhanced by 25% as part of the Rebuilding Support Scheme (RSS), to reflect the ongoing pandemic pressures practices are facing. This support has been extended at 10% until the end of this financial year. Further, I am pleased to have been able to implement the DDRB recommended pay increases in each of the years I have been Minister. I have considered and accepted the recommendations made by the Review Body on Doctors' and Dentists' Remuneration (DDRB) in their 50th report that would provide a 4.5% pay uplift to General Dental Practitioners for 2022/23.  However, unlike my colleagues in other parts of the United Kingdom, I am unable to announce the immediate implementation of these pay awards locally, as Northern Ireland still does not have an agreed Executive Budget for 2022/23.  Whilst elements of the pay uplift are outwith the control of the Department of Health, particularly this year, I will do everything I can to implement as soon as possible.My Department remains committed to Health Service dentistry, and continues to engage with the dental industry via the British Dental Association (BDA).  In 2021 the Contract Reform Group was established to work with representatives from the BDA in a collaborative approach, to examine how to make improvements in the dental contract in the short and longer term. One of the key goals of any new or reformed contract will be to ensure that Health Service dentistry is sustainable and delivers value for both patients and the profession.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) what dental workforce planning is being progressed at this time; and (ii) what efforts are being made to address recruitment and retention issues caused by pay disparity, the absence of Clinical Excellence Awards, pensions mitigations and timely funding to recruit to posts, particularly within the Hospital dental services.
answer_text: (i) The Department of Health has established a strategic regional Project Steering Group to examine the workforce required to deliver dental services within Northern Ireland.  The Group’s aim is to inform the commissioning of education and training numbers of dentists and support informed decision making on future workforce requirements, workforce development and care pathways.Skills for Health have carried out extensive research and gathered input across the range of key stakeholders.  However, this work was halted due to the COVID-19 pandemic and is now somewhat out of date.  The Project Steering Group is now considering next steps, informed by that work and giving due consideration of the current key issues facing the dental workforce.(ii) My Department published the comprehensive Health and Social Care Workforce Strategy 2026: Delivering for Our People in May 2018.  The aim of the Strategy is that ‘by 2026, we meet our workforce needs and the needs of our workforce’.The Strategy’s second action plan, covering the period 2022/23 to 2024/25, was published on 15th June 2022 following detailed engagement with stakeholders from across the health and social care system.The second action plan contains a range of actions that address recruitment and retention of staff across health and social care.  These include actions to develop focussed campaigns to showcase and support recruitment into a career in health and social care including dentistry and to develop retention initiatives for specific professions including our dental workforce.Medical and Dental pay issues are a devolved matter in Northern Ireland. Medical and Dental trainees in England have been on a different contract to those in Northern Ireland since 2016. Any change to the Northern Ireland contract would be a matter for negotiation between the Department and the British Medical Association. Following mediation in August 2022, the Department is now working collaboratively with the BMA to co-design a new future Clinical Excellence Scheme.  My Department is currently working with HSC employers on the introduction of guidance regarding pension flexibilities.I have often said that an increased and sustained investment is necessary to support our ambitions for the health and social care workforce and to deliver the workforce requirements and development recommended by workforce reviews.  My Department currently faces a very challenging financial position with funding pressures in health becoming increasingly significant.  Our financial situation will undoubtedly be constrained whatever the final budget settlement and we will not be able to do everything we want.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of the current challenges facing Health Service committed practitioners; and (ii) what financial support is being made available to address the exceptional nature of soaring expenses impacting on Health Service dentistry.
answer_text: Prior to the arrival of COVID-19, General Dental Services (GDS) in Northern Ireland faced many challenges, however access to treatment was good and the general oral health of the population was improving.  The impact of the Covid-19 pandemic on delivery of dental services was significant, indeed the effects are still being felt, even as restrictions have been lifted. In parallel rising costs are placing an additional strain on practices. In recognition of this the support for General Dental Services (GDS) will remain under review.In April 2022 I introduced the Rebuilding Support Scheme (RSS), which has paid an enhancement on all Items of Service undertaken in the first two quarters of 2022/23, and will continue with a 10% enhancement throughout Q3 and Q4.In addition, I recently announced a 25% increase in the fee paid to dentists for the provision of dentures.  The increase, which is a further recurring investment of £1.2m per annum in GDS, is intended to offset the rise in costs associated with the provision of this vital service.I have also considered and accepted the recommendations made by the Review Body on Doctors' and Dentists' Remuneration (DDRB) in their 50th report that would provide a 4.5% pay uplift to General Dental Practitioners for 2022/23.  However, unlike my colleagues in other parts of the United Kingdom, I am unable to announce the immediate implementation of these pay awards locally, as Northern Ireland still does not have an agreed Executive Budget for 2022/23.However, investment alone will not deliver the improvements we need and that is why the Department’s Dental Contract Reform Group (CRG) was established.  The CRG is looking at how to make improvement in the dental contract in short and longer term to ensure that Health Service dentistry is sustainable and delivers for both patients and the profession.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) what percentage uplift will be applied towards the Expenses component of the Doctors and Dentists Remuneration uplift; (ii) what methodology is being used to calculate the expenses uplift; and (iii) when will the Doctors and Dentists Remuneration uplift be paid to dentists.
answer_text: I have considered and accepted the recommendations made by the Review Body on Doctors' and Dentists' Remuneration (DDRB) in their 50th report that would provide a 4.5% pay uplift to GDPs for 2022/23.  However, unlike my colleagues in other parts of the United Kingdom, I am unable to announce the immediate implementation of these pay awards locally, as Northern Ireland still does not have an agreed Executive Budget for 2022/23.Whilst elements of the pay uplift are outwith the control of the Department of Health, particularly this year, I will do everything I can to implement as soon as possible. A communication to dental practices regarding the expenses element will be made as soon as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he intends to introduce a primary care audiology model on par with existing community eye services and similar to the model committed to by the Scottish Government.
answer_text: Audiology Services in Northern Ireland are currently provided via Secondary Care services, with GPs making the necessary referrals to ENT/Audiology in a Health and Social Care Trust. The Scottish audiology sector is currently undergoing a full independent review. I am advised that the model of care for any new community hearing service in Scotland will be informed by the recommendations from the review, which is scheduled to report in Spring 2023. My Department will note those recommendations in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he has considered commissioning community-based, primary care audiology services, similar to services for eyecare under the Northern Ireland Primary Eyecare Assessment and Referral Scheme.
answer_text: Audiology Services are currently provided via Secondary Care services, with GPs making the necessary referrals to ENT/Audiology in a Health and Social Care Trust.  Any potential revised model for audiology services would need to be considered under the auspices of the Northern Ireland Adult and Paediatric Audiology Quality Standards.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what steps he is taking to reduce waiting times for audiology services.
answer_text: To date, £69m has been allocated in 2022/23 for additional waiting list activity for the period April to December. The funding is being used to help reduce waiting times for assessment and treatment across a range of services, including audiology. The funding is being targeted at those patients with the highest clinical priority and involves creating additional in-house health service capacity over and above normal day to day work, as well as paying independent sector providers to assess and treat patients who are on waiting lists.Bringing our waiting lists to an acceptable level, including looking at innovative ways to manage these across a range of specialties, is a long-term collective effort requiring sustained and substantial investment and additional staffing.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance what plans he has to bring forward a pay award for the Northern Ireland Civil Service.
answer_text: I refer you to the statement that I made on 11 October which sets out the position in relation to Civil Service pay:https://www.financeni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/dfp/Finance%20Minister%20-Written%20Ministerial%20Statement%20-%202022-23%20Budgetary%20Position.pdf

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what support is provided by his Department in the form of bursaries to social work students.
answer_text: My Department provides a Social Work Student Incentive Scheme for all  NI Degree in Social Work students who are domicile and studying full-time in Northern Ireland. Social Work students can access a non-means tested payment of £4,000 for each year of their study.  In addition, they are awarded £500 per year to cover expenses whilst on Practice Learning placements. The Student Incentive Scheme is in addition to any Student Finance support, which can be applied for separately.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has considered reviewing bursary levels for Health and Social Care students in light of the cost of living crisis.
answer_text: On 27 September 2022 I announced changes to the rates of means-tested allowances available to students, depending on their individual circumstances, on pre-registration nursing and midwifery programmes commissioned by my Department, to help to alleviate the impact of the current cost of living pressures. A 15% increase has been applied, effective from 1 September 2022, to the Dependants’ Allowance for spouse or first child; the allowance for subsequent children; and to the Parents’ Learning Allowance.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many unpaid carers have been awarded Self-Directed Support based on their own identified needs (i) in Northern Ireland; and (ii) in each Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: This information was not held centrally and data had to be obtained from the Health and Social Care Trusts.(i) As data is unavailable for some Health and Social Care Trusts a Northern Ireland figure could not be obtained.(ii) Southern and Western Health and Social Care Trust were unable to provide a response to this AQW.  Data for the 3 remaining Trusts below relates to 31st March 2022.Belfast TrustThere were 110 carers receiving Self-Directed Support based on their own identified needs.Northern TrustThere were 842 receiving Self-directed support broken down as; 287 direct payment for carers, 552 Trust arranged services for carers, 3 Managed budget for carers.South Eastern TrustThere were 347 carers in receipt of a service based on their own identified needs.  The Trust is unable to provide a data split for all categories under the Self-Directed support umbrella, but are able to confirm that of the 347 carers in receipt of a service, 144 are receiving a service via Direct Payments.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for a breakdown of the bursary support provided to students by his Department to each faculty of Health and Social Care in universities.
answer_text: The current support provided to students on healthcare programmes commissioned by my Department is as follows.My Department currently pays the tuition fees, and provides a non means-tested bursary of £5, 165 per annum, to nursing and midwifery students on a commissioned pre-registration training place at our local universities. Students may also be eligible for a range of means-tested allowances, depending on their individual circumstances.In relation to medical and dental students, NI-domiciled students undertaking their studies at a UK Medical or Dental School are provided with tuition fee support by my Department for their final year of study. They may also be eligible for grants towards their living and travel costs, from Student Finance NI, on entering the fifth year of a first degree course in medicine. Support may also be available from Student Finance NI to Medical students undertaking a one year, intercalated degree as part of their undergraduate degree course.For Allied Health Profession students, my Department currently pays the tuition fees, and provides a means-tested bursary, to students on a commissioned pre-registration training place at Ulster University. For Social Work students, my Department currently provides a non means-tested Social Work Student Incentive Scheme of £4,000 per year and an annual contribution towards Practice Learning costs of £500.00 for NI domicile students to study the Degree in Social Work on a full time course approved by NISCC.  Students are also entitled to apply for a range of grants and means-tested loans from Student Finance NI.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what guidance is in place regarding the distance that a new domestic dwelling in the countryside can be sited beside an existing high volume poultry business.
answer_text: There is no specific Departmental guidance in relation to new dwellings in the countryside in close proximity to existing high volume poultry businesses. The framework for decision-taking on such proposals, including proposals for new dwellings, includes the Strategic Planning Policy Statement (SPPS) and retained PPS 21: Sustainable Development in the Countryside (where relevant under the transitional arrangements of the SPPS); the local development plan (LDP); and, planning guidance contained within ‘Building on Tradition: A Sustainable Design Guide for the Northern Ireland Countryside’. In practice, planning applications for new dwellings in the countryside, will be determined by local councils on their individual merits taking account of the LDP for the area; regional planning policy and guidance (including the SPPS and retained PPS 21); the expert views of statutory consultees; local circumstances; and, all other material considerations. Where a proposal for a new dwelling in the countryside is in close proximity to an existing poultry house the views of the council’s Environmental Health Department, will be particularly important in respect of the potential impact on residential amenity from noise, odour and air quality.  Ultimately, the relevance and weight to be attached to planning policy and all other material considerations is a matter of planning judgement for the council as decision taker, on a case by case basis.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what plans are in place to utilise existing community infrastructure to support those most at risk in the winter months as a result of rising costs and disruption to services.
answer_text: The cost-of-living crisis is having major impact, particularly on those who are already struggling financially. I am deeply frustrated that the continued absence of a functioning Executive and agreed Executive budget prevents additional measures being introduced.	My Department will do all it can to support those experiencing hardship, and all options are being explored to provide both immediate and medium to longer term interventions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how her departmental officials have engaged with their counterparts in other parts of the United Kingdom, to consider new models of support for families most affected by the cost of living crisis.
answer_text: The cost-of-living crisis is having major impact, particularly on those who are already struggling financially. I am deeply frustrated that the continued absence of a functioning Executive and agreed Executive budget prevents additional measures being introduced.	My Department will do all it can to support those experiencing hardship, and all options are being explored to provide both immediate and medium to longer term interventions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer (i) AQW 357/22-27; (ii) AQW 1344/22-27; (iii) AQW 1615/22-27; (iv) AQW 1620/22-27; and (v) AQW 1621/22-27.
To ask the Minister of Health whether all Health and Social Care facilities across Trusts are operating with the same policy for patient visitations.
answer_text: Visiting arrangements in all Hospital and Hospice settings continue to be governed by the guidance contained in the “Pathway to Enhanced Visiting” document published in May 2021.  Since the start of this summer, our hospitals/hospices have progressed to the final stage of this Pathway, which sets out the minimum expected visiting arrangements, with specific arrangements set out for specific areas of care. Individual wards/units are free to relax arrangements further if this is supported by an appropriate risk assessment.  In certain circumstances, local decisions may have to be made by Hospices or Health & Social Care Trusts (HSCTs) to apply additional restrictions for limited periods to protect patients, families and staff. This could include specific concerns regarding clinically extremely vulnerable patients or local incidents of infection outbreak in a hospice, hospital and/or evidence of rapidly increasing community transmission.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department will take into consideration the latest Census results and allow other faith organisations or denominations to join hospital chaplaincy roles.

To ask the Minister of Health to outline (i) what type of hospital wards are still operating visitation restrictions due to Covid-19; (ii) why they are continuing to operate such restrictions; and (iii) whether this is consistent across all Trusts.
answer_text: My Department’s policy position remains that visiting in all Hospital and Hospice settings should continue to follow the guidance contained in the “Pathway to Enhanced Visiting” document published in May 2021.  Since the start of this summer, our hospitals/hospices have progressed to the final stage of the visiting Pathway, which sets out the minimum expected visiting arrangements, with specific arrangements set out for specific areas of care. All Trusts are expected to apply this updated guidance in a consistent fashion.While individual wards/units are free to relax arrangements further if this is supported by an appropriate risk assessment, local decisions may also have to be made by Hospices or Health & Social Care Trusts (HSCTs) to apply additional restrictions for limited periods to protect patients, families and staff. This could include specific concerns regarding clinically extremely vulnerable patients or local incidents of infection outbreak in a hospice, hospital and/or evidence of rapidly increasing community transmissionWhile most pandemic related restrictions have been lifted across society, the risk of transmission of the virus remains, and those in hospital are especially vulnerable so a degree of risk-assessed visiting management remains appropriate. Since we have moved to the position of considering how society should deal with the challenge of living with Covid, my officials are working with PHA colleagues to develop hospital/hospice visiting guidance which will offer a return to more normalised visiting arrangements in hospitals and hospices here. I anticipate this will be published shortly, and my expectation is that all Trusts will apply this updated guidance in a consistent fashion.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what progress has been made on securing funding for the construction of a new library facility in Enniskillen.
answer_text: The redevelopment of Enniskillen Library remains a priority for Libraries NI and my Department. Options to secure available capital funding are currently being explored.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail when the telephone infrastructure for emergency call centres was last updated.
answer_text: The information requested is not held by my Department.Calls made using 999 or 112 are identified and prioritised by the communications providers and forwarded to, and then answered by, call handling agents in one of six call-handling centres. The call-handling centres are owned by BT Plc, who operate the service on behalf of all the communications providers (mobile, fixed line and broadband).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline the procedures for call handling centres if there is technology or telephone infrastructure issues or failures.
answer_text: The information requested is not held by my Department.Calls made using 999 or 112 are identified and prioritised by the communications providers and forwarded to, and then answered by, call handling agents in one of six call-handling centres. The call-handling centres are owned by BT Plc, who operate the service on behalf of all the communications providers (mobile, fixed line and broadband).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance what discussions he has had with the UK Government in relation to securing funding for the European Social Fund Programme.
answer_text: The European Social Fund (ESF) programme is coming to an end as a direct result of Brexit. It will not continue past the end of March 2023. Its successor is the British Government’s the Shared Prosperity Fund (SPF). I have had extensive engagement with British Government ministers on the SPF, most recently on the 13th of October when I met with the Secretary of State for Levelling Up and other ministers and pressed on them the importance of replacing the ESF in full through the SPF.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 2942/22-27, whether there is an audit planned on the planning decisions made by Mid Ulster District Council.
answer_text: There are currently no plans by my Department to undertake an audit on the planning decisions made by Mid Ulster District Council.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, following reports of an incident involving a number of nursing staff at Knockbracken Mental Health facility in August, what actions have been taken following recommendations to improve staff safety at the facility.
answer_text: A review was undertaken by the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust following the assaults on patients and staff in Shannon Clinic in August 2022.A number of workforce issues were identified by the Trust, and these are now being addressed, including:Reduction in the use of agency staff;Additional training provided for all staff within Shannon Clinic;Assessing the needs of individual admission requests and increasing staffing temporarily if required; andAdditional training for staff in the ward affected by the incident in August, in order to positively deal with disturbed and challenging behaviours.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) whether each Integrated Care Partnership Committee in Northern Ireland has a carer representative in post; and (ii) what steps are being taken to recruit carer representatives to vacancies that may exist.
answer_text: Of the seventeen Integrated Care Partnerships (ICPs) in place across Northern Ireland, there are currently carer members on eleven of them. Work is progressing on the development of an Integrated Care System (ICS) for NI and as part of the managed process to transition to the new model, vacancies arising within ICP Committees are not being filled.The draft ICS framework highlights the importance of working in partnership with service users and carers and there will be opportunities for carers to be represented within the ICS structures once established.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how many people in Fermanagh and South Tyrone are currently at the assessment stage for supported living accommodation.
answer_text: The Housing Executive have advised that supported housing schemes are not let by participating landlords in the same manner as general needs accommodation i.e. on the basis of points awarded under the Housing Selection Scheme. Supported applicants are considered without reference to points and apart from the general needs waiting list. The assessment for supported living is Trust led and where an applicant requires this type of accommodation, the Health Trust or Social Services will make a direct referral to the Housing Support Officer working within the Housing Executive thereby bypassing the assessment stage customary to other general needs applicants. As set out in the response to AQW 3589/22-27, there were 15 such supported applicants registered within the Fermanagh South Tyrone Parliamentary Constituency as of 30th June 2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how many people are currently on a housing waiting list for supported living accommodation in the North Fermanagh region.
answer_text: The Housing Executive have advised that as of the 30th June 2022, there were 15 ‘supported applications’ registered in the Fermanagh South Tyrone Parliamentary Constituency. A ‘supported application’ is made by  applicants for whom only a supported housing option will meet their accommodation needs.The Housing Executive have confirmed that figures are obtained every quarter and September’s figures have not yet been verified.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of people on social housing waiting lists in the North Fermanagh region, broken down by the (i) type of home required; and (ii) number of bedrooms required.
answer_text: The Housing Executive has advised that it is not possible to answer this question in terms of the type of home required. Applicants are assessed in line with the rules of the Housing Selection Scheme in relation to the number of bedrooms required. This means that an applicant may be reasonably offered any type of home with that number of bedrooms (e.g. house/bungalow/flat) depending on their individual circumstances/need. question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of a Certificate of Lawful Use or Development being issued for application LA09/2021/0353/LDP.
answer_text: A Certificate of Lawful Use or Development (CLUD) was issued under Section 170 of the Planning Act (Northern Ireland) 2011 (application ref: LA09/2021/0353/LDP) by Mid Ulster District Council on 23rd March 2021. The Department has no powers to process or assess CLUD applications and responsibility for determining these cases lies with the relevant Council.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether it is normal practice for planners to issue a Certificate for Lawful Use or Development before a development has commenced.
answer_text: Provision is made under section 170 of the Planning Act (NI) 2011 for a person to make an application for a certificate of lawfulness of proposed use or development if they wish to ascertain whether any proposed use of buildings, or other land, or any operations proposed to be carried out in, on, or under land would be lawful.    Under section 170, where the Council is provided with information satisfying it that a use or operation would be lawful if instituted or begun at the time of the application, it must issue a certificate to that effect.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure under what circumstances can a Certificate of Lawful Use or Development be granted by a Planning Authority.
answer_text: Provision is made in the Planning Act (NI) 2011 for a person to make an application for and a council to issue; under section 169 a certificate of lawfulness of existing use or development; and under section 170 a certificate of lawfulness of proposed use or development. A certificate under section 169 can be issued where the council is provided with information satisfying it of the lawfulness at the time of the application of the use, operations or other matter described in the application, and under section 170 where the council is provided with information satisfying it that the use or operations would be lawful if instituted or begun at the time of the application.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how many times, since 2016, has his Department carried out an audit on planning decisions in Mid Ulster District Council.
answer_text: My Department has not undertaken an audit on any planning decisions in Mid Ulster District Council since 2016.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what measures her Department will consider to increase the level of provision of special needs nursery places in the Dungannon and wider South Tyrone area.
answer_text: The Department aims to provide a funded pre-school place for every child in their immediate pre-school year whose parents want it. The Education Authority's (EA) Pre-School Education Group (PEG) is responsible for securing sufficient provision in local areas to achieve this aim.   Where a child has a statement of special educational needs (SEN) their admission to an education setting is dealt with via a separate process. The placement of children for whom the EA maintains a statement of (SEN) is arranged through the Statutory Assessment and Review Service (SARS). The SEN placements process is managed to ensure each child receives a suitable placement that will appropriately support and cater to their individual needs. This is managed on a case by case basis. Securing appropriate provision for children with statements of SEN remains a key priority for me and is being taken forward by the EA supported by the Department and key partners, to ensure children are placed appropriately and in line with their needs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what current provisions are in place in the Dungannon and wider South Tyrone area for young children with special needs who require a nursery place.
answer_text: Sperrinview Special School currently serves Dungannon and South Tyrone catering for children with severe learning difficulties from the age of 3 to 19.  Due to a continuous increase in demand the school is operating at capacity and experiencing accommodation pressures. In order to meet these pressures a satellite site for Sperrinview Special School was established in Cookstown in September 2021 to address the demand for Early Years provision in the Dungannon and Cookstown areas. Further new provision has been established at St Patrick's PS, Annaghmore for September 2022, in close partnership with Sperrinview Special School, to facilitate the placement of up to 16 nursery aged children.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) his Department’s resurfacing programme for Fermanagh and South Tyrone, for the remainder of this financial year; and (ii) a commencement date for each scheme.
answer_text: My Department’s response to your question AQW 29239/17-22 in February provided details of the schemes that were being considered for inclusion in the resurfacing work programmes. Details of the finalised resurfacing programme for Fermanagh and South Tyrone, for the 2022/23 financial year will be included in the Department’s Council Reports to Fermanagh & Omagh, Mid Ulster and Derry City & Strabane Councils which are now being finalised, following recent confirmation of the Division’s 2022/23 contingency funding allocation. The Report will in due course be presented to each respective Council, published on the Department’s website and placed in the Assembly library.Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact DfI Roads Western Division at: dfiroads.western@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what consideration he has given to the further expansion of the 20mph speed limit programme.
answer_text: My Department has invested £2million over the past two financial years which will see the introduction of part-time 20mph speed limits at 215 schools.  The second tranche of part-time 20mph speed limits at schools is being delivered this year. When all of the tranche 2 sites are fully operational and have had sufficient time to bed in, my Department intends to carry out a review of this work to determine if a further tranche is justified. Until this review is carried out I am not in a position to commit to install part-time 20mph speed limits at any more schools.Should you have any further questions on this issue, please contact DfI Roads Western Division at dfiroads.western@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk .

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether Lisbellaw Primary School, Co. Fermanagh, will be included in 20mph speed limit programme.
answer_text: The second tranche of part-time 20mph speed limits at schools is being currently being delivered and it is hoped the measures will be operational for the start of the new school year. Upon completion, I am pleased to be able to confirm that 215 schools across the North will be benefitting from this initiative, however as you are aware Lisbellaw Primary School was not included in either the first or second tranche of schools on the basis of its ranking within the overall assessment process.When all of the tranche 2 sites are fully operational and have had sufficient time to bed in, my Department intends to carry out a review of this work to determine if a further tranche is justified. Until this review is completed I am not in a position to commit to the installation of part-time 20mph speed limits at any additional schools.Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact DfI Roads Western Division at dfiroads.western@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for an update regarding the delays to resurfacing works following legal challenges to the 2021 procurement competition; and (ii) when his Department plans to have the new resurfacing contract for Fermanagh in place.
answer_text: My officials advise that no schemes in the Fermanagh section of the Fermanagh & South Tyrone constituency were impacted by the delays arising from legal challenges to the procurement process in the 2021/22 financial year.Fermanagh is in Phase 3 of the new term contract strategy for asphalt resurfacing, which comprises four phases with 6 new term contracts in each phase. It is anticipated that Phase 3 will be awarded in November 2022.Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact DfI Roads Western Division at dfiroads.western@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 345/22-27,  (i) whether the A4 Enniskillen Southern Bypass is a priority project for his Department; and (ii) whether the necessary funding for the project has been ring-fenced by his Department.
answer_text: I recognise that the Enniskillen Bypass scheme is of significant importance to the town of Enniskillen and the surrounding area.The decision to proceed and statutory orders for the scheme were made in 2021. Site clearance and fencing works commenced in January 2022, and are now substantially complete. NIE diversionary works are also scheduled to commence during the summer months.While I remain committed to such schemes, the refusal of the DUP to form an Executive means a three year budget cannot be agreed which makes it extremely difficult for my Department to plan future investment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether the recruitment process to the Northern Ireland Fire and Rescue Service has been paused; and to outline the rationale for this position.
answer_text: The recruitment process has not been paused.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what measures his Department is taking to improve diabetes care, following the disruption to services caused by the COVID pandemic.

To ask the Minister for Communities what consideration her Department and the Northern Ireland Housing Executive have given to the potential to reopen fireplaces within Housing Executive properties, due to the rising costs associated with home-heating fuels.
answer_text: The Housing Executive has advised that there are health and safety concerns with tenants reopening previously closed fires. The Housing Executive maintains its heating systems through an annual safety check of the appliance and flue, with repairs carried out where defects are found. The Housing Executive will not have completed an annual safety check of any flue which has been blocked up, and is therefore redundant.There is also a risk that any structural changes, resulting from the attempted reopening of a closed fireplace could lead to a fire risk or result in a build-up of poisonous gases. In order for any modifications to be undertaken, building control approval must be obtained and a proper structural investigation of the existing chimney structure and the integrity of the existing flue must be undertaken to confirm that the fire and flue can be used safely.The Housing Executive has been installing more energy efficient heating systems and removing solid fuel fires in an effort to combat fuel poverty, to reduce carbon emissions and particulate and smoke pollutants.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice what is the average waiting time for an application for a firearms certificate to be processed.
answer_text: My Department does not process applications for a firearms certificate and does not hold this information.Applications for the grant of a firearm certificate, under Article 4 of the Firearms (Northern Ireland) Order 2004, are made to the Chief Constable.  This is an operational policing matter and as such, I suggest you address this question to the Chief Constable of the PSNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice what actions her Department is taking to reduce the processing time of an application for a firearms certificate.
answer_text: My Department does not process applications for a firearms certificate. Applications for the grant of a firearm certificate, under Article 4 of the Firearms (Northern Ireland) Order 2004, are made to the Chief Constable.  This is an operational policing matter and as such, questions about the system and timescales for processing applications for a firearms certificate should be addressed to the Chief Constable of the PSNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will commit funding to provide schemes, such as the Golden Hello and New to Practice Schemes in GB, to help recruit GPs in areas where there are shortages.
answer_text: I am aware of the ongoing pressures facing General Practice across Northern Ireland and the particular pressures facing practices in rural areas.  My Department continues to work closely with GP representatives to consider how best to respond to these challenges. In doing so, my Department will consider possible options to support the recruitment and retention of GPs, especially in areas where current available numbers place continuing service delivery under pressure.	I am very conscious of pressing demands for extra funding across many areas of health and social care.  As Minister, I will continue to do the best I can to deliver for the public with the resources I have available. Amid ongoing budget uncertainty, I am on record as warning that funding pressures in health may be significant by the second half of 2022/23. The financial situation will undoubtedly be constrained whatever the final budget settlement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department is currently investigating any bursary schemes for GPs, such as the Golden Hello and the New to Partnership Schemes, particularly in rural areas.
answer_text: I am aware of the ongoing pressures facing General Practice across Northern Ireland and the particular pressures facing practices in rural areas.  My Department continues to work closely with GP representatives to consider how best to respond to these challenges. In doing so, my Department will consider possible options to support the recruitment and retention of GPs, especially in areas where current available numbers place continuing service delivery under pressure.	I am very conscious of pressing demands for extra funding across many areas of health and social care.  As Minister, I will continue to do the best I can to deliver for the public with the resources I have available. Amid ongoing budget uncertainty, I am on record as warning that funding pressures in health may be significant by the second half of 2022/23. The financial situation will undoubtedly be constrained whatever the final budget settlement.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline what resurfacing plans his Department has in Killyman, Co Tyrone.
answer_text: My Department currently has no plans to resurface roads in Killyman; however, inspections and repairs will continue to be carried out on roads in Killyman in accordance with our current maintenance policies.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline what resurfacing plans his Department has in Moygashel, Co Tyrone.
answer_text: My Department currently has no plans to carry out resurfacing of roads in Moygashel during this financial year. However, my officials will continue to inspect and carry out repairs to defects identified on roads in the Moygashel area in accordance with our current maintenance policies.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline what resurfacing plans his Department has in Lisbellaw, Co. Fermanagh.
answer_text: Due to the position with the resurfacing contracts in the Fermanagh area (as outlined in my response to AQW 880/22-27) my Department currently has no plans to carry out any resurfacing works in the Lisbellaw area during this financial year. However, my officials are proposing to carry out some small scale remedial patching works on minor roads in the Lisbellaw area, which have been identified and prioritised for repair as part of our routine maintenance inspection regime.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline what resurfacing plans his Department has in Kesh, Co. Fermanagh.
answer_text: Legal challenges to a 2021 procurement competition prevented the award of a number of road resurfacing contracts, including the one covering the Kesh area.  Regrettably my Department’s plans to resurface the C409 Crevenish Road and A47 Boa Island Road will be delayed until new contracts are in place. It is hoped that the new resurfacing term contract for Fermanagh will be in place in late 2022; in the meantime roads in the Kesh area will be inspected and maintained in accordance with our current road maintenance policy.However, in the intervening period my officials are proposing to carry out some small scale remedial patching works on some minor roads in the Kesh area which have been identified and prioritised for repair as part of our routine maintenance inspection regime.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the current waiting time for a first appointment with a long COVID clinic in each Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Information on waiting times for an appointment at a long COVID clinic is not available centrally in the Department. The following information has been provided by the Belfast, Northern, South Eastern and Southern Health and Social Care Trusts.In the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust, the current waiting time is 17 weeks.In the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust the current waiting time is 16 weeks.In the Southern Health and Social Care Trust the current waiting time is 36 weeks. question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when the new build for Fivemiletown Library is due to commence; and when this work is expected to be completed.
answer_text: Work to construct a new public library facility in Fivemiletown commenced on the existing library site on the 10 January 2022 and the project is expected to complete by early autumn 2023.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to list the GP practices set to benefit from the multi-disciplinary teams rollout, that was announced in March 2022.
answer_text: The continuing rollout of the MDT Programme is a key priority for my Department and remains key to stabilising and strengthening vital Primary Care services in the community.  Details of the proposed running order, for further rollout, are as follows:Proposed orderFederation AreaTrust Area1North BelfastBHSCT2South WestWHSCT3East AntrimNHSCTMy ability to plan strategically, including on the further rollout of the MDT Programme, is being significantly impaired by the ongoing budgetary uncertainty.  However, GP Federations and HSC Trusts can now start to work in earnest with the MDT Programme team on plans for rollout in their areas.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the A4 Enniskillen Southern Bypass.
answer_text: I recognise that the Enniskillen Bypass scheme is of significant importance to the town of Enniskillen and the surrounding area and that its delivery will help tackle regional imbalance, enhance the economy, connect communities and improve road safety.The decision to proceed and statutory orders for the scheme were made in 2021. Site clearance and fencing works commenced in January 2022, and are now substantially complete. NIE diversionary works are also scheduled to commence during the summer months.Timescales for progressing the scheme to procurement and construction will be dependent on budgets made available to my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when work to repair Kesh footbridge will begin and be completed by.
answer_text: I am fully aware of the vital importance of this private footbridge to the residents of the village of Kesh and visitors to the area.My officials are continuing to work closely with their counterparts from Fermanagh & Omagh District Council to deliver the necessary repair works to the river wall and footbridge foundation. I understand the Council has recently secured £200k through the Covid Recovery Small Settlements Regeneration Programme for this project. Subject to the completion of work on the detailed design and the agreement of all stakeholders, the Department hopes to be in a position to commence works this summer with an anticipated construction period of 12-15 weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance what work his Department is undertaking to ensure rural communities have access to 24 hour ATMs, in light of the recent announcements of bank closures.
answer_text: While the regulation of Financial Services is a reserved matter for the British Government and its regulatory authorities, I have met with the local banks a number of times to directly raise my concerns about the impact of branch closures locally.Most recently, I chaired a roundtable meeting in February this year on the future of banking in the North with representatives from the banking industry, trade unions, business bodies, and consumer and community organisations. I sent a report of that discussion, including the issues raised around access to cash in rural communities, to the Chief Secretary to the Treasury for a response given his overall responsibilities in this area. I have not yet received a reply. In terms of my responsibilities as Finance Minister, I have also extended the rate relief scheme for rural ATMs for another year, which will provide an exemption from business rates to eligible ATMs in designated rural areas until March 2023. The aim of this is to help rural communities by encouraging banking companies to keep ATMs in these areas by reducing their overheads.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for a breakdown of the funding that has been allocated to the Western Health and Social Care Trust for the rollout of multi-disciplinary teams.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail what work he and his Department have done to prevent The Firs residential care facility from closing.
answer_text: The Department has closely monitored the planned sale of The Firs residential care facility and has engaged with the Southern Health and Social Care Trust to ensure that residents could be accommodated should the home close. The Department is guided by the outcome of the Trust’s options appraisal and value for money considerations. The SHSCT worked with the provider to identify potential buyers. However, it is with regret that this care facility will close as a suitable buyer has not been identified. The Trust has engaged with the residents and families involved to identify alternative accommodation arrangements and has started to transition residents to new placements in the surrounding area.  It is hoped that this process will be expedited as quickly as possible to ensure the minimum distress to the residents and families involved.
